text:
DOCK LEDGER

Six bells, and the hereditary Bearer processes down the quay holding the ceremonial clipboard at the regulation angle of respect. The Harbor Efficiency Trust has reclassified all loading before breakfast as enthusiasm hours, which are like wages except the money part is replaced by dignity. The pay-ledger circle keeps a rival count in chalk on the belly of crane four, and the tally widow of pier two wants your name on it.

choices.text: Chalk your hours under crane four

success.text: The widow lends you her chalk like it is a small sword. Your hours join two hundred others on the crane's underside, an accounting the Trust cannot audit because officially the underside of a crane does not exist. The Bearer walks past, sees nothing, and notes on the ceremonial clipboard that morale is up, which for once is true.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_assembly The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: Halfway through your total a rope parts and a crate of salt fish makes its argument against your shoulder. The circle chalks you in anyway, with an asterisk meaning hurt on the job, which the Trust classifies as enthusiasm with complications. The clipboard records the incident as zero incidents.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_assembly The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: File a wage query on official Trust paper

outcomes.text: You request the form for querying unpaid hours and the clerk goes pale, because someone finally asked for it and it exists. The Trust must now answer within thirty days or the query becomes a finding, a rule they wrote for a world where nobody could read. The Bearer initials your copy with genuine warmth, then checks whether he was allowed to, and the widow chalks a small star beside your name.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_assembly The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: Your query is accepted, stamped, and reclassified as feedback. Feedback goes in the enthusiasm file, which is a barrel. The Bearer holds the ceremonial clipboard between you and the barrel the whole time, out of what he later calls respect.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_assembly The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Sell the Trust a list of who chalks

outcomes.text: A Trust steward pays eight for the chalk names and a bonus of six because your handwriting is legible, a rarity on this dock. Overnight the names are gently rained off the crane by a man with a sponge and a title. The circle re-chalks by Thursday, angrier and higher up the crane, and nobody suspects you, which somehow does not feel like an outcome.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_assembly The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

Rain on the net loft roof, and forty dockers argue about arithmetic in a room that smells of tar and old herring. The circle wants one honest ledger of every unpaid loading hour, itemized, presentable, impossible to sneer at. The Bearer has come in what he calls a neutral capacity, holding the ceremonial clipboard, which rattles faintly when he laughs, as if something loose lives inside it.

choices.text: Draft the ledger in the Trust's own format

outcomes.text: You copy the Trust's margins, its seals, its habit of numbering paragraphs that contain nothing. The finished ledger looks so official that two clerks file it out of reflex before anyone asks them to. The Bearer peers at it and says quietly that it is beautiful work, then coughs and adds, administratively speaking.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

The Bearer peers at it and says quietly that it is beautiful work, then coughs and adds, administratively speaking.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: Your officialdom is too convincing: a Trust inspector mistakes the ledger for a genuine internal document and reports the Trust to itself. The resulting inquiry is into the wrong thing, conducted by the wrong people, and somehow billed to the fish shed. The ceremonial clipboard is brought in to restore dignity and rattles the entire session.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Charge a copying fee for every ledger page

outcomes.text: You set up at the door with paper, ink, and a tariff. Dockers pay because the ledger matters and you hold the only pen that works, eight in coin by supper and six more for rush copies. You have invented a toll on other people's honesty, which the Trust would admire if they knew, and two gangs grumble about it loudly enough to count as weather.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Take every docker's count down by hand

success.text: Hour by hour you take their numbers: the night gangs, the widow's double tides, the boy who oils the cranes for the privilege of being near cranes. The total comes out heavier than anyone joked it would, heavy enough to need two men to carry conversationally. Even the Bearer leans in to look, then remembers his neutrality and studies the ceiling, which he later audits.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: You transcribe until your hand cramps into a claw shaped exactly like holding a pen, an injury the Trust manual files under clerical enthusiasm. The count gets finished, but the roof leak takes the last page and you spend an hour restoring the widow's Tuesdays from memory. She confirms every number without looking, which is the most frightening thing on this dock.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_retaliation The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

Monday the Trust answers the chalk with ceremony: every gang must sign the enthusiasm roster before touching a rope, witnessed by the Bearer, whose clipboard has been elevated to an Instrument of Record. The Bearer performs the elevation with a small speech nobody asked for, and one person applauds, and it is him. Under the new rule, unsigned hours do not exist, and neither, administratively, do you.

choices.text: Ask the Bearer why his clipboard rattles

success.text: You catch him at dusk oiling the hinge and ask, gently, why an Instrument of Record rattles. He goes very still, then admits it has rattled since his aunt's day, and that Bearers are forbidden to open it, dignity being load-bearing. He pays you four to keep the hinge oiled and says nothing else, but he says it like a man who has waited thirty years to be asked. The circle notes you drinking with management and chalks a small question mark by your name.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: You work a thumb under the clip while the Bearer is mid-speech and something folded slides free: Trust scrip, old issue, still spendable. You pocket eight in dead men's wages before the clip snaps back with a crack that everyone hears and nobody can place. The Bearer checks the harbor, the sky, and his shoes; the circle checks you, and the question mark by your name gets chalked deeper.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Refuse the roster until the whole gang refuses

outcomes.text: The morning gang stands with ropes coiled and hands ostentatiously idle, an entire dock volunteering nothing at maximum volume. By noon three ships are queued and the Trust discovers that enthusiasm, unlike wages, cannot be invoiced in advance. The Bearer marks the roster blank with trembling ceremony, and the blank page becomes the most read document in the harbor.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: The gang holds until a steward starts naming names off the roster, and then it is shoving, and then a boathook is involved in a supervisory capacity. You take a knock keeping the widow's count sheets out of the scrum. The Trust logs the morning as spontaneous enthusiasm, and the clipboard comes away with a hairline crack the Bearer covers with his thumb for a week.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Demand the founding wage charter be read aloud

success.text: You stand on a bollard and demand the founding wage charter, which the new rule claims as its grandfather. A clerk is sent, a chest is opened, and the charter says loading is paid work from first rope to last, in letters large enough to survive lawyers. The Trust adjourns to consult the charter's spirit, having lost on its body.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: The Trust produces a scholar who explains the charter predates the invention of hours and therefore covers none of them. It is nonsense, but it is nonsense with a wax seal, which on this dock outranks arithmetic. The roster stays, a crack opens along the clipboard's spine by a knuckle's length, and the Bearer begins carrying it like an egg.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_coalition The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

Word arrives by fast gull: the Trust is sending Auditor Fenwick, author of the enthusiasm program, to certify the harbor efficient in person. The circle decides the honest ledger must be finished before he lands, which means rag paper, ink, and witnesses, none of which are free. The crack down the ceremonial clipboard has stopped being deniable, and the Bearer has taken to holding it against his chest, less like an Instrument and more like a patient.

choices.text: Pay into the kitty for ledger paper

outcomes.text: You drop three coins in the herring tin, which the widow shakes once, her version of a receipt. By evening the kitty has bought good rag paper and the services of a scribe who owes the ferrymen a favor from a night nobody details. The ledger starts to look less like a grudge and more like a document, which is the most dangerous thing a grudge can become.
effects: schedule 5 r18_dock_ledger_crisis The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: You offer your back instead of coin and volunteer to haul the donated paper, and the paper wins: two reams, one gangplank, low tide. You land on the shingle holding every sheet dry above your head, which the net menders applaud and your ribs do not. The widow enters you in the ledger under materials, damaged, one.
effects: schedule 5 r18_dock_ledger_crisis The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Invite the Bearer to witness the honest ledger

outcomes.text: You point out that a ledger of this gravity surely requires witnessing by an Instrument of Record, and there is exactly one on this dock. The Bearer arrives in his good coat, clips the first page in above the crack, and signs with the full hereditary flourish, four generations of loops. The Trust cannot dismiss a document dignified by its own ceremony, which is the sort of trap dignity was always going to walk into.
effects: schedule 5 r18_dock_ledger_crisis The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: The Bearer agrees, glowing, and a steward catches him mid-flourish and reports the Instrument as compromised. The Trust suspends the clipboard pending a review of its loyalty, and the Bearer goes home carrying it wrapped in oilcloth like a relative. Sympathy for him runs so high that even your critics concede the plan half worked, at his expense.
effects: schedule 5 r18_dock_ledger_crisis The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Sell Fenwick's arrival date to the night boats

outcomes.text: The night boats pay eight for the date and six more for the hour, since an audited harbor is a harbor with witnesses. On the day itself the contraband simply takes a holiday, and Fenwick will find the docks miraculously lawful, which he will credit to his program. Everyone gets what they wanted, which is how you know something has gone wrong somewhere.
effects: schedule 5 r18_dock_ledger_crisis The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

Auditor Fenwick lands at noon with a folding table, a travel inkwell, and the smile of a man who invented a number and has come to visit it. The audit will certify that the harbor loads itself for free, enthusiasm being counted as weather. For the signing ceremony the Trust requisitions the ceremonial clipboard, crack and all, because dignity photographs well, and the Bearer hands it over the way men hand over hostages.

choices.text: Enter the honest ledger into the audit record

success.text: You cite the audit's own charter: all documents pertaining to labor costs shall be received. Fenwick's clerk receives the honest ledger because the alternative is admitting the audit does not receive documents, which is the one heresy. It enters the record as Appendix A, and Fenwick's certificate now legally contains the thing it was built to unsee.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_compromise The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: Fenwick examines the ledger and rules it inadmissible on grounds of legibility, the harbor's hours being too clearly written to be authentic accounting. The refusal is stamped in triplicate against the ceremonial clipboard, which creaks under each blow like a ship's timber. You are thanked for your participation, a phrase the Trust keeps in a drawer for exactly this.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_compromise The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Bill Fenwick for orientation services, itemized

outcomes.text: You present Fenwick an invoice covering harbor knowledge, tide interpretation, and smell tolerance, itemized. He pays eight on the spot and six on receipt of a second page, because a person billing him is a person who believes in billing, and belief is what he audits. You are now a line item in the certificate against your neighbors, which pays better than being an appendix.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_compromise The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Pack the quay with every counted hour's owner

outcomes.text: By the time Fenwick uncaps his inkwell the quay holds every person whose hours were rounded to zero, standing in a silence with excellent posture. Nobody threatens anything; they simply watch him count, the way gulls watch a man eat chips. Fenwick's pen slows, then stops, and he asks his clerk in a low voice how many witnesses an efficiency can support.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_compromise The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: The crowd forms, then the stewards form a crowd about the crowd, and the two crowds briefly disagree about geometry. You catch an elbow ushering the crane boy out of the crush. Fenwick signs nothing, reschedules everything, and notes the harbor as overenthusiastic, the first honest figure in the file.
effects: schedule 2 r18_dock_ledger_compromise The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

The Trust proposes peace: half your loading hours acknowledged, payable in gratitude credits redeemable for Trust-brand boots, sized by lottery. The circle is offered a signing ceremony, a small band, and a promise that the word unpaid will be retired in favor of pre-appreciated. The Bearer is to certify it all on the cracked clipboard, and he keeps practicing his signature in the air like a man rehearsing a surrender.

choices.text: Hoist the signing table sixty feet up

success.text: At dawn the public cranes, worked by persons unknown wearing the crane boy's exact grin, lift the signing table, the band's chairs, and the commemorative ribbon sixty feet above the water. The Trust cannot hold a dignified ceremony underneath its own furniture, and everyone present develops a neck ache from admiring the problem. The circle disavows the maneuver in a statement drafted through visible laughter.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: The load swings wide, the stewards arrive with pikes and a fresh banner reading COMPLIANCE IS COMFORT, and the morning goes to bruises. You take three good knocks shielding the winch handle from confiscation. The Trust announces the docks will now be patrolled for everyone's convenience, a word that has never once meant convenience.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Vote the boots down by chalk, one stroke each

outcomes.text: The circle votes under crane four, where counting was invented, one stroke of chalk apiece. The tally runs nine to one against boots, the one being a man who genuinely needs boots, so the kitty buys him a pair on the spot and he changes his stroke barefoot no longer. The refusal goes to the Trust in beautiful copperplate, with appendices.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: The vote holds firm but the meeting runs long, and you sleep three hours before a double tide. A hatch coaming finds your shin in the dark, an injury you file, from habit now, under complications of enthusiasm. The refusal stands; your leg registers a minority opinion.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Request the credits' exchange rate in writing

outcomes.text: You ask, formally, for the exchange rate between one gratitude credit and one act of gratitude. The Trust's clerks work through the night and publish a table proving a docker must be thanked for forty years to afford the left boot. The table is copied, chalked, and set to a tune; the compromise dies of arithmetic, the one disease the Trust cannot reclassify.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: The Trust replies that gratitude floats against the guilder, and hires a crier to explain floating to the dock at volume. It sounds almost reasonable shouted, which is the entire technology of criers. Three gangs waver toward the boots, and the widow spends her evening un-explaining, chalk in one hand, someone's shoulder in the other.
effects: schedule 3 r18_dock_ledger_reversal The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

Certification day, and Fenwick has a ribbon, a lectern, and a speech about a harbor that runs itself on spirit alone. Tradition requires the Bearer to strike the ceremonial clipboard three times for silence, a duty four generations old. On the second strike the crack gives way and the clipboard splits along its spine in front of the assembled harbor. Out of the hollow slides a hundred years of paper: original wage tables, pay slips stamped destroy, every true count that every Bearer was ordered to burn and instead filed inside the dignity itself.

choices.text: Read the oldest pay slip aloud to everyone

success.text: You lift the oldest slip, brittle as a moth, and read out a name, a cargo, an hour, a wage, paid. Then the widow reads one, then the crane boy, then a steward who forgets which side he is on, and the harbor spends the afternoon reading itself its own money. Fenwick stands at the lectern the entire time, holding the ribbon, waiting for a ceremony that has left without him.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_legacy The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

fail.text: Stewards dive for the papers and you dive better, coming up with an armful of the eighteen forties and somebody's boot print on your ear. What you save gets read from a crane platform until dark. The Trust confiscates the rest and learns, too late, that seizing evidence in front of four hundred witnesses is just publishing with extra steps.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_legacy The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Append the spill to Fenwick's own audit

outcomes.text: You inform Fenwick that documents surfacing during an audit are, by his charter, part of that audit. His clerk, a decent woman at the end of a long month, agrees before he can inhale. The certificate now proves the hours were always paid work, in the Trust's own hand, and Fenwick's program is eaten at one sitting by its bibliography.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_legacy The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

outcomes.text: Fenwick declares the papers ceremonial in nature and therefore exempt from meaning, a ruling he delivers while standing in a drift of them. It buys the Trust a week of pretend. But clerks have already copied pages out of pure archival instinct, and pretend does not survive copying.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_legacy The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

choices.text: Palm a fistful of antique scrip

outcomes.text: In the scramble your hand finds the antique scrip layer, still honored at face value by any bank too proud to admit it cannot read. Eight in old money folds into your boot, and six more into your hat, while everyone else is having a moment. History gets made an arm's length away; you get liquidity, which history has never once paid out.
effects: schedule 4 r18_dock_ledger_legacy The clipboard rattles when someone says your name

text:
DOCK LEDGER

A month on, the pay office keeps the split clipboard by the door, its two halves rehinged as the covers of the open ledger, which anyone may read and everyone does. Loading pays from first rope to last, posted daily, and the phrase enthusiasm hours survives only as dock slang for a nap. The Bearer holds a new hereditary title, Keeper of the Open Ledger, and Fenwick, granted flexibility by the Trust, has been seen at the hiring line learning what an hour weighs.

choices.text: Draft the open ledger's rules in plain words

outcomes.text: You write the rules in nine sentences a tired person can read by lamplight: hours are hours, work is paid, the book stays open, disputes get tea. Lawyers circle the document for weeks and find no crevice to den in. The Keeper reads it aloud each morning at the strike of six, with the full flourish, and for the first time in four generations the flourish is load-bearing.

You write the rules in nine sentences a tired person can read by lamplight: hours are hours, work is paid, the book stays open, disputes get tea. Lawyers circle the document for weeks and find no crevice to den in. The Keeper reads it aloud each morning at the strike of six, with the full flourish, and for the first time in four generations the flourish is load-bearing.

Your plain rules scandalize the clerks, who cannot invoice a sentence they understand, and a rival draft appears with sixty clauses and a glossary. For a while the harbor has two rulebooks, and pressure gathers like weather. The Keeper settles it by clipping your version over theirs, hereditary authority's last official act, and the glossary retires to the barrel.

choices.text: Sell guided tours of the famous clipboard

outcomes.text: Pilgrims of middle management arrive from three ports to view the clipboard that swallowed an audit, and you meet them at the gate with a tariff. Eight for the tour, six for touching the hinge, which you declared a sacrament this morning. The Keeper disapproves on the record, and the noon tour is taken off the books, which is to say it costs more.

choices.text: Take the empty chair on the pay-ledger circle

success.text: The widow nominates you, the crane boy seconds by grinning, and the chalk vote goes your way under crane four. Your first duty is the dawn count, hours in one column, people in the other, nothing pre-appreciated. On slow mornings the Keeper stands beside you and holds the ledger open against the wind, which is, he notes, what an Instrument was always for.

fail.text: You win the seat and spend your first watch hauling a stranger's snagged line off the crane track, because the chair apparently comes with the whole harbor attached. You pull a muscle you will be hearing from all winter. Still, the dawn count goes up in your handwriting, and a docker you have never met reads it, nods, and pays for your breakfast without a word.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Tuesday, six in the evening, and the herald from Baron Vacant Holdings stands on the doorstep with a brass eviction trumpet and a scroll of notice. By statute the notice must be sounded indoors, in the hearing of the tenants, which is awkward, because the trumpet will not make a sound anywhere else and the lobby is mid rehearsal for the annual talent show. The widow on floor two, keeper of the master keys, looks him up and down and writes him into the program as act fourteen. He asks when act fourteen goes on. She says that depends entirely on act thirteen.

choices.text: Book the herald's notice as a two minute stage act

outcomes.text: You file his eviction as a performance request, form and fee, one copy to the widow and one to the archive. The herald signs where indicated, relieved to be processed by somebody, anybody. His notice is now item fourteen on a bill of forty, and the bill is legally binding. The trumpet waits in the cloakroom with a ticket on its handle.
effects: schedule 3 r18_tenant_tower_assembly Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: You file his eviction as a performance request, form and fee, one copy to the widow and one to the archive. The herald signs where indicated, relieved to be processed by somebody, anybody. His notice is now item fourteen on a bill of forty, and the bill is legally binding. The trumpet waits in the cloakroom with a ticket on its handle.
effects: schedule 3 r18_tenant_tower_assembly Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: You file the request upside down, which in this jurisdiction means an appeal. The Baron's clerks pounce, cite you for frivolous scheduling, and post the citation in the lobby where the whole tower reads it during the interval. The herald apologizes to you personally, which is somehow worse. The trumpet stays on the doorstep, silent, as designed.

choices.text: Sell the herald a map of the tower's quiet hours

outcomes.text: He pays in fresh Baron coin for a schedule of the hours when nobody in Lantern Tower is singing, rehearsing, or celebrating anything. You invent the schedule on the spot, since no such hours exist. He pays a second time for the annotated version. The widow watches you pocket it and moves your name to the bottom of the cake rota without comment.

outcomes.text: You volunteer for the door and take a folding chair across the shin before anyone mentions that the chairs are act nine and they travel. The audience assumes the limp is choreography and applauds warmly. The widow signs you up anyway, on the grounds that you stayed down politely. In Lantern Tower that counts as an audition.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Monday's program meeting convenes in the laundry room, chaired by the widow, minutes kept by whoever loses the coin toss. The herald has formally requested two minutes for a legal announcement with musical accompaniment, and the caretaker, who conducts the stairwell choir, insists that all announcements be in key. The trumpet sits on the folding table between the raffle tin and the agenda, gleaming like it knows something.

choices.text: Auction the closing slot to the Baron's office

outcomes.text: The Baron's office pays handsomely for the final slot on the bill, reasoning that the last word is legally the loudest. You collect a deposit, then a rush fee. What you do not mention is that by tower tradition the show has never once reached its final slot, and the tradition is in excellent health. The trumpet is now booked to play at an hour that has not occurred since the founding.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_retaliation Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Run the stairwell choir straight through his two minutes

outcomes.text: The caretaker lifts both hands and the choir opens every door on every landing, which is what the stairwell is for. The herald's two minutes arrive, occur, and end inside a wall of harmony four floors deep. He does not blow the trumpet, partly because notice requires audibility, mostly because he is holding the low part of the descant and does not want to lose his place.

outcomes.text: You take the alto stairs two at a time with the hymn sheets and meet the tenor stairs coming down. Four steps, one railing, and the sheets go everywhere like startled doves. The choir sings on around your body, professionally. The caretaker marks you present, which in his ledger is the highest available praise.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_retaliation Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Move the legal announcement to item forty one, after the raffle

success.text: Seconded, minuted, adopted. Item forty one sits behind the raffle, the encore, and the formal thanking of the kettle, and the meeting agrees the law deserves nothing less than a full house. The herald checks the program twice and finds it flawless, which is the trouble. Even the Baron's clerks concede the running order is procedurally gorgeous.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_retaliation Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

fail.text: The clerks read your running order and file it as evidence of obstruction by agenda, a crime you were not aware had been invented yet. Pressure arrives in envelope form. The herald is reassigned to stand nearer the trumpet, in case of sudden legality. The raffle, at least, remains beyond reproach.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_retaliation Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Dawn on Thursday brings six men in Vacant Holdings livery, a door ram, and a work order for vacancy improvements, which is the Baron's phrase for removing the part of a building that objects. The stairwell choir is mid warmup, and the acoustics at six in the morning are the best in the district. The herald stands behind the movers holding the trumpet like a man asked to bring a knife to a christening.

choices.text: Hand the movers hymn sheets and space them by voice

outcomes.text: The choir absorbs the movers one by one, which is how the choir absorbs everything. The largest of them turns out to be a baritone of real feeling, and the door ram is set down so he can hold his note. By the second verse two of them are crying, quietly and in key. The work order stays in someone's pocket, going soft at the corners.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: You wedge yourself between the ram and the doorframe just as the choir hits the loud part and everyone surges on the crescendo. An elbow finds your eye with real conviction. The movers retreat anyway, unsettled by a building that harmonizes while it bleeds. The caretaker asks if you can do that again, but on the downbeat.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Return the work order with corrections in red ink

outcomes.text: You mark seventeen errors, including the date, the address, and the word improvament, and send it back graded. The magistrate's office, which loathes sloppy paperwork more than it loves the Baron, voids the order with a stamp you can hear across the courtyard. The movers leave with their ram and their feelings. The herald stays for breakfast, on the technicality that nobody served him notice to go.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: Your corrections are ruled marginalia, and marginalia, a clerk explains, carries no legal weight and considerable legal nuisance. The order stands, freshly annotated in your handwriting, which now reads like a confession. Pressure gathers along the street the way weather does. The widow files your red pen under lessons.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Barricade the lobby with the birthday trestle tables

success.text: The tables go up in a wedge formation the widow devised for a very different kind of party, and the movers cannot get purchase without scratching the varnish, which even they seem to feel would be crossing a line. They withdraw to await instructions. The tower cheers. The widow does not, because those tables are booked for Mr. Aldergate's ninetieth birthday, and now one of them has a boot print.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

fail.text: The wedge holds until the ram does what rams do. You are behind the second table at the time and briefly become part of the furniture. The movers step over you with apologies, then leave anyway, spooked by the choir descending the stairs in full voice like weather with opinions. The Baron adds a line to his ledger under damages, meaning you.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_coalition Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

text:
TENANT TOWER

By Friday the Baron has hired a picket of vacancy consultants to stand outside and discourage attendance, since the tower's rehearsals have begun drawing neighbors from three streets over. The widow answers by scheduling an open rehearsal, the caretaker prints programs, and the herald, who has attended every night this week in a personal capacity, has started bringing his own music stand. The trumpet has a slot now. Nobody remembers agreeing to this.

choices.text: Deputize the consultants as ushers, sashes included

outcomes.text: You issue each consultant a sash, a stack of programs, and the sacred power to shush. Within the hour they are showing neighbors to their seats and glaring at latecomers with a devotion the Baron never got out of them. Professional discouragement, it turns out, transfers beautifully to theater work. Their invoices continue to go to Vacant Holdings, which everyone agrees is the funniest part.
effects: schedule 2 r18_tenant_tower_crisis Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

The open rehearsal proceeds under a cloud of billing. The trumpet's slot moves twice to accommodate the arguing.

choices.text: Scalp the lobby's best sightlines to the neighbors

The chief consultant buys the stair landing for observational purposes and stands there all evening taking notes on attendance for the Baron. The widow says nothing, which from her costs more than a fine, and you watch the show from the back, next to the herald and his music stand.

Attendance doubles. The stairwell choir attempts a piece in eight parts and lands seven, which the caretaker calls a triumph with a vacancy. The herald copies the phrase into his notebook.

Nothing sells a rehearsal like a nosebleed at the door.

text:
TENANT TOWER

The orders arrive under the Baron's biggest seal: a vacancy hearing, to be held inside Lantern Tower itself, on the very evening of Mr. Aldergate's ninetieth birthday, a party that has been rescheduled four times and will not, the widow says, be moving again. The herald delivers the summons personally and cannot look anyone in the eye. He is to sound the trumpet during the cake, or surrender his post, his livery, and the trumpet.

choices.text: Sell the Baron's clerks the party's seating chart

outcomes.text: They pay in advance for a map of who sits where, so the magistrate can be placed at maximum legal advantage. You supply the chart from two reschedules ago, which seats him between the raffle tin and a man who no longer lives here. They pay again for a corrected copy that is also wrong. The birthday's true seating lives in the widow's head, where the Baron's coin has never once been legal tender.
effects: schedule 3 r18_tenant_tower_compromise Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Seat the hearing as item nine, between the twins and the cake

success.text: The magistrate arrives to find his hearing printed in the program with an interval on either side, and something in him, possibly the part that once had a grandmother, decides to allow it. He gets cake. He hears testimony in the form of toasts. Mr. Aldergate turns ninety in front of the law itself, which the widow calls the best security money cannot buy. The trumpet spends the evening in the cloakroom, checked in under its own ticket.
effects: schedule 3 r18_tenant_tower_compromise Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

fail.text: The trick candles are the twins' contribution and nobody briefs you. You lean in to steady the cake during the third relighting and give the tower a demonstration of how eyebrows burn, briefly but memorably. The party carries on around your smoldering dignity. Mr. Aldergate declares it the best birthday he has had in four attempts, and he has notes on the other three.
effects: schedule 3 r18_tenant_tower_compromise Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Cite the lobby's booking, four reschedules senior to the hearing

outcomes.text: You produce the booking ledger: the lobby was reserved for this birthday in writing before the hearing was so much as a gleam in the Baron's seal. Precedence of booking is the one law even his clerks fear, since their whole trade depends on it. The hearing is moved to the courtyard, where, as everyone knows, the trumpet does not work. The magistrate sends up his regrets and a modest gift.

Hearing and birthday will now share the room. Pressure settles over the tower like a tarpaulin. The widow begins reassigning seats with the face of a general who has read the terrain and packed accordingly.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Envelopes appear under every door on Saturday, wax sealed, offering flexible relocation packages with the first month free somewhere described only as comparable. The widow steams them open over the kettle for the rota's records and reseals them so neatly the wax looks flattered. Downstairs, the herald has begun taking trumpet lessons from the caretaker, strictly, he says, for professional development.

choices.text: Pool the relocation offers into the birthday kitty

outcomes.text: One by one the envelopes arrive at the widow's table, unopened or expertly resealed, and the tower votes to treat the Baron's generosity as a donation. The kitty has never been so healthy. Comparable, the caretaker observes, is doing a great deal of work in that sentence, and the choir sets the word to music, four notes, all of them doubtful.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: You collect envelopes floor by floor and learn on floor five that the kettle is not the only thing up there that steams. A door swings, a tray flies, and you take a teapot's worth of hospitality across the wrist. The envelopes survive. The widow logs your burn in the rota's margin as sustained in service, which comes with a biscuit.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Reply to every offer with a season ticket to the show

success.text: Each envelope goes back to Vacant Holdings containing one season ticket, row assignments strictly by date of eviction attempt. The Baron's office, unsure whether the tickets are mockery or assets, declares them assets, and now legally cannot dispose of them. Somewhere in a ledger, the Baron holds forty seats to a show about not leaving. The clerks request an aisle.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

fail.text: The Baron's office accepts the tickets and asserts standing as season subscribers, entitled to comment on the program. Notes arrive: less choir, more vacancy. The magistrate declines to rule on whether satire is binding, which everyone agrees is itself a ruling. The caretaker adds a second choir number out of spite, in a minor key.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Stage a fine print reading, admission one coin at the door

success.text: You read the relocation packet aloud from the lobby stairs in your best magistrate voice, and the crowd howls at clause nine, wherein comparable is defined as sharing at least one wall with weather. Coins fill the hat. The widow docks your standing for making the rota's business a box office, then stays for the encore. Clause eleven kills, as you knew it would.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

fail.text: The reading goes so well you extend the bill with selected excerpts from the tenants' own leases, names lightly redacted, laughter no longer entirely kind. The house doubles and the hat runs over. Floor five recognizes its own late fees and stops laughing. The widow closes your run, one night, sold out, never again.
effects: schedule 4 r18_tenant_tower_reversal Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Inventory night, and the widow's master keys finally meet the one lock nobody had matched, a low cupboard behind the lobby stairs. Inside sits the tower's original fixtures schedule, and item six stops the room: one brass horn, property of Lantern Tower, lent to the Baron's predecessor for ceremonial use, term ninety years, never returned. The caretaker does the arithmetic twice. The Baron has spent all season trying to evict the tenants with their own trumpet, and the loan expired on Tuesday.

choices.text: File a recall notice for one brass fixture, long overdue

outcomes.text: The recall goes out on tower letterhead, polite as a hymn, requesting the return of one horn, condition fair, sentimental value newly discovered. The Baron's clerks, who have built entire careers on the sanctity of fixtures schedules, cannot find a way around item six without burning down their own church. The trumpet comes home in a velvet case with an apology drafted by committee. The herald carries it up the steps himself, and indoors, where it works, it seems to sit up.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_legacy Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

outcomes.text: The clerks answer with a counterclaim: storage fees, ninety years, compounding. The sum has more digits than the tower has windows. The magistrate freezes the horn as disputed property, so the trumpet now legally belongs to nobody, which the herald says is the most peace it has ever known. The widow starts a fresh ledger page titled patience.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_legacy Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

choices.text: Name the Baron a price for the fixtures page

outcomes.text: You meet a Vacant Holdings clerk in a tea shop and sell him the page, folded twice, still warm from your pocket. He pays, inspects it, and pays again for your silence, which you price generously since it does not exist. The widow had three copies made the night the cupboard opened, one for the archive, one for the magistrate, one for under the cake tin. Your coin is real, and so is the seat you no longer have at the rota table.

choices.text: Pass the hat to frame item six for the lobby wall

outcomes.text: Everyone gives something, and the fixtures page goes up over the mailboxes in a frame the caretaker gilds himself, slightly crooked, all offers of straightening refused. Tenants touch the frame on their way out like a doorpost. The herald reads item six every evening he visits, moving his lips at the words never returned. It is either legal study or devotion, and nobody makes him choose.

outcomes.text: The framing party goes beautifully until the hanging, which you attempt from a chair, on tiptoe, against advice. The chair is act nine, and it travels. You come down with the frame held high and safe, sacrificing an elbow to history. The widow enters it in the rota as an injury sustained protecting documents, the tower's highest honor with a biscuit attached.
effects: schedule 5 r18_tenant_tower_legacy Lantern Tower is holding your seat by the stairwell.

text:
TENANT TOWER

Victory concert night, and the tower wears every lamp it owns. The Baron's charter, it turns out, only permits Vacant Holdings to hold vacant buildings, and Lantern Tower has never been less vacant in its life, so the portfolio quietly amends itself. Mr. Aldergate has the front row. The herald, out of livery for the first time anyone can remember, waits backstage with the trumpet and a written request signed by every floor: close the show.

choices.text: Sell souvenir eviction notices, signed by the herald

outcomes.text: He signs three hundred, seated at the raffle table, spelling every name right. You price them scandalously and the neighbors pay twice, once for the notice and once for the joke of framing it. The widow inspects the takings, removes the rota's share, and slides the rest back to you with one eyebrow doing the invoicing. It is the first money an eviction notice has ever made in this building, and the herald keeps asking whether his signature really helps, and it does, and that is the strange part.

It is the first money an eviction notice has ever made in this building, and the herald keeps asking whether his signature really helps, and it does, and that is the strange part.

choices.text: Give the herald the closing slot and the full choir behind him

outcomes.text: He walks out to the one place the trumpet plays, indoors, among tenants, and sounds it, by request, for the first time in its ninety year loan. Nobody is evicted. The note is long and low, the choir comes in under it four floors deep, and the caretaker keeps his hands raised a full breath after the sound has gone. Afterward the widow hands the herald a key, floor three, small room, good light, and tells him the rent depends on what he can do with an encore.

Afterward the widow hands the herald a key, floor three, small room, good light, and tells him the rent depends on what he can do with an encore.

The piano must reach the stage first, and you volunteer for the bottom corner, which is where pianos keep their weight. Something in your back files a formal complaint. The concert is glorious anyway, and the closing note comes down the stairwell to the cot where the caretaker has ordered you to lie flat. Heard from below, through four floors of tower, the eviction trumpet sounds exactly like being allowed to stay.

choices.text: Print the Baron's final notice as tonight's program

success.text: The program reprints the Baron's last notice in full, annotated in the margins by every floor, with the trumpet solo printed where the vacancy date used to be. The magistrate requests six copies, officially for training purposes, unofficially for his landlady. Scholars of paperwork will argue for years over whether the notice was served or staged. The tower's position is that there is no difference, provided the acoustics are good.

fail.text: A clerk at the printers reports the program upstream, and the Baron's lawyers arrive at the interval claiming performance of a legal instrument without a license. The magistrate, dragged from the front row, rules that he will need to see the whole show to be sure. The second half runs long, out of caution. The lawyers stay to the end, and one of them buys a souvenir notice, which his colleagues pretend not to see.

text:
REFINERY RIVER

On an ordinary morning. the river testing club discovers that Bright Tomorrow Petrochemicals has made a river that now catches fire politely official policy. The dispute centers on the water table. a fish serving as its own expert witness has been appointed to keep the process dignified.

choices.text: Take the private offer concerning the water table

outcomes.text: Bright Tomorrow Petrochemicals pays promptly and calls the transaction peace. a fish serving as its own expert witness witnesses the receipt. The immediate crisis softens for you and hardens for everyone else.

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Midnight, and a man in a gray coat is measuring the stockpot with a licensed ladle. He is the soup inspector from the Office of Licensed Hunger, and his notice declares that dinner is now a regulated activity, effective immediately, applications in triplicate. The night cook goes on chopping onions, because the onions were cut before the regulation and are therefore, she says, grandfathered. Three neighborhoods eat here after dark, and the inspector's ladle, christened Verity, is already leaning toward the soup.

choices.text: Chip in for onions and take a shift at the pots

outcomes.text: You put your coins in the onion tin and your arms in the washing-up, and by the second pot you are, administratively speaking, a ringleader. The inspector records you as 'one additional appetite, unlicensed, stirring.' When he reaches for Verity to take an official measurement, the ladle is already in the stockpot, serving an old man seconds. He writes this up as instrument error and underlines it twice.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: You seize the heaviest pot with confidence and set it down on your own thumb. The queue applauds the soup and boos the notice, which is not the calming effect the night cook wanted. The inspector measures the booing at three ladles, a new local record, and logs the thumb as an unlicensed injury. Verity, he notes, was in the stockpot at the time and cannot corroborate.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: File for a dinner license, in triplicate, tonight

outcomes.text: You complete the application at the bread counter, and it is flawless: nature of hunger, general; hours, whenever people are hungry, a condition the form has no box for, so you draw one. The inspector must accept any complete form, and this one is aggressively complete. He stamps it PENDING, which under Office rules means dinner may continue while the Office thinks, and the Office thinks slowly on purpose. Verity drips approvingly onto the stamp.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: You answer the form's question about projected diners with 'everyone,' which is honest and legally catastrophic. The Office reads 'everyone' as a confession of unbounded appetite and opens a file with a red cover. The inspector is instructed to attend nightly from now on. He seems privately pleased; the soup is better than the canteen at the Office.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Sell the Office the kitchen's serving schedule

outcomes.text: The Office pays well for the schedule and pays again for the recipe, which a sub-clerk files under CONTRABAND, SAVORY. Fourteen coins, and the only cost is that the night cook now serves you last, and silently. The inspector arrives at the listed hour to find the pots moved and the hour wrong; your map expired before the ink did, because the kitchen keeps no fixed hours, only hungry people. Verity is not on his belt when he leaves, and nobody can say where the ladle got to.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_assembly Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Seven o'clock in the bread cellar, where the three neighborhoods are drafting a reply to the Office's summons. The Office has scheduled a Licensing Vigil: applicants must stand a full business day over a symbolic empty bowl, to demonstrate respect for hunger as a regulated condition. The night cook points out that an empty bowl is the exact problem the kitchen was invented to correct. The soup inspector attends as an observer and spends the whole meeting looking for Verity, who is in the stockpot again, handle out, like a flag.

choices.text: Attend the vigil with the bowl already full

success.text: The regulation specifies a bowl and a business day; it does not, anywhere, specify emptiness. You stand the full day over a bowl of soup, refilling it as required, since an applicant must maintain the bowl 'in its presented condition.' Clerks come to check on the vigil and stay to check on the soup. By closing time the Office has eaten the exhibit, and the inspector rules the vigil observed 'in an enriched form.' Verity did the refilling; he logged her hours without meaning to.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_retaliation Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: You bring the full bowl, and a sub-registrar produces the appendix: presented bowls must match the illustration, and the illustration is empty. You are cited for Excess of Contents, a violation so rare the fine has to be looked up in the old currency. The vigil is ruled void and the summons reissued. The inspector confiscates the exhibit for evidence and returns the bowl the next night, washed, with Verity's compliments.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_retaliation Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Cater the Office's vigil at premium rates

outcomes.text: You quote the Office a ceremonial catering rate, and the Office, which cannot imagine an unpriced meal, pays it with relief. Fourteen coins for soup that was free two streets away. The clerks praise the vendor's generous portions; the night cook hears about the invoice and strikes your bowl from the shelf, which in the kitchen is a formal document. The inspector eats two servings and grades them privately in the back of the clipboard, a page that is becoming a habit.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_retaliation Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Put every neighborhood's pot on one rota

outcomes.text: You walk the three neighborhoods with a chalkboard and come back with a rota: hill pots on washing, river pots on bread, middle pots on the door. Nobody agreed to join anything; they agreed to Tuesdays. The Office cannot summon a rota, since a rota is not a body, it is a chalkboard, and the chalkboard declines to attend. The inspector copies it out anyway, in his best hand, for the file, and the file starts smelling faintly of thyme.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_retaliation Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: The hill neighborhood insists on seasoning rights, the river neighborhood on ladling rights, and you are between them holding the pepper when the dispute becomes kinetic. You take a wooden spoon to the ear, ceremonially. The rota survives; your hearing takes the evening off. The inspector measures the disturbance at two ladles, writes 'seasoning schism, contained,' and stays to help contain the leftovers.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_retaliation Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Dawn brings a procession up the oven lane: four clerks, a warden of seals, and a spool of crimson ribbon carried on a cushion. The Office has ordered the community ovens sealed until the license matures, which is the Office's word for a delay it caused. The warden reads the Rite of Discontinued Warmth aloud while the ovens, still hot from the night, soften the wax faster than he can apply it. The soup inspector stands at the back, checking his belt for Verity out of habit, though everyone including him knows where she is.

choices.text: Hold the oven doorway against the procession

success.text: You stand in the oven doorway holding a bread peel like a halberd, ceremonially, which is the only language the procession respects. Sealing a door with a person in it requires the Consent of the Obstruction form, which the warden did not bring. The procession retreats in good order to fetch it, and by the time it returns the ovens have produced a day of bread and a legend. The night cook says you looked ridiculous and that the constables will be back with the form and a grudge; the queue says you looked like a door with opinions. The inspector's report calls you 'architectural.'
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: The warden's escort turns out to include two constables of appetite with batons blessed for the purpose. You are removed from the doorway in a manner the report calls 'assisted compliance' and your ribs describe differently. The ovens are sealed with ribbon, wax, and a short hymn. That night the seals steam loose on their own, since sealing a warm oven is a promise wax cannot keep, but nobody tells the Office and the Office does not ask. Verity spends the raid hidden in the flour barrel, dusted white, like a veteran.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Scatter the pots into every hearth on three streets

outcomes.text: The stockpots leave by the back door in prams, tool bags, and one baptismal font on loan. By nightfall dinner is cooking in forty hearths, and no ribbon on earth is long enough. The Office has sealed two ovens and licensed, by omission, an entire district. Verity travels with the largest pot as its official escort; the inspector marks her down as 'seconded,' because the truthful word is not one the form offers.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: You carry the largest stockpot yourself, on principle, and the principle is heavier than it looks. Two streets in, your back files a grievance. The pots arrive and the dinner survives, but the hill neighborhood loudly blames the river neighborhood for the wobble in the broth, which becomes a whole thing. The inspector measures the whole thing at two and a half ladles, then helps you set the pot down, professionally, with both hands.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Appeal the seal on grounds of temperature

outcomes.text: You file an Objection of Ongoing Warmth: the Rite of Discontinued Warmth applies, by its own first line, to ovens that have discontinued. These ovens are hot, therefore continuing, therefore outside the rite, therefore the wax is litter. The warden checks the first line twice and goes pale in the manner of a man betrayed by his own liturgy. The seals are withdrawn 'pending cooling,' and the kitchen makes certain the ovens never learn what cooling is. The inspector testifies to the temperature personally: he has measured it, he says, with an instrument he trusts.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: The tribunal accepts your objection and then discovers a counter-appendix: warmth pending review is official warmth, and official warmth requires a warmth license, which is a different queue. You have won the argument and doubled the paperwork. The ovens stay lit under an interim permit that expires hourly and must be renewed by whoever is standing nearest, so the queue takes turns. The inspector renews it twice himself before remembering whose side he is on, and cannot immediately remember.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_coalition Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Thursday's dinner runs from the hill to the river, one long table made of every borrowed door in three neighborhoods. The Office responds with a Census of Appetites: every diner must be enumerated, and an unenumerated mouthful is now a misdemeanor. A hook has appeared by the kitchen door with Verity hanging on it, polished, as if she lives there, and the soup inspector has begun arriving out of uniform, on the theory that a coat cannot witness what it is not worn to. Three neighborhoods eat; officially, nobody does.

choices.text: Seat the census takers mid queue, by the grandmothers

success.text: You seat the census takers between the hill grandmothers and the bread, which is a tactical position no clipboard survives. They come to enumerate mouths and stay to be one. Their return to the Office lists a total of 'some,' with gravy on the figure, and the census is quietly declared complete. The inspector, out of uniform, takes Verity down from her hook and serves the second sitting like a man who has stopped arguing with himself.
effects: schedule 4 r18_free_kitchen_crisis Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: One census taker refuses soup on procedural grounds, and the hill grandmothers treat refusal as a medical emergency. In the resulting hospitality you catch an elbow meant for a bread basket. The census taker escapes uncounted and unfed, the worst of both worlds, and files a report describing the district as 'aggressively generous,' so the Office triples the enumeration budget. Verity is found afterward in the stockpot, as usual; the inspector no longer writes this down anywhere official.
effects: schedule 4 r18_free_kitchen_crisis Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Declare the dinner legally nonexistent, with citations

outcomes.text: You read the Office's own definitions aloud over pudding: dinner, 'a meal exchanged for consideration'; diner, 'one who so exchanges.' Nothing here is exchanged, everything is given, therefore by the Office's own dictionary there is no dinner, no diners, and nothing whatsoever to enumerate. The census takers check the definitions and find them, to their horror, load bearing. The Office marks the district NO DINNER DETECTED, which the neighborhoods paint on a banner and hang over the longest table in the city. The inspector laughs once, briefly, into his sleeve; Verity rings against the pot like a small bell.
effects: schedule 4 r18_free_kitchen_crisis Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: Your citation is flawless, so the Office amends the dictionary overnight, which it can do, because it owns the words. Dinner now means 'any food eaten after a time the Office will announce later,' and the retroactive appetite of that sentence unsettles the whole district. The census resumes at double strength, and the banner is impounded as evidence of a meal. The inspector reads the new definition three times, closes the clipboard, and goes to wash pots without saying anything at all.
effects: schedule 4 r18_free_kitchen_crisis Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Sell the census a headcount, rounded generously

outcomes.text: You invent a figure with the confidence of a man who has seen a spreadsheet, and the Office pays for it twice: once for the number, once for the certified copy. Fourteen coins for arithmetic that was never performed. The number is wrong in both directions at once, which takes talent, and the Office builds a quarterly forecast on it. At dinner your bowl is filled last and least; the night cook says the ladle did it, and Verity, in the inspector's absence, is not answering questions.
effects: schedule 4 r18_free_kitchen_crisis Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Eleven bells, and the soup inspector arrives in full ceremonial gray for the strangest duty of his career: a formal inspection of premises the Office has certified as nonexistent. Regulation forbids official lamplight in an unofficial building, so the inspection will proceed by candle, with the clipboard held at reading distance from the flame. He unrolls the Rite of Examination and asks, first question, for the establishment's ladle. There is a silence with three neighborhoods in it, and then Verity is handed forward, warm from the pot, and he looks at her the way men look at letters from home.

choices.text: Submit to inspection, candle by candle, in full form

outcomes.text: The kitchen passes the Rite of Examination in eleven candles: pots, boards, hands, all found 'exemplary, considering they do not exist.' The inspector's report is a masterpiece of the conditional tense; it praises soup no one served, in a building no one built, to diners no one counted. The Office cannot fault the report, cannot file it, and cannot stop reading it, so it circulates internally as forbidden literature. On his way out the inspector leaves Verity on her hook, and for the first time it looks less like forgetting than like custody arrangements.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_compromise Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: Candlelight is honest, and so is the pot scrubber who answers the question of how long the kitchen has operated with 'since the famine, obviously.' A nonexistent kitchen with a history is a paradox the clipboard cannot hold. The inspector is required to escalate, and the file goes upstairs, where files go to breed. Pressure settles on the district like weather. Verity stays in the stockpot the whole night; whatever happens upstairs, she has stopped pretending to be evidence.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_compromise Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Sell candle seats to watch the secret inspection

outcomes.text: You sell viewing seats to the curious: one coin standing, two coins with soup, and a premium bench for people who enjoy watching a man interrogate a ladle. The inspection acquires an audience, the audience acquires opinions, and the Office is reviewed aloud in real time, which no rite anticipates. Fourteen coins, and the inspector's hands shake slightly through the fifth candle. The night cook calls it a circus; she is not wrong, and she is not refunding anyone either. Verity performs beautifully under pressure, which is more than can be said for the Office.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_compromise Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Feed the inspector first, as guest of honor

success.text: You seat him at the head of the table with a full bowl before a single question is answered, because in this kitchen the examination waits for the meal and never the reverse. He eats by candlelight with three neighborhoods watching, and somewhere in the second bowl the inspection quietly changes hands. He asks how the broth gets its depth; the night cook says that is a licensed secret. He writes 'depth: confirmed' and signs it. Verity serves him thirds without being asked, insubordinate to the last.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_compromise Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: The guest of honor's chair collapses, because it was a borrowed door balanced on two crates and the district's carpentry runs on hope. You break his fall with the arm you like best; he is unhurt, and you are filed under 'furnishings, injured.' The neighborhoods argue for days about whose crate failed. The inspector finishes his soup standing up and grades it, out loud, for the first time: eight ladles of ten. The room goes quiet; nobody knew the scale went that high.
effects: schedule 5 r18_free_kitchen_compromise Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

Noon at the Office of Licensed Hunger, where the Deputy Sub-Registrar of Appetite receives the kitchen under a portrait of the first licensed meal. The Office proposes terms: a provisional dinner license, renewable forever, in exchange for fixed hours, fixed portions, and a registry of the hungry. The night cook observes that hunger does not keep fixed hours, and the Deputy replies that it will once it is licensed, that being the point of licenses. On the table between them, listed as Exhibit A, lies Verity, recalled to the Office for recalibration she is visibly not receiving.

choices.text: Take the Office's coin to draft the license yourself

success.text: The Office hires you to draft the terms, since you are the only person present fluent in both languages. You write a license with teeth and no jaw: hours fixed as 'whenever the licensee is hungry,' portions fixed 'to the size of the need.' The Deputy signs it with the long pen, satisfied that dinner is at last contained. The kitchen never notices it is licensed, which was the craft of it, though the night cook eyes your Office coin in a way that costs you something anyway. Verity comes home in the exhibit crate, listed as a rounding error.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: You draft honestly and the Office edits harder; every soft clause comes back with a buckle on it. The license fixes dinner at two hundred portions, seven to nine, names registered at the door, and you are paid in full, which is the part the queue remembers. The night cook posts the new hours upside down in protest, and the Office cites her for inverted display. Verity stays impounded as Exhibit A. The inspector visits her on his lunches; this is known, and no longer discussed.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Decline the registry; offer the Deputy a bowl instead

outcomes.text: You decline the registry on behalf of everyone it would list, and you slide a bowl across the treaty table, because the kitchen negotiates in its own currency. The Deputy, who has not eaten unregistered food since taking office, commits a misdemeanor in four spoonfuls. Terms soften on the spot: registry withdrawn, hours 'customary,' portions 'traditional.' Nobody defines the words and everybody understands them. On the way out the night cook lifts Verity off the exhibit table with the calm of a woman repossessing a relative, and no clerk in the building finds a form fast enough to object.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: The Deputy takes refusal as ingratitude, and the meeting ends in a scrum of ushers with ceremonial staves. You are escorted out with more thoroughness than the doorway requires. The terms stay on the table and the registry stays in them, but the story of the refused bowl walks the three neighborhoods overnight and comes back taller. Verity remains Exhibit A, though someone in the evidence room has begun polishing her, which the Office would investigate if it dared open that cupboard.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Accept the license, issued to the soup itself

success.text: You accept on one amendment: the license names the soup, not the kitchen, as the licensed party. The Deputy agrees, pleased to license anything at all. But a licensed soup may lawfully be anywhere, served by anyone, at any hour: the license attaches to the broth, and the broth is portable. The Office has regulated the one participant it can never summon. The inspector, ordered later to inspect the licensee, reports that it was delicious and fully compliant, and files Verity's measurement as its signature.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

fail.text: The Deputy's counsel spots the trap at the last second and names the stockpot instead, and a pot, unlike a soup, can be impounded. The big pot spends a week in Office custody wearing an evidence tag while dinner limps along in small pans, portions thin, tempers thinner. The pot is eventually released for lack of testimony. It comes home with Verity inside it, smuggled by a party the Office declines to identify and everyone else can name in one guess.
effects: schedule 2 r18_free_kitchen_reversal Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

The scandal breaks on Office letterhead: an internal audit finds the Office canteen has been serving the midnight kitchen's soup for a month, bought at the back door by a clerk with a pram and a guilty walk. The Office of Licensed Hunger is, by its own definitions, a den of unlicensed dinner. The Deputy Sub-Registrar orders the matter contained; containment requires an inspector; the only inspector with jurisdiction is a man who now spends his evenings elbow deep in the evidence. He arrives at the kitchen holding Verity and his coat, one of which he intends to return.

choices.text: Buy the inspector an apron with a ladle hook

outcomes.text: The kitchen pools coins and presents him with an apron, gray, well cut, with a hook at the hip exactly where a belt would carry a ladle. It is the only regalia he has ever received that fits. He inspects it for a long moment, then hangs his Office coat on Verity's hook, which everyone present understands to be a resignation letter in wool. The Office receives the actual letter two days later; it is one sentence long and smells of thyme.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_legacy Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: The apron ceremony is going beautifully until the Office arrives to repossess its coat, its clipboard, and if possible its man. In the tug of war over official wool you are on the losing end of a sleeve seam and go down over a bench. The coat tears along the shoulder, which every tailor in three neighborhoods pronounces unfixable with visible satisfaction. The inspector keeps the apron and the Office keeps half a coat. Verity keeps her hook, and her opinion.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_legacy Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Report the Office to the Office, forms attached

outcomes.text: You file a citizen's complaint accusing the Office of unlicensed dinner, attaching the canteen menu as evidence and the Office's own dictionary as expert witness. Procedure is merciless: the complaint is valid, the defendant is the plaintiff, and the fine is payable from the Office to itself, with a processing fee it cannot waive. The cheapest exit, the clerks calculate, is to declare the midnight kitchen's soup retroactively licensed since the famine, so that no crime occurred anywhere. It is so ordered. Dinner has been legal for years; nobody thought to check.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_legacy Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

outcomes.text: Your complaint is airtight, so the Office invokes the Doctrine of Internal Weather: events inside the building are not events, they are climate. The canteen scandal is reclassified as humidity. You are invoiced one coin for tracking weather into the lobby; you frame the invoice instead of paying it. The district hears all of this and draws its own conclusions about buildings where the truth is a form of damp. The inspector testifies to nothing, on the advice of Verity, who has never once filled out anything.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_legacy Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

choices.text: Supply the canteen's contraband broth at a margin

outcomes.text: You find the clerk with the pram and offer to become his supplier, at a markup, cutting out the guilt. The Office canteen now buys its contraband through you, invoiced as 'consulting broth.' Fourteen coins and rising. The night cook knows, because the night cook always knows, and your bowl arrives full but cold, which in this kitchen is a formal censure. The inspector audits the canteen ledger, finds your handwriting, and grades it two ladles: legible, shameless.
effects: schedule 3 r18_free_kitchen_legacy Dinner requires a license; the ladle disagrees

text:
FREE KITCHEN

A year on, the Office of Licensed Hunger publishes an official recipe titled Civic Broth, Standard Issue, which three neighborhoods recognize down to the thyme. Attribution is disputed: the Office cites internal development, the kitchen cites the famine, and the truth is in a pram somewhere between. The midnight kitchen still serves whoever comes, and its strictest volunteer is a man in a gray apron who grades the soup nightly and posts the score on the door. Verity has not measured rebellion in months; there is none left at her depth, only dinner.

choices.text: Demand the Office prove authorship from memory

success.text: You challenge the Office under its own Rules of Provenance, which require an author to reproduce the work from memory before witnesses. A committee attempts the broth in the Office atrium and produces something the official taster describes, in writing, as 'punitive.' The kitchen reproduces it blindfolded, at scale, while arguing, which the rules do not forbid. Provenance is awarded to 'the district, historically,' which is how the Office spells surrender, and the reprinted cards carry no author at all, the correct name for it. The inspector grades the settlement nine ladles, explaining that a ten would only make the Office suspicious.

fail.text: The committee demands the kitchen's proof first, and the night cook refuses on the grounds that the recipe is not written anywhere, it is remembered in shifts. Unwritten evidence is inadmissible, so the Office keeps its byline. The neighborhoods respond by renaming the dish out loud: everyone orders 'the stolen soup,' including, eventually, the Office canteen, on official requisition forms. The Office wins the attribution and loses the noun. The inspector posts that night's score with a footnote: eight ladles, provenance disputed, flavor beyond appeal.

choices.text: Sell certified copies of the disputed recipe

outcomes.text: You print handsome certificates of the recipe, stamped AUTHENTIC in a font you found convincing, and sell them to clerks, tourists, and two archivists who should know better. Fourteen coins, and the recipe now exists in more disputed editions than most scriptures. The night cook points out that anyone can have it for nothing by asking at the door; you explain that this is exactly what makes the certificate valuable, and she concedes the economics while confiscating your dinner privileges for a week. The inspector buys one, grades it, and files it under FICTION, ORNAMENTAL.

choices.text: Take the midnight shift for good

outcomes.text: You take the midnight shift permanently, which in this kitchen is the closest thing to a title anyone holds. Your name goes on the rota in the night cook's own chalk, misspelled, which is how you know it is affectionate. The Office sends a final letter reserving all rights over dinner in general; it is used to prop the door open in warm weather, where it serves. At close of night the inspector tastes the pot and writes eight and a half ladles on the door, docking the half, as always, 'to leave the kitchen something to want.' Verity hangs above the stove now, licensed to no one, polished by everyone, the only instrument in the city that measures what it serves.
