You take the shift and discover why it was open: the midnight pot is the heavy one, and the cellar stairs were built by optimists. You and the pot arrive at the bottom in a different order than planned; the soup is saved, and your shoulder is granted a rota of its own. The hill and river neighborhoods each blame the other's stairs, which comforts no one and is a tradition already. The inspector grades the rescue nine ladles and the stairs zero, and posts both. By morning a carpenter has confessed to the steps just to be part of the record.

text:
VETERANS PEACE

Six in the morning, and the banners go up over the memorial square: REMEMBER THE FALLEN, VACANCIES AVAILABLE. General Market Opportunity has booked the stone, the flags, and a table where a clerk pins medals on anyone who attends the meeting about medals. At the square's edge the sergeant who cooks parks a soup pot on a trolley and reads the banner twice, as if it might correct itself. Beside him the widow who keeps the list holds forty names in a cloth folder, the way you hold a sleeping thing. Behind them, the veterans against the next war unfold a trestle table that has survived worse mornings.

choices.text: Read the permit aloud to the clerk

outcomes.text: The permit licenses mourning, and mourning only; the clerk checks the schedule of permitted emotions twice and finds no line for recruitment. The banners come down by category, VACANCIES first. The General's aide notes your name with a pen that cost more than the soup pot. The sergeant ladles you a bowl for reading the small print at the correct volume.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_assembly Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: The clerk finds an appendix: grief-adjacent commerce, permitted where tasteful. Tasteful is defined by the General's office, in a font that costs extra. The banners stay up, and you are now a name in the objections column, which the aide underlines twice.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_assembly Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Take the drive's stipend as a heritage associate

outcomes.text: The pay is real and prompt, which is more than the fallen ever got out of this office. You hand out leaflets with a golden bugle on them and learn the phrase career pathway in a bereavement context. At the table they pin an attendance medal on you for attending your own hiring. The sergeant says nothing, which takes him a while.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_assembly Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Help wheel the soup pot onto the square

success.text: The pot goes up the memorial steps on three planks and an old argument about angles. By noon it is steaming beside the stone, unbannered, unbranded, smelling of onions and patience. The widow sets her folder on the pot's lid, the one surface in the square she trusts. You are on the rota now; the sergeant writes your name in pencil, which is his version of a vow.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_assembly Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: The trolley wins on the third step and the pot takes your shoulder down with it. Soup on the flagstones, gulls incoming, the clerk already drafting an incident report titled UNLICENSED BROTH. The sergeant hauls you up, checks the pot before your arm, and apologizes for doing it in that order. You are on the rota anyway; injuries count as attendance.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_assembly Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

Back room of the boxing club, folding chairs, a kettle that takes sides. The wake is planned like a supply line: who cooks, who carries, who reads, who catches the widow's eye if a name sticks in her throat. Across town the drive is planning too; their agenda leaks, and item one is a second medal, awarded for attending the meeting about the first medal, with a bar for punctuality. In the corner of their payroll sits the bugler with the bad lip, who has started, rumor says, practicing laments on his own time.

choices.text: Sell the wake's timings to the drive's marketing man

outcomes.text: He pays in crisp notes and calls the wake a competing grief event, which he writes down without flinching. Now their bugle is scheduled directly opposite the reading of the names. The kettle boils at you, specifically. Some of the younger veterans start talking about unplugging things, and not gently.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Take the carrying shift on the rota

success.text: You get tables, chairs, the good urn, and the crate of the memorial's spare bronze letters, kept in the mason's shed since the stone lost its second N. The widow checks your knots and upgrades one, kindly. The rota fills, name by pencilled name, until the wake has more hands than the drive has slogans. The kettle accepts you.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: The good urn survives; your hand argues with a door frame and loses. The sergeant wraps it with field habits he never talks about and moves you to the shift that carries opinions instead. The rota holds. The kettle, mollified, pours you one first.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Write the wake's invitation to outshine the banner

outcomes.text: You keep it to nine words: soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome. That is nine more honest words than the square has seen in weeks. The market posts it, the boxing club posts it, and somebody pins one to the drive's own table, between the medals.

effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: Your second draft adds a joke about the General's bugle, and the joke escapes into circulation. His office logs it as harassment of a musical instrument. The invitation still travels, but now it travels with a case number attached.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_retaliation Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

Tuesday, and the square wakes up renamed: OPPORTUNITY PLAZA, on a sign bolted where the pigeons used to argue. The medal meeting is weekly now, attendance paid, loudspeakers going up on poles like tin flowers. The widow finds the crate of spare letters gone from the mason's shed, requisitioned, receipt pending. The sergeant reads the new sign for a long moment and stirs the pot as if the pot were the one being renamed.

choices.text: Stand with the widow at the mason's shed

outcomes.text: You and half the rota fill the doorway while the requisition men consult a clipboard that outranks them. What they came for does not leave; the letters end up under the widow's stairs with the folder of names on top, standing guard. The chess players move their board onto the shed step, and a game that slow is very hard to move.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: The requisition men come back with a van and fewer manners. You take an elbow in the ribs defending a crate of punctuation. The letters ride away to be weighed; the widow writes the van's number in the back of the folder, where the accounts of the living are kept.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Contest the renaming at the registry of places

success.text: The registrar, a woman who has renamed nothing in thirty years and is proud of it, produces the square's title deed: memorial in perpetuity, pigeons implied. OPPORTUNITY PLAZA lasts eleven days, which the chess players agree is longer than it deserved. The sign comes down; the bolts stay, four small holes the square wears the way old soldiers wear weather.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: The registrar is away on secondment; her replacement wears a lanyard from the General's office and a stamp that says STRATEGIC. The renaming is upheld pending review, and review is where place names go to retire. You are advised in writing to direct all grief through approved channels.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Attend the medal meeting and take notes

success.text: They pay you a per diem at the door, which answers your first question about the attendance figures. The agenda is one item: the awarding of medals for being present at the awarding of medals; the minutes record that the minutes were recorded. You leave with four coins, a chestful of tin, and pages the widow files under evidence. The rota eyes your medal until you turn it around.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: You ask one question too clever and get promoted to observer emeritus, with stipend. It is astonishing what they will pay a person to sit still while a bugle is described. The notes are good; the look the sergeant gives your new lapel is better documented still.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_coalition Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

The wake needs bread, tables, and forty chairs that agree with each other, so the rota goes visiting. The market remembers the fallen by their orders: two of sugar, the heel of the loaf, a man named Wren who bought thyme every Friday and taught the sergeant the soup. The chess players offer their tournament tables on the condition that nothing patriotic happens to them. Attendance medals keep arriving in the kitty, donated by owners who would rather they were bread.

choices.text: Ask the stallholders for day-old bread, in their language

outcomes.text: You talk margins, not memory: day-old loaves at closing, dignity by the crate, baskets returned washed. The flour man signs the rota in flour. By Thursday the wake has more food promised than the drive has budget codes, and the drive has a great many budget codes.
effects: schedule 5 r18_veterans_peace_crisis Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: You over-egg it and quote the General's own slogan at a baker who lost a nephew. The stall goes quiet in a way ovens cannot. You leave with an apology to deliver and no bread; the sergeant sends the widow next, who says eight words and comes back with the van full.
effects: schedule 5 r18_veterans_peace_crisis Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: License official wake souvenirs to a quick printer

outcomes.text: The printer pays upfront for exclusive rights to a grief he has not attended. By evening there are tea towels with the memorial on them, second N missing, faithfully reproduced. The kitty will not touch the money, so it sits in your pocket, warm as a borrowed coat. The pigeons approve of nothing.

choices.text: Put your own coins in the bread kitty

outcomes.text: You reach the shed as two of the drive's postermen are improving its door with a golden bugle. Words happen, then a shove, and the chalk tally takes your cheekbone's side, but from the ground. The postermen leave half a poster up, which reads REMEMBER THE, and for once the branding is accurate.

text:
VETERANS PEACE

The drive announces its Grand Medal Meeting: same square, same hour as the wake, loudspeakers doubled, attendance mandatory for every medal holder in the district. The letter calls it a Unified Remembrance Experience and lists the fallen as legacy assets. The widow reads that phrase once, closes the folder, and asks the room, very quietly, who is free on Thursday to stand in a square until morning. Every hand goes up, and the kettle's cord appears to be trying.

choices.text: Hire on as night steward for the medal stage

outcomes.text: The drive pays double for overnight setup and asks no questions about where your sympathies sleep. You bolt a stage together over the chess squares and are issued a clipboard, a lanyard, and your second attendance medal, one per lapel, in case of symmetry. From the pot's side of the square, nobody waves. The pay is very good; the soup, you happen to know, is better.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_compromise Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Stand the night in the square with the rota

outcomes.text: Forty of you hold the flagstones the old way, in shifts, with soup. The chess players play by candlelight and declare the square occupied by residents, which no clipboard has a box for. When the stage crew arrives at dawn there is nowhere to put a stage that is not full of breakfast. The foreman, a reasonable man on overtime, has his crew carry tables instead, and the drive is billed for it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_compromise Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: The night turns cold enough to file a complaint about. You take the two a.m. shift and the frost takes a toe's worth of interest in you. But dawn finds the square still held; the sergeant hands you a bowl and says nothing at length. The stage crew waits on the curb like a delivery no one ordered.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_compromise Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: File against the double booking of the square

success.text: The registrar is back from secondment and in a mood scholars will one day study. Two events, one square, one permit: she rules that mourning has seniority, having booked the stone first by several wars. The Grand Medal Meeting is relocated to the drive's own car park, where the loudspeakers can finally address the parking.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_compromise Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: The hearing is scheduled for the morning after it would matter, a scheduling art the General's office has raised to a craft. Your objection is received, stamped PENDING, and filed in a drawer with the square's original name. Thursday will be decided in the square itself, by whoever is standing in it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_veterans_peace_compromise Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

Thursday, six o'clock, and the square is halved by a chalk line nobody drew officially. On one side, the drive: bunting, medal table, a stage with rows of empty chairs marked RESERVED. On the other, the wake: the pot, the candles, the long tables, and the widow with the folder, waiting for seven. On the chalk itself stands the bugler with the bad lip, warming up, looking at his sheet music like a man reading two letters at once.

choices.text: Serve soup across the chalk line to anyone

outcomes.text: The pot does not check lapels. Half the drive's ushers drift over on their break and stay; one holds bowls, then trays, then a candle. By seven the chalk line is mostly a place where people queue from both directions. The General's aide records a catering irregularity, which the pot declines to read.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: An usher's supervisor arrives mid-bowl and the bowl becomes a jurisdiction. In the tug of it you take hot soup down your wrist, which the first aid box has opinions about. The usher is marched back across the chalk; he leaves his candle on the wake's side, lit.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Give the bugler an honest set list

outcomes.text: You put one page on his stand: play what they played for Wren. At the fanfare cue he lifts the bugle and lets a lament out of it, slow, the bad lip steady for once. There is no procedure for interrupting a lament, and the loudspeakers relay it beautifully to the whole district. The medal table waits with sixty medals and no fanfare to hide behind.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: The stage manager smells the substitution and swaps the sheet back at the last minute. The bugler, caught between contracts, plays the recruitment fanfare so badly it becomes a kind of review. His office fines him for tone. The wake starts anyway, seven sharp, names first.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Unplug the loudspeakers at the source

success.text: The cable runs behind the mason's shed, and then, briefly, it does not. Sixty reserved chairs hear the General's speech at the volume of one man in a square, which is its true size. On the wake's side the reading of the names needs no amplifying; it never did. The rota is grateful, and also on record disapproving of your methods, both sincerely.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

fail.text: The generator's guard is an old sapper with a longer reach than his job requires. You get the cable and he gets you, twice, professionally. The loudspeakers survive, and the drive adds a line item for security, hazard rate. You watch the wake begin from the first aid tent, which at least has good acoustics for the names.
effects: schedule 3 r18_veterans_peace_reversal Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

After midnight the bugler comes to the boxing club carrying his contract, his bugle, and a ledger he was never meant to see. There it is in the drive's own hand: the mourning booked as a conversion funnel, grief segment; the bugle billed as an emotional asset; and one line the room reads twice. Medal stock: sourced from memorial bronze, spare letters, three squares' worth, requisitioned. Every attendance medal in the district is somebody's missing N, melted down and pinned to whoever sat through a meeting about it. The kettle boils without being asked, which everyone takes as a vote.

choices.text: Lay the ledger before the registrar

outcomes.text: She reads it standing up, which for her is a riot. Melting a memorial is theft from the dead, and the dead, it turns out, have standing; the drive's permit is revoked with a stamp she has kept sharp for years. The General departs to pursue opportunities elsewhere, the one line of his branding everyone believes. The bugler's fine is struck out, and his contract, on inspection, was never legal to begin with.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_legacy Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

outcomes.text: The registrar reads it and agrees; her superior reads it and goes to lunch. The file ascends to a level where ledgers are weather. Still, copies exist now, and copies are a kind of pigeon: they roost everywhere and cannot be recalled.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_legacy Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Sell the ledger back to the General's office

outcomes.text: The aide names a figure before you finish the sentence, which tells you what the sentence was worth. The ledger goes into a briefcase, the briefcase into a car, and the car has a bugle on the door, gold, tasteful, definition pending. The bugler watches his evidence drive away and starts, very quietly, learning to whistle. The money is real. So is the whistling.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_legacy Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

choices.text: Stand by the bugler when the drive comes for him

success.text: They arrive at the boxing club with a breach of contract and leave without him; forty people are drinking tea in front of the door, slowly. His bugle is declared an asset in dispute, and the room disputes back, and the room has more chairs. That night he plays at the wake's rehearsal, unbilled, unfined, the bad lip finally on the right side of the chalk.
effects: schedule 4 r18_veterans_peace_legacy Soup at six, the names at seven, all welcome

text:
VETERANS PEACE

A month on, the square keeps its old name and its four bolt holes, and the stone has its second N again, recast from returned medals. The last medal meeting was adjourned for lack of attendance, permanently; the minutes record that no minutes were needed. What tin remained went to the market smith, who made the sergeant a ladle with the faint impression of a bugle on the handle, upside down. The wake is a fixture now, first Thursday of every month, Wren's thyme in the pot, and the rest of the time the square belongs to the pigeons and the chess players, who run it well.

choices.text: Accept the General's settlement to tell it his way

outcomes.text: The cheque arrives with a nondisclosure the length of a campaign history. You sign, and somewhere a version of events is printed in which the drive concluded successfully and the soup was incidental. The money spends fine. At the wake they still fill your bowl, from the ladle, which is the trouble with soup: it keeps no ledger.

The money spends fine. At the wake they still fill your bowl, from the ladle, which is the trouble with soup: it keeps no ledger.

choices.text: Take the first Thursday rota, indefinitely

success.text: Your name goes in the book in ink this time; the pencil era, the sergeant says, is over for those who stayed. You learn the work of it: the counting of chairs, the warming of the urn, the pause before each name that is not silence but making room. Peace, it turns out, is mostly lifting things. The ladle hangs by the door between shifts, retired from ceremony, on active service.

fail.text: You take the rota, and on your first Thursday the memorial steps collect your ankle like an old debt. The widow does the carrying while you do the reading, which is how you learn the pause is the hard part. Nobody pins a medal on you for it, on principle, and the principle is now chalked inside the shed door.

choices.text: Write the square's new notice board yourself

outcomes.text: You keep the tone the square taught you: CHESS BY THE SHED, SOUP FIRST THURSDAYS, PIGEONS AT ALL TIMES, MEETINGS ABOUT MEDALS ADJOURNED, SEE LADLE. Visitors laugh in the right place and then go quiet in the right place, which is everything a notice can hope for. The registrar has a copy framed.

Your first draft is eloquent, which the square does not need. The chess players edit it down to eleven words with the ruthlessness of men who play the long game against each other twice a week. The eleven words are better. Your name is not on them, and the square keeps working anyway.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

The closure order arrives Tuesday, stamped twice, addressed to the reading room where the keepers of the names say the registry of vanished families aloud, one name per breath, every week. The Ministry of Necessary Forgetting has sent a censor to supervise the closing. He makes it one step past the card catalogue, sneezes eleven times, and withdraws to the corridor: the man is allergic to alphabetical order, and the registry is kept perfectly. At the lectern the reader marks her place at the letter F and waits for quiet.

choices.text: Take the Ministry's fee to map the reading room

outcomes.text: The Ministry pays fourteen in coin for a tidy map of the reading room: who sits where, which drawer holds which letter, where the lectern stands. You draw it well, and by evening the street knows you drew it. In the corridor the censor shelves your map under miscellaneous, the one heading that costs him nothing. The reader watches you pocket the money and returns to the letter F, and somehow that is the whole conversation.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_assembly One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Buy candles and take a turn at the lectern

outcomes.text: Candles are three coins and the reading runs long past dark. You take the lectern for the letter G: a glover who whistled through his teeth, a girl who collected other people's door keys, a schoolmaster who was kind about wrong answers. The reader nods you into the rhythm, one name per breath, no faster, and the radiator knocks along as if it has always known the pace. In the corridor the censor blows his nose, and the keepers count you as one of their own.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_assembly One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You take the lectern and read standing for three hours, because sitting feels wrong and nobody corrects you. Somewhere in the letter H the floor comes up to meet you, and it is surprisingly comfortable, which is worrying. The keepers prop you on the bench by the radiator, put water in your hand, and decline to make it a story; you are one of them now, and this is how they treat their own. Outside, a rumor starts that the Ministry has begun starving the readers, which was not the Ministry, it was you skipping lunch.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_assembly One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Demand the closure order's legal grounds in writing

outcomes.text: You ask, in writing, under which statute a reading in progress may be closed, and you request that the reply be alphabetized for ease of filing. The Ministry takes eleven days to answer and concedes that no such statute exists in any order. The censor is recalled to the corridor on a standby basis, which he appears to find restful. The reader adds your letter to the registry's box of useful papers, which is the highest honor she keeps on a shelf.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_assembly One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You cite the Charter of Open Shelves with great confidence; it was repealed, it turns out, before the shelves in question were built. The Ministry thanks you for drawing its attention to the gap and begins drafting something worse to fill it. In the corridor the censor sneezes twice, which the keepers have learned to read as sympathy. The reader does not scold you; she moves you to candle duty, where confidence does less harm.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_assembly One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

Week four, and the reading room is full: mill hands, two ferrymen, a midwife who lost a whole street of mothers. The Ministry of Necessary Forgetting now requires an attendance register in triplicate, delivered by the censor, who passes the forms through the doorway on a long pole while holding his breath. Newcomers keep rushing the names out of nerves, and the reader will not have it. Each of the vanished gets one whole breath; that is the entire liturgy, and it is not negotiable.

choices.text: Teach the newcomers the pace, one name per breath

outcomes.text: You sit with the newcomers and teach the pace the way it was taught to you: say the name, then the trade, then let the breath finish on its own. A dye worker who sang to her vats. A ferryman who feared deep water and crossed anyway, twice a day, for thirty years. By the end of the night the rushing has stopped, and the reader gives you the small nod she otherwise reserves for correctly kept paper.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You teach the pace all evening and forget to keep any breath for yourself, and your voice goes somewhere around the letter M. The midwife feeds you honey in hot water and tells you that grief is work and work wants shifts. Outside, someone mistakes your croaking for the start of a protest song, and the street picks it up with enthusiasm. The censor, alarmed, reports an incident; the reader, unmoved, puts you down for a rest week as if it had always been on the rota.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Register the reading as a standing seminar

success.text: You enter the reading in the rolls of the public archive as a standing seminar, subject: local history, enrollment: open, duration: until complete. The clerk who approves it does not look up, which is how most mercy gets through. A seminar in progress cannot be closed without a curriculum review, and no reviewer can enter the room; the censor confirms this by trying, from the doorway, and sneezing his signature onto the form. The reading is now, officially, education.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: The seminar application requires a syllabus, so you write one: names, in order, forever. The review board rejects it for lacking measurable outcomes, and the Ministry circulates the rejection as proof the reading is unserious, then pencils the room in for a follow up inspection with relish. The keepers pin the rejected syllabus to the wall, where it reads better than most certificates. The censor delivers the bad news through the door on his pole and, unusually, apologizes to the pole.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Sell the Ministry a weekly attendance report

outcomes.text: The Ministry offers fourteen in coin for a weekly account of who attends, and you take it. You write the report in strict alphabetical order, small kindness or small cowardice, because that way the one official who handles it cannot bear to read it. The censor files your pages unread, eyes watering, week after week; the Ministry believes it is watching the room, and the room believes nothing, because the room saw you take the money. The reader moves your chair one place farther from the lectern, and nobody moves it back.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: The Ministry offers fourteen in coin for a weekly account of who attends, and you take it. You write the report in strict alphabetical order, small kindness or small cowardice, because that way the one official who handles it cannot bear to read it. The censor files your pages unread, eyes watering, week after week; the Ministry believes it is watching the room, and the room believes nothing, because the room saw you take the money. The reader moves your chair one place farther from the lectern, and nobody moves it back.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_retaliation One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

Someone has been in during the night. The catalogue drawers hang open and the cards lie shuffled across the floor like a frost, ten thousand names out of order, which is to say: a door propped open for the censor. He stands at the threshold at dawn, breathing freely for the first time in months, and does not look happy about it. The reader kneels among the letters and begins with A, because there is nowhere else to begin.

choices.text: File a trespass complaint against the Ministry

outcomes.text: You submit a trespass complaint, itemized: one lock, one latch, ten thousand cards disturbed, each one numbered and named. The magistrate rules that disorder inflicted must be paid for at the clerical sorting rate, per card, and the Ministry of Necessary Forgetting learns what memory costs by invoice. The fine buys new drawers with brass labels. The censor reads the ruling in the corridor and sneezes once, preemptively, out of respect for what the drawers will become.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You submit a trespass complaint, itemized: one lock, one latch, ten thousand cards disturbed, each one numbered and named. The magistrate rules that disorder inflicted must be paid for at the clerical sorting rate, per card, and the Ministry of Necessary Forgetting learns what memory costs by invoice. The fine buys new drawers with brass labels. The censor reads the ruling in the corridor and sneezes once, preemptively, out of respect for what the drawers will become.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: Your complaint names the Ministry directly, and complaints that name the Ministry are handled by the Ministry. The file is transferred, then improved, then lost; you are invited to resubmit it in a format that does not exist. Meanwhile the shuffled cards wait on the floor, patient as only paper is. The keepers sort while you appeal, and the reader suggests, gently, that next time the two efforts might go in that order.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Bar the door against the night clerks

success.text: The clerks come back the second night for the registry itself and find you in the doorway with a lantern and no plans for the evening. There are six of them and one of you, but seizing an archive requires a witness signature, and you decline, at length, to provide one. They withdraw to seek instructions, and instructions, at the Ministry, take days. The reader is not grateful: the keepers hold names, not doorways, and she worries aloud about what you have taught the Ministry to bring next time. The street, though, sleeps prouder.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: The clerks come with a filing cart, and a filing cart, fully loaded, wins arguments. You wake on the corridor floor with a bruise the shape of a drawer handle and the censor's spare handkerchief folded under your head, which neither of you ever mentions again. The cart took the spare catalogue and left the registry, because the clerks could not tell which was which and the censor, consulted, declined to go in and check. The Ministry posts guards on its own basement afterward, having discovered that archives can be seized.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Sort cards until dawn with the keepers

outcomes.text: You kneel at the letter A with the keepers and sort until the windows go gray. Every card gets said aloud as it goes home: a cooper, a bell ringer, a woman who salted her tea and defended the habit. By dawn the registry is whole, the room is in order, and the censor, arriving for his shift, gets no farther than the mat. He looks at the restored drawers almost fondly, sneezes, and goes off to report that nothing can be done.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You sort all night and your hands give out before the alphabet does: paper cuts across both thumbs and a cramp that stays for days. The midwife wraps your thumbs and remarks that paper draws more blood than people expect. By dawn the registry is whole anyway. The street, hearing of the break in, is out of temper for a week, and the reader posts a night rota so that nobody has to be furious alone.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_coalition One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

A laundry two streets over has started keeping its own list: eleven names, read aloud over the mangle every washing day. Then a chapel, a boatyard, a tea room with a back shelf. None of them call it anything, and all of them keep it alphabetized, on quiet advice, since a certain official cannot come near a well ordered list. The censor has begun arriving early on reading nights to stand in the corridor, and the keepers pretend not to notice that he mouths along at the letter A.

choices.text: Sell a printer exclusive rights to the story

outcomes.text: A printer offers fourteen in coin for exclusive rights to the reading room's story: illustrated, dramatic, the sneezing official as comic relief. You sell it. The pamphlet is lurid, popular, and wrong in the ways popular things are wrong, and the Ministry quietly adores it, because a room that is entertainment is easier to close than a room that is grief. The street argues about it for a week, and the boatyard sends elsewhere for advice now. The reader never mentions it, which is a sentence you serve rather than hear.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: A printer offers fourteen in coin for exclusive rights to the reading room's story: illustrated, dramatic, the sneezing official as comic relief. You sell it. The pamphlet is lurid, popular, and wrong in the ways popular things are wrong, and the Ministry quietly adores it, because a room that is entertainment is easier to close than a room that is grief. The street argues about it for a week, and the boatyard sends elsewhere for advice now. The reader never mentions it, which is a sentence you serve rather than hear.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Carry spare pages to the other reading rooms

success.text: You spend a week walking pages to the other rooms and teaching the pace: name, trade, breath. At the laundry you learn their eleven by heart; at the boatyard the list is kept in chalk, and the foreman rewrites it after every rain without being asked. Nothing gets a title and nothing needs one; there are simply more rooms now where the vanished are spoken of as people. The censor's allergy is said to trouble him across half the district, which he reports, accurately, as a worsening of conditions.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: You walk pages to four rooms in one day, and the rain gets into your boots and then your chest. The tea room feeds you broth and takes the pages anyway, and their list goes up on the back shelf, damp but in order. You cough for a week. The chapel's copy, made in your failing handwriting, holds one name in the wrong place for three days, and you walk back through the rain to set it right, because misplaced is a small word for what it is.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Have the registry certified as a reference collection

outcomes.text: You petition the public archive to certify the registry as a reference collection: cross indexed, cited in two almanacs, indispensable to the study of who lived here. Certification means the Ministry cannot close it without publishing a notice of what, exactly, is being lost, and naming things is the one expense the Ministry of Necessary Forgetting cannot bear. The certificate arrives with a ribbon. The reader puts the ribbon in the box of decorations and the certificate in the box of armor.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: You petition the public archive to certify the registry as a reference collection: cross indexed, cited in two almanacs, indispensable to the study of who lived here. Certification means the Ministry cannot close it without publishing a notice of what, exactly, is being lost, and naming things is the one expense the Ministry of Necessary Forgetting cannot bear. outcomes.text: The certification hearing goes sideways when the assessor asks for the registry's scientific value and you say, truthfully, that it keeps people from disappearing twice. Truth reads as sentiment in a hearing room, and sentiment scores poorly. Certification is denied pending further review; the Ministry cites the denial in three internal memos with visible relief and pencils the room in for one of its patient looks. The keepers keep reading, and the almanacs cite the registry anyway, out of scholarly spite.
effects: schedule 2 r18_archive_names_crisis One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

Two in the morning, and there are wagons at the door of the reading room. The Ministry has ordered the registry seized tonight and, having learned, sent the censor in first with an inventory shuffled by machine, so that no order on the page can trouble him. He stands in the doorway holding the scrambled list, able to enter at last, and looks past the lantern to the lectern, where the reader is already standing. She opens the registry to the letter A and begins, at two in the morning, the full reading, and the wagon horses settle as if this had always been the plan.

choices.text: Hold the doorway with readers and a soup pot

success.text: Word moves at the speed of knocking, and by half past two the doorway is three deep with readers taking their turn: the midwife, the glover's daughter, a ferryman still in his nightshirt. Someone carries the soup pot down and it steams on the step like it has opinions about sieges. By the Ministry's own regulations a proceeding in progress may not be interrupted, and a full reading, one name per breath, takes until Thursday. The wagons leave at dawn to seek clarification. The censor stands in the doorway all night, holding his shuffled inventory, and never takes the step his list would allow; asked later why the seizure failed, he writes, allergy, and no one is cleared to question that.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_compromise One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: The knocking works, the doorway fills, and the reading holds until Thursday, but you spend the whole siege on the cold step ladling soup, and your back registers an objection it never withdraws. The wagons leave; the street stays up for three nights afterward, angry in shifts. Somewhere in the crowd a bowl goes through a window, which the reader calls, flatly, not our liturgy. The registry is intact, and so is your new certainty that stone steps in March are not furniture.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_compromise One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Wake a magistrate and contest the seizure order

outcomes.text: You wake a magistrate, who arrives in a coat over a nightgown and no mood. The seizure order, read by lamplight, authorizes removal of the archive as inventoried, and the inventory, machine shuffled for the censor's comfort, matches nothing in any drawer. Invalid as written, resubmit in proper order, she rules, knowing the Ministry cannot alphabetize the list without its own official fleeing the building. She also rules, unprompted, that the soup pot may remain, claiming jurisdiction over everything on the step. The wagons leave empty except for the horses' opinion of the whole affair.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_compromise One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: The magistrate you wake turns out to owe the Ministry a favor, and the hearing on the step is brief. The order stands; the wagons roll forward; then they stop, because the drivers refuse to load an archive over a doorway full of people reading, not from principle, they explain, but because the manifest does not list work of that kind. The registry survives on a technicality of the carters' rulebook, and your legal gambit survives as a cautionary tale. The reader adds the magistrate's name to a different, much shorter list she keeps in her apron.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_compromise One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Take the night fee and unlock the side door

outcomes.text: The Ministry's man on the step offers fourteen in coin for the side door key, and the key is in your pocket, and then the coin is. The clerks go in the side way and meet the alphabet head on: the censor, following his own scrambled inventory, leads them in circles past drawers he cannot face, and by half past three the seizure has dissolved into sneezing and contradictory orders. The registry survives the night without you. That is the part the keepers remember: not that the door opened, but whose pocket the key came out of.
effects: schedule 3 r18_archive_names_compromise One name per breath, every week, in order.

The clerks go in the side way and meet the alphabet head on: the censor, following his own scrambled inventory, leads them in circles past drawers he cannot face, and by half past three the seizure has dissolved into sneezing and contradictory orders. text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

The Ministry's offer arrives on good paper: the reading room may remain open as a statistical abstract, names replaced with quantities, one line per district, condolences available in aggregate. Signature expected by Friday. In the corridor the censor now sits on a cushion he brought himself and has stopped pretending he is not listening; last week he corrected a newcomer's pronunciation through the door, quietly, and got it right. The reader sets the offer on the lectern, underneath the registry, where it can think about what it is.

choices.text: Accept the word abstract and redefine it

success.text: You accept the designation and return the contract with one definition appended: an abstract, for the purposes of this room, is a short account of a single person, comprising name, trade, and one habit, read aloud. The Ministry's lawyers, trained to fight deletions, have no procedure for an addition, and the definition stands. The registry is now a book of four thousand abstracts, fully compliant. The censor reads the amended contract in the corridor and makes a sound the keepers charitably log as a sneeze.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: Your amended definition goes back on Friday and comes back on Monday, rejected, with a note observing that words mean what the Ministry needs them to mean. Worse, the exchange has taught them what the room actually values, which is a thing you never hand an opponent in writing. The offer is withdrawn in favor of something patient and unspecified. The reader takes the news evenly and puts an extra week of readings on the rota, the way other people stock firewood.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Let the scholar pay to copy pages for study

success.text: A scholar of the public archive offers four coins a week for permission to copy the registry, page by page, for study. You watch her work: she copies the trades and the habits too, and when she reaches an entry that is only a name, she leaves a respectful margin in case the rest is ever found. Her first paper calls the vanished a population of particular persons and cites a beekeeper's ladder as evidence. The keepers pocket the fee uneasily, on the grounds that copies leave rooms; you note privately that this is also what makes copies useful.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

fail.text: The scholar's institute doubles the fee and sends its own copyists, who are quick, accurate, and wear gloves. The copies come back stamped SPECIMEN in the corner, one stamp per page, four thousand stamps. They are locked in a reference drawer across the city, preserved, catalogued, and never read aloud, which is a kind of vanishing with better paperwork. You are paid eight; the reader does not ask where the money came from, and the not asking is its own line in the ledger.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Refuse the counts, learn ten names by heart

outcomes.text: You refuse the quantities by the only method that matters: everyone in the room takes ten names to learn by heart, trade and habit included, so that the registry has forty living backups with opinions. The midwife takes her ten from the letter M and gets, among them, Odile Marsh, who kept bees and lent her ladder to anyone, and whose blue chair still sits by the window because no one has had the heart to call it furniture. Reciting practice runs after every reading now. The Ministry's offer expires unsigned on the lectern, and nobody informs it.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

The midwife takes her ten from the letter M and gets, among them, Odile Marsh, who kept bees and lent her ladder to anyone, and whose blue chair still sits by the window because no one has had the heart to call it furniture. outcomes.text: The memorizing goes slower than the plan: names hold, but trades and habits slip, and getting a person half right feels worse than looking them up. You stay three nights running drills and wear yourself down to a cough. The street hears that the Ministry means to swap names for numbers and takes it badly; two clerks are jeered off their own doorstep. The reader ends practice each night the same way, with the full name of one person and a minute of quiet, and the minute is the part everyone gets right.
effects: schedule 4 r18_archive_names_reversal One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

Sunday, before the reading, the censor comes to the door with his hat in both hands and no forms. He says there is a room in the basement of the Ministry of Necessary Forgetting that he has never been able to enter, and that this week, after four years of walking past it, he finally asked himself why. The reader looks at him for a long moment and then fetches the corridor stool in herself. Behind that door, he says, the Ministry keeps its own index of the vanished: complete, cross referenced, and perfectly alphabetized, because forgetting, done professionally, requires excellent records.

choices.text: Sell the basement tip to a scandal sheet

outcomes.text: A scandal sheet pays fourteen in coin for the tip and prints it in letters usually reserved for war: THE MINISTRY REMEMBERS. Half the city refuses to believe it, the other half always knew, and the arguments run for a week on every corner. Under cover of the noise the basement is emptied and moved somewhere the censor has never worked, so the keepers lose the index and keep only the confirmation. The censor takes it hardest: he brought them a door, and it was sold as a headline.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

Half the city refuses to believe it, the other half always knew, and the arguments run for a week on every corner. Under cover of the noise the basement is emptied and moved somewhere the censor has never worked, so the keepers lose the index and keep only the confirmation. The censor takes it hardest: he brought them a door, and it was sold as a headline.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Pay copyists to transcribe the basement index

outcomes.text: You hire three copyists with your own money, and the censor draws the basement from memory: which corridor, which hours, which clerk takes long lunches on saints' days. The copying takes two nights, and the whole index comes out in aprons and bread baskets, four thousand entries returning to the letters they came from. Where the registry had only a name, the Ministry's index has trades, streets, dates, the bones of whole persons, and the keepers spend a week welcoming their own dead home properly. The censor stands in the corridor while the merged registry is re-sorted, allergic and satisfied, a combination he reports to no one.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

outcomes.text: The copying is nearly clean: two nights, three copyists, one basket left behind with a heel of bread and four pages of the letter R. A porter finds the basket; a whistle finds the porter; you get out over a wall with the last satchel and an ankle that will describe the wall to you for a month. The pages that made it home are read the following Sunday, trades and streets restored. The four left behind are read too, names only, with the respectful margin the scholar taught everyone to leave.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

choices.text: Demand certified copies of the Ministry's own index

outcomes.text: You take the censor's testimony to the certification bench and demand the Ministry produce its index, on the reasoning that an office cannot both deny the vanished existed and employ a man to alphabetize them. The Ministry's lawyers ask for time and are given, instead, a deadline. Certified copies arrive in eleven crates, delivered with the special sullenness of an institution obeying its own rules. The keepers merge the records aloud, one person per breath, and the censor requests, and is granted, the honor of not being in the building when the crates are unpacked in order.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

The Ministry's lawyers ask for time and are given, instead, a deadline. Certified copies arrive in eleven crates, delivered with the special sullenness of an institution obeying its own rules. The keepers merge the records aloud, one person per breath, and the censor requests, and is granted, the honor of not being in the building when the crates are unpacked in order.

outcomes.text: The bench accepts the petition and the Ministry accepts the bench, which is to say the hearing moves buildings twice and arrives smaller each time. In the end a sub clerk rules that the basement index is internal stationery and therefore exempt from existing. The ruling is laughed at on four streets, which does not overturn it, and the inspectors come around more often for a while. The censor, cited in the pleadings as an anonymous official with a health condition, buys a very plain hat and wears it low for a season.
effects: schedule 5 r18_archive_names_legacy One name per breath, every week, in order.

text:
ARCHIVE NAMES

Forty households, forty pages, one registry. That is the reader's answer to every future seizure: the registry copied out by hand into the family bibles of the district, one page per household, in between the births and the marriages, where no ministry has ever had jurisdiction. The reading room keeps the original and the habit; the houses keep the insurance. On the lectern the registry lies open at the letter A, and by the window Odile Marsh's blue chair holds the afternoon light the way it always has, as if it had been asked to.

choices.text: Take page seventeen into your own keeping

outcomes.text: You copy page seventeen into the bible between your grandmother's marriage and a cousin's birth, and the names settle in as if they had been waiting for the room. Learning them takes a winter: a chandler, a bell founder's wife, a boy listed only as apprentice, loved, which is a full biography if you read it slowly. On Sundays the retired censor comes up the lane with the week's new page, because the households are ordered by lane and kinship, no alphabet anywhere, the one delivery route in the district his condition permits him to enjoy. He accepts tea, corrects one pronunciation, and gets it right. The Ministry of Necessary Forgetting eventually closes the registry file as unenforceable, and the keepers do not celebrate; there is a reading on Thursday, and the pace does not change for good news.

Learning them takes a winter: a chandler, a bell founder's wife, a boy listed only as apprentice, loved, which is a full biography if you read it slowly. On Sundays the retired censor comes up the lane with the week's new page, because the households are ordered by lane and kinship, no alphabet anywhere, the one delivery route in the district his condition permits him to enjoy. He accepts tea, corrects one pronunciation, and gets it right. The Ministry of Necessary Forgetting eventually closes the registry file as unenforceable, and the keepers do not celebrate; there is a reading on Thursday, and the pace does not change for good news.

You take page seventeen and then, because you are you, you walk all forty houses in one wet afternoon to check every other page against the original. Thirty nine are perfect; one has a mended corner and a tea stain shaped like an apology. The street, watching you squelch from door to door, concludes the Ministry must be planning something, and spends the week on edge. You catch a cold that stays a fortnight, and the retired censor, meeting you on the lane, lends you his handkerchief, ironed, and does not ask for it back. The reader hears about the forty house audit and adds you to the rota under a heading she does not explain: continuity.

choices.text: Scatter the order so no list can be seized whole

success.text: You arrange the dispersal so that no two households hold consecutive pages, and the only complete index lives in the reader's head and one sealed envelope. Any future seizure would need forty warrants in forty parlors, served in an order no one can determine without the envelope, and the envelope is kept, the reader says, safely. A Ministry actuary prices the closure and attaches the figure to a recommendation of strategic patience. The censor, retired, walks the lanes each Sunday delivering new pages and reports the whole arrangement uncatalogable, with something in his voice the keepers have stopped logging as a sneeze.

fail.text: Your scattering scheme is elegant and slightly too elegant: two households receive the same page, and one receives an envelope meant for the reader. For three days the letter S exists in duplicate and the letter W not at all, and a widow crosses the district in the rain to swap papers, because her page, she says, has people on it. A Ministry observer notes the confusion hopefully in a memo, and the inspectors linger for a while. By Sunday the retired censor has quietly re-walked the whole route and set every page where it belongs, lane by lane, no alphabet involved; the reader amends the plan in pencil and does not amend her opinion of cleverness, which was already on record.

choices.text: Sell the emptied binding to the Ministry

outcomes.text: A Ministry procurement officer offers fourteen in coin for the registry's original binding, emptied, to display in the atrium as proof the archive was closed. You sell it; the pages were already forty houses away, and the binding is only leather and thread and the smell of the room. The Ministry exhibits it under glass with a small plaque and considerable satisfaction, and schoolchildren are marched past it for years, each one told the matter is finished. On Sundays, along the lanes, the finished matter is delivered page by page by a retired official with an excellent hat, and read aloud on Thursdays, one name per breath. You were paid in full, which is what the plaque says too.

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

Gallery Nine, ten in the morning: the descendants' return delegation stands before a glass case holding their grandmothers' cooking pots and household gods, labeled ARTIFACTS OF A VANISHED PEOPLE by people who are visibly not vanished. The under-curator explains, with the patience of a man who has practiced in a mirror, that ownership legally expired during shipping. The eldest descendant asks who did the shipping. He declines to check.

choices.text: Inventory the case beside the delegation

success.text: You read the placards aloud while the eldest descendant corrects them from memory, shelf by shelf, until the official versions start to sound like guesses. A scholar from the research halls begins taking notes on her side of the rope, which is how you learn the rope has sides. The under-curator repeats that ownership expired in transit, and you ask whether the transit is over. He does not have a form for that. The delegation decides you will do.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_assembly The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: The guards escort you out by a method the museum calls guidance and your ribs call a door frame. Outside, the delegation is already chalking the case's contents on the pavement from memory, item by item, and a crowd is checking the museum's windows against the list. The under-curator watches from the steps, saying ownership expired in transit to no one in particular. You limp home enlisted.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_assembly The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Demand the provenance file for case nine

outcomes.text: You cite the public registry statute and request the file for case nine dash four. The under-curator produces it proudly, since paperwork is the one thing this museum has ever truly collected, and the first page lists the objects as PURCHASED, SELLER UNAVAILABLE, PRICE UNRECORDED. Even the clerk who fetched it winces. The delegation thanks him warmly for the confession and orders a certified copy.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_assembly The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The records office explains that provenance files are released only to owners, and the owners are unavailable, having expired in transit. You point out that the file is what would prove who the owners are, and the office agrees that this is exactly why it cannot be released. The circle is perfect, and you walked it in public.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_assembly The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Take the museum's fee as a shipping expert

outcomes.text: The museum pays handsomely for an independent expert to confirm that ownership evaporates somewhere over open water. You sign a statement, then a supplementary statement, then a receipt for signing statements. The under-curator shakes your hand as a colleague, which is the moment your hand starts to feel rented. Outside, the delegation watches you leave by the staff door. The money is real, which is more than the museum can say for its title.

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

The grandmother's kitchen table has become a planning floor, the museum's blueprint weighted down with teacups and every exit relabeled in pencil as an entrance. The locksmith who only locks things anymore is delighted, since this job is a burglary run backwards. The honest forger is drafting provenance papers that are entirely true, which she says is the hardest forgery of her career. The problem is simple: the building is designed to keep objects in, and the objects want out.

choices.text: Tour the museum as a visiting damp surveyor

outcomes.text: You arrive with a clipboard and a theory about damp, and no institution on earth can resist a theory about damp. The under-curator personally walks you through the freight corridors, indicating every door, every lock, and every schedule while you nod gravely at the skirting boards. The locksmith transcribes your notes into a route by suppertime. The museum has excellent drainage, you report, truthfully, because the forger insists on accuracy.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_retaliation The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: Your theory about damp collapses when the under-curator turns out to have his own theory about damp, with citations. Two hours of rising ground water later you know nothing about the freight corridors and a great deal about membrane failure. The crew redraws the plan from the public galleries and the grandmother's memory of the service stairs, which is older than the blueprint and, it turns out, more accurate.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_retaliation The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Sell a copy of the pencil marked blueprint

outcomes.text: A private collector's agent pays generously for the marked blueprint, no questions asked, which is itself an answer. You keep the original and sell the copy, telling yourself that geometry belongs to everyone. The money arrives in a plain envelope with a card reading FOR FUTURE CONVENIENCE, and the crew now shares its plan with a stranger who wears gloves indoors. The teacups get moved to a different kitchen.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_retaliation The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Rehearse the carry with bricks and laundry

success.text: Every evening the crew rehearses in the yard: pots wrapped in shirts, gods cushioned in bedding, passed hand to hand up a staircase drawn in chalk. The eldest descendant times the runs and criticizes your elbows. By the fourth night a full case's worth travels forty yards in ninety seconds without one clink. The laundry baskets have never felt so important, and neither, frankly, have you.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_retaliation The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: The rehearsal goes flawlessly until the stand-in god, a brick in a pillowcase, meets the yard's one uneven flagstone through your hands. The brick survives; your toe files a grievance; the eldest descendant institutes a rule about boots that is clearly about you. The neighbors, drawn by the shouting, stay to help carry, so the crew grows by four and the plan by one flagstone warning.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_retaliation The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

An invoice arrives addressed to the household gods themselves, care of the delegation: storage fees, glass polishing, curatorial attention, ninety years compounding. The museum has decided that if the gods have owners after all, then the owners owe rent. The under-curator has also introduced an admission fee for anyone who looks at the case with recognizable longing. Retaliation, in an institution, always arrives itemized.

choices.text: Slip into the records annex after hours

success.text: You go in alone without telling the crew, which they will hold against you, and find the shipping ledger the invoice was too proud to check: crates, dates, and a receiving stamp reading ACQUIRED IN TRANSIT in the museum's own ink. A copyist pays you four coins for an honest duplicate before dawn. The crew is furious about the solo trip and jubilant about the ledger, in that order.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: You find the shipping ledger, and also a drawer of unregistered miniatures that would never be missed, and you resolve the tension in your character by pocketing two. The fence pays well and asks nothing, unlike the crew, who ask where you were. The ledger goes unmentioned along with everything else, and the plan proceeds with one page missing that only you know exists.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Pass a hat to pay the longing fee in bulk

outcomes.text: You stand at the door with the street's hat and pay the longing fee for everyone the museum tries to charge, one by one, loudly, with receipts. By noon the gallery is full of neighbors gazing at the case with maximal recognizable longing at bulk rates. The under-curator calculates that the fee is now generating attendance he cannot explain to his superiors. Retaliation, it turns out, can point both ways.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The doorman decides that a hat of coins constitutes unlicensed banking, and the discussion becomes physical near the turnstile. You take an elbow; the turnstile takes worse; the street takes note. The fee is suspended by evening, officially for maintenance, unofficially because the museum has discovered what longing looks like in bulk.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Contest the storage invoice before the magistrate

success.text: You argue that storage requires a bailment, a bailment requires consent, and the gods signed nothing. The magistrate examines the invoice, asks the museum's counsel who exactly commissioned ninety years of glass polishing, and strikes the bill with a ruling that no one may charge rent to property they claim as their own. The museum has been billing its own story, and now there is a stamp saying so.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: The magistrate accepts the museum's argument that the invoice is not a bill but a courtesy notice of costs incurred, which cannot be contested because it demands nothing. You object that it arrived with a payment envelope. Counsel replies that envelopes are traditional. You lose on tradition, the oldest clause there is.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_coalition The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

Midnight in the museum's freight yard, where the night cleaners take their break among empty crates, and the crew has come recruiting. The plan needs doors, and every door in that building has a person who knows its moods: the cleaners, the boiler man, and the registrar of outgoing loans, who has stamped three in thirty years and remembers each like a wedding. The locksmith calls them the hinge crew. The forger just calls them colleagues.

choices.text: Stand the night shift a proper supper

outcomes.text: Three coins buy bread, sausage, and the good beer, and you eat sitting on crates while the boiler man explains which corridors go warm at four when the pipes wake. Nobody is bought; everybody is fed; the difference is that bought people stay quiet and fed people talk shop. By the end the registrar has agreed, in principle, that some loans are overdue in the outgoing direction. The forger takes notes on a napkin she will later certify.
effects: schedule 3 r18_museum_return_crisis The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The supper goes beautifully until the night supervisor arrives off schedule, and the exit over the freight gate costs you a palm's worth of skin. The cleaners cover for you so smoothly it is clearly not their first cover. Word moves through the yard that the museum's own people are choosing sides, and the museum, counting mops in the morning, can feel it.
effects: schedule 3 r18_museum_return_crisis The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Ask the registrar for an outgoing loan

outcomes.text: You ask the registrar what an outgoing loan requires, and he recites the form from memory with tears in his eyes, a man who has waited thirty years to be needed. Destination institution: the forger's papers name the grandmother's kitchen as a certified house museum, which by any honest definition it is. The registrar reads the application twice and pronounces it the finest he has ever received. It is also entirely true, which the forger says was the difficult part.
effects: schedule 3 r18_museum_return_crisis The gods want to go home and the files agree

You draft the application anyway and it dies in his tray, beautifully complete. The registrar files it under PENDING with the care of a man pressing a flower. The plan reroutes through the loading dock, where signatures matter less than shoulders.

choices.text: Pocket the rival collector's finder's fee

outcomes.text: The gloved agent returns with an offer: his employer will happily receive the objects for safekeeping the moment they leave the museum, and he pays finders in advance. You take the fee and give him a delivery date, which is not the real one, though you have not yet decided by how much. The envelope says FOR CONTINUED CONVENIENCE. The crew's plan now has a fork in it that only you can see, and it pays better than the truth.
effects: schedule 3 r18_museum_return_crisis The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

The provenance archive at two in the morning is not where you planned to be: the plan said the file room would be empty, and the plan lied. The under-curator sits at the reading desk in his coat among open ledgers, and you are in the doorway holding the museum's original receiving book, stamped ACQUIRED IN TRANSIT ninety years ago in ink the museum mixed itself. Somewhere below, the night bell is trying to decide whether it heard something. He looks at the book, then at you.

choices.text: Read him the receiving book aloud

success.text: You read the entries the way clerks fear most, slowly, with the dates. Crate by crate the museum's own hand admits what shipping meant: acquired in transit, seller a garrison, price a signature. When you finish, he says he has been rereading them all week, which is not the line either of you rehearsed. He does not ring the bell. He initials your copy, and archives file confessions and countersignatures exactly the same way.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_compromise The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: You read the entries aloud and he corrects your pronunciation of the old shipping terms, which is how you learn he knows the book by heart. Knowing it by heart, it turns out, is not the same as being moved by it, and he rings the night bell mid sentence. You leave through the window with the copy half made and the alarm inventing your description. The original stays with a man who can recite it and won't.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_compromise The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Accept the hush money he counts out

outcomes.text: There is a discretionary fund for moments like this, and the under-curator counts it out with the sorrow of a man feeding a machine he built himself. You take the money, hand back the receiving book, and walk out through the front door like a patron. Behind you he files the confession back where nobody asked for it. The coins are heavy, which the crew would call conscience and a fence would call silver, and by morning you still have not decided who is right.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_compromise The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Whistle for the cleaners and pass the book

outcomes.text: One whistle and the archive goes dark, mop buckets rolling through the corridor like weather. The receiving book travels hand to hand down the freight stairs, wrapped in a dust sheet, faster than any alarm can gossip. By the time the lights return you are outside holding nothing, and the book is three streets away being read aloud in a kitchen. The under-curator, left alone with an empty desk, does not report the buckets.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_compromise The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The whistle works; the stairs do not. You take a railing in the dark at speed, and the book survives because you elect to land on your face instead. The kitchen gets its reading; you get an eyebrow that will tell this story for years. The museum blames the archive lights on the wiring, fires no one, and quietly doubles the locks, which the locksmith takes as a professional compliment.
effects: schedule 4 r18_museum_return_compromise The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

Three crates stand on the loading dock at dusk, packed to the forger's true manifest: gods in bedding, tools in shirts, the great feast pots nested like sleeping cats. The museum, sensing weather, has offered a compromise, a long term loan of the objects to their own descendants, renewable at the museum's pleasure, over the under-curator's signature in a hand that has started to waver. The eldest descendant read the offer, folded it into a fan, and fanned herself. Tonight the loan goes out under older terms.

choices.text: Shoulder the crates past the watchman

success.text: The watchman opens his mouth and you walk through it, crate first, with the boiler man and two cleaners carrying like pallbearers at a wedding. Nobody swings anything; mass and certainty do the arguing. By midnight the feast pots are on a stove for the first time in ninety years, and the street eats from them, which was always their provenance. The museum posts a reward for the pallbearers' names, which makes the yard a warier place, and the research halls disapprove of your methods in a memo nobody finishes.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: The watchman turns out to be two watchmen and a bar across the freight gate that the blueprint called decorative. The crates make it; your shoulder pays the toll, three days of it. The museum hires guards in matching coats by the weekend, and the coats change what the yard feels like at night. The pots still reach the stove. You eat the homecoming supper one armed.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Stage the getaway as a delivery in reverse

outcomes.text: At the agreed hour the street arrives at the dock as itself: porters, laundresses, the man with the vegetable cart, everyone with an honest reason to be carrying something. The objects go home the way they left, in ordinary hands, except this time the direction is correct. No one runs, because deliveries do not run. The eldest descendant walks the smallest god home in a shopping basket, and the getaway is indistinguishable from an evening.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The delivery in reverse works until the vegetable cart loses a wheel at the worst corner in the city, and you hold up the axle with your back while gods and turnips change hands above you. Everything arrives; your spine files paperwork for a week. A patrol takes down names it cannot alphabetize, since the street gives them freely and identically. The neighborhood spends the next day pleasantly agitated and slightly fed.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Sign the museum's own loan offer and load up

outcomes.text: The forger reads the museum's compromise twice and starts laughing at clause nine, which requires the borrower to store the objects with due domestic care but never says where. You sign the museum's own offer, load the crates, and leave a receipt. The gate guard checks the paperwork and finds it perfect, since the museum wrote it. The feast pots go home cooking within the hour, under terms drafted by the people who lost them, which the forger frames as her masterpiece: a true document, written by the mark.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree
