outcomes.text: Clause nine holds; clause fourteen does not: renewable at the museum's pleasure turns out to mean revocable by lunch. A courier reaches the kitchen with a recall notice before the pots are even warm. The eldest descendant signs for it, thanks him, and files it in the stove. Legally the position is now unclear, which is at least an improvement on legally clear and wrong, and the pots stay put while the letters multiply.
effects: schedule 5 r18_museum_return_reversal The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

Someone knocks at the kitchen door before dawn carrying a ledger wrapped in his good scarf: the under-curator, resigned as of midnight, holding the museum's original shipping manifests. He has been rereading his own doctrine, he says, and it holds: ownership really does expire during shipping. The manifests show that every crate the museum ever received arrived by shipping. He would like, if convenient, to watch things go back on shelves.

choices.text: Hand him an apron and a low shelf

success.text: The eldest descendant looks him over, decides remorse is a kind of reference, and assigns him the lower shelves, where the damage would be least. He handles each god like a man being introduced. By noon he knows the household order better than the catalogue ever did, since the catalogue never asked. When the doorstep god goes up last, he steps back with everyone else, one of the crowd, which is the promotion.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_legacy The gods want to go home and the files agree

fail.text: He drops nothing; you do, catching a pot he was never going to fumble, and the rim leaves you a souvenir above the eye. The kitchen laughs, he apologizes for existing, and the eldest descendant rules that guilt and crockery must be carried separately. The street, hearing that the museum's own man is shelving gods in an apron, comes to see it, and the lane stays crowded and loud past dark.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_legacy The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: File his doctrine as precedent in court

outcomes.text: You submit the manifests with the under-curator's published doctrine attached: ownership expires in transit, sworn testimony, author available. The museum's counsel objects that the doctrine was never meant to point in that direction, and the magistrate asks counsel to show where a direction is specified. There is no such clause. The doctrine that kept a thousand objects in now holds every door open, and it was theirs.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_legacy The gods want to go home and the files agree

outcomes.text: The clerk rejects the filing on the grounds that doctrines belong to their institutions, and the institution declines to testify against its own bloodstream. The under-curator offers himself as author; the clerk explains that resigned men have opinions, not doctrines. The manifests go home unstamped, and the letters from counsel resume with fresh energy.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_legacy The gods want to go home and the files agree

choices.text: Retail the transit doctrine by the hour

outcomes.text: By the week's end you are a consultant: every family in the city with a relative behind museum glass wants the transit doctrine, and you sell it by the hour with the manifests as advertising. The money is excellent and the queue is long. The under-curator watches you retail his repentance and says nothing, which is somehow louder than saying something. The delegation stops saving you a seat at supper, and the doctrine, which wanted to be a key, spends the season as a toll.
effects: schedule 2 r18_museum_return_legacy The gods want to go home and the files agree

text:
MUSEUM RETURN

Gallery Nine holds one empty case now, glass polished, label pending, and the museum cannot decide what to call it. In the kitchen the gods are home: the pot god over the stove, the small tools in the drawer that remembers them, and at the threshold the little god whose name is only said at home. The eldest descendant says it. Ninety years late counts as on time, in her ruling.

choices.text: Write the empty case's label yourself

outcomes.text: You submit label copy through the registrar, who stamps it in as an incoming loan of text: RETURNED, FOR THE CONTENTS VISIT THE OWNERS, THEY ARE HOME. The museum, unable to locate a policy against accuracy, hangs it. Visitors stand before the empty case longer than they ever stood before the full one, and attendance rises, which alarms the museum into keeping it. In the visitors' book someone writes that the museum has finally acquired something honestly, and no one can prove it was you.

The museum, unable to locate a policy against accuracy, hangs it. Visitors stand before the empty case longer than they ever stood before the full one, and attendance rises, which alarms the museum into keeping it. In the visitors' book someone writes that the museum has finally acquired something honestly, and no one can prove it was you.

Your label survives two committees and dies in the third, replaced by CASE TEMPORARILY UNDER STUDY. The city reads the official version and improves it nightly in chalk on the museum steps, with spelling that deteriorates and accuracy that does not. The museum washes the steps every morning, which everyone agrees is the most honest exhibit it has ever mounted.

choices.text: Charge a coin to see the gods at home

outcomes.text: You hang a modest sign and charge visitors a coin to see the gods where they actually live, guided tour, no rope. Business is embarrassing in its health; the museum never had queues like this. The eldest descendant permits it for exactly one month, on the grounds that the gods have rent to recoup, then confiscates the sign and the argument. You keep the takings. The doorstep god, you would swear, has been counting them.

choices.text: Set the museum a chair at the feast

success.text: The feast pots cook for the whole street, and the eldest descendant sets two chairs for the museum: one for the under-curator, who comes, and one for the institution, which sends a letter that is read aloud to comfortable booing. The registrar arrives in his best coat with a blank loan form, in case anyone feels like history. You get the seat by the stove and a title, keeper of the coming and going, which is less a job than a warning. The under-curator eats three helpings from pots he once catalogued and asks whether the empty case might be given a feast day too. He has finally understood exhibits.

fail.text: The feast overruns the lane and the lane overruns the corner checkpoint, and a patrol wades in asking who authorized this quantity of supper. You take a shove rescuing the smallest pot from a bollard, which the street calls heroism and your elbow calls a fact. The patrol retreats with instructions nobody heard over the singing. The feast runs past its own permit, which never existed, and across town the empty case keeps the only quiet vigil in the city.

text:
OXYGEN PATENT

Tuesday, six in the morning, the meter on your wall charges you four cents for a sigh you had not finished. Universal Air Ventures has moved the atmosphere to a subscription model, with tiers named Crisp, Alpine, and Just Enough. At the hall of the breathers' defense league, the retired midwife is drafting a warranty claim with the patience of a woman who has met ten thousand first breaths: product complaint, air fails to meet advertised specification. She needs a witness whose lungs still have some standing.

choices.text: Countersign the warranty claim as a witness lung

outcomes.text: The claims clerk reads the words air fails to meet advertised specification, and something old wakes up in him. He stamps it RECEIVED while holding his breath, so that nobody can bill him as a beneficiary of the product under review. The claim is now a legal creature with a docket number, and Universal Air Ventures is obliged to feed it answers. Outside, the street breathes a little easier, which your meter records but, for the first time, does not itemize.

outcomes.text: The claim comes back rejected in a gorgeous envelope. The tariff, it explains, defines air as whatever exits the pipe, and defines specification as whatever the air does, so the product has never once failed to be itself. You have been charged a review fee, plus the sigh you sighed while reading, itemized. The midwife files the rejection under Evidence, because a document that confident will eventually confess.

At the league hall, the retired midwife finds your name in her ledger of first breaths and crosses it out in pencil, which is worse than ink.

Your meter observes the donation and, puzzled, files it under expenditures it cannot bill.

outcomes.text: You volunteer as a demonstration lung, breathing the premium product on the record so the defect can be witnessed. Somewhere around the fourth lungful of Alpine you taste pennies, then nickels, then a coughing fit that folds you over the good table. The midwife logs the cough as Exhibit A, pats your back with professional precision, and enrolls you before you can get enough air back to decline. The street hears you coughed on the record and takes it personally, on your behalf, at volume.

The league has voted to read the atmosphere's terms of service aloud, in public, from clause one to whatever comes last. The document runs four hundred pages and consumes air at roughly a page per lungful, the meter in the corner billing every syllable as usage. By clause ninety the room's Crisp is down to Just Enough, and the retired midwife is calling for relief readers in the voice she once saved for difficult deliveries.

choices.text: Sell UAV the roster of relief readers

outcomes.text: The vice president of atmosphere pays eight for the roster and six for your discretion, which is generous, considering you have none. Within a week every relief reader receives a personalized invoice for public performance of a copyrighted document, priced per syllable. The read-aloud collapses, and the quieter members of the league begin talking in a way that involves fewer documents and more crowbars. Your meter prints your receipt without comment, which from a meter is a comment.

outcomes.text: You read clauses two hundred through two sixty in relay, breathing in rota, one lung resting while the next one works. The laughter is worth it, and the kitty covers it. Behind you the meter has fallen three sighs behind the reading, hesitating, like a clerk who has started to read what he types.

success.text: You total the reading's consumption, four hundred pages at a lungful a page, and invoice Universal Air Ventures for air consumed by their own document. Their tariff, clause forty, says consumption is chargeable to the party that causes it, and nothing in recorded history has caused more breathing than their terms of service. Legal spends eleven days looking for an exit from their own clause and pays under protest. The meter prints the credit with what witnesses later describe as a flourish.

text:
OXYGEN PATENT

Overnight, every meter on the street receives a firmware update called Loyalty Pricing. Sighs are up forty percent, gasps count double, and between two and four in the morning the air is described as buffering. Your meter has begun printing tiny apologies at the bottom of each receipt, in a font it clearly hopes management cannot read.

choices.text: Open every window on the street at the same hour

outcomes.text: At the agreed hour, every window on the street goes up with a sound like the neighborhood clearing its throat. Free sky pours through forty kitchens at once, and the meters can only watch, because windows predate the patent and grandfathered openings bill at zero. The street institutes a draft rota, ten windows per shift, the widow at number nine directing crosswinds like weather that owes her money. Your meter records an evening of zero consumption, prints a question mark, and stands it in the corner of the receipt like a small flag.

You put your shoulder to the sash, the sash puts itself onto your fingers, and the street learns several words that cost extra to say while sighing. The draft happens anyway, glorious, cross ventilated, unbillable, and the neighbors cheer through it. You get a free evening of sky and a hand that will be describing that window to strangers for a week.

choices.text: Seek an injunction against Loyalty Pricing

Counsel explains it flows from the customer to the product, as is traditional. The injunction lands the same afternoon: prices frozen, gasps restored to single billing, the firmware wardens recalled to the depot to await instructions that never come. Your meter reverts with an audible relief it will deny in later testimony.

The league disavows your methods at a volume that suggests they are keeping the finding.

Word of the warranty claim travels. The choir arrives first, fined ruinously for a held note in a requiem, followed by the baker, whose yeast has been classified as forty thousand unregistered subscribers. The retired midwife adds each grievance to the claim in her delivery room handwriting, and your meter, unprompted, waives one sigh, then looks away.

choices.text: Merge every grievance into one enormous claim

outcomes.text: You staple the choir to the baker to the pigeon keeper to nine hundred smaller griefs: one claim, one docket, one spine of green thread. The finished object outweighs the terms of service by two pounds, and documents, whatever they pretend, respect weight. UAV requests three continuances in a row, which is the sound a company makes while backing up slowly. The wardens on the street are quietly reassigned to depot inventory, where the shelves do not sigh and there is nothing to fine.

text:
OXYGEN PATENT

Universal Air Ventures announces a full product recall of the atmosphere. The product, they say, has a defect, namely that people keep using it without upgrading, and all units must be returned to the depot in original packaging. The notice does not say what the original packaging of air is, but the small print suggests lungs. Compliance wardens go door to door with collection bags, and your meter prints RECALL in letters that get smaller each time, as if hoping to disappear inside the word.

choices.text: Take a paid shift at the recall depot

outcomes.text: The depot pays two cents a unit to certify returned atmosphere, and the returned atmosphere is empty jars, which you certify with a straight face for eight hours. Fourteen coins, plus a paper hat that says RECALL TEAM. Your neighbors pass the window in a queue, holding jars of used breath and their opinions, both plainly visible. At shift's end the meter prints your wages, itemized, and beneath them, unprompted, a small apology on your behalf.

choices.text: Return the street's air one story at a time

success.text: The street complies with the recall completely, one exhale at a time. Neighbors queue to breathe their air back into the collection bags, and between breaths they tell stories, long ones, with genealogies. By the third hour the wardens understand they are hostages of procedure, their own manual forbidding them to refuse a return in progress. At dusk the recall is suspended on the grounds that the product is coming back too slowly to matter, and the street cheers, which technically returns a little more.

text:
OXYGEN PATENT

At the hearing, the System appoints a standards inspector with a certified nose and a long career of not smiling. Two valves are wheeled in for a blind test, one piped Alpine at premium rates, one drawn from the stairwell outside, free where it stands. The inspector inhales each, twice, makes a note, inhales again, and stops. His nose, certified since before the patent existed, cannot tell them apart, and he would like everyone to remain calm while he says so for the record.

choices.text: Fill the gallery with neighbors who breathe both

outcomes.text: Forty neighbors sample both valves under oath and produce forty descriptions of the premium product, of which thirty nine are the word air, and one is air, but with fewer opinions. The stairwell valve receives, on average, warmer reviews. The inspector notes that the products are distinguishable only by invoice, and the phrase enters the record like a knife going into a drawer. In the corner your meter, present as an exhibit, waives a sigh nobody sighed, practicing.

He then inhales actual Alpine and asks whether the valve is broken. The inspector writes one sentence with no adjectives in it, and UAV's counsel requests a recess in the voice of a slow puncture.

Counsel for the league calls its final witness, your meter, carried to the stand on a cushion. Asked to state its occupation, it prints, quietly, THE COUNTING OF BREATH. Asked whether Alpine ever differed from stairwell air, it begins to hyperventilate, tape spooling in loops over the rail, and what comes out is a confession: the premium pipe has been open to the sky at both ends since installation, and the oxygen works contains one fan, facing a forest.

The gallery goes quiet in the way rooms go quiet when a machine says the true thing.
effects: schedule 3 r18_oxygen_patent_legacy The air is under warranty, and it is failing

outcomes.text: Court adjourns and a warden makes a last try for the witness, on the steps, in daylight, with the whole street watching. The tug of war is brief and impolite, and you keep the meter at the cost of a thumbnail and your good coat's left elbow. The street forms an escort home, twelve neighbors deep, the meter riding in a bread basket like a small mayor. The warden files an incident report that nobody, including the incident, will ever read.
effects: schedule 3 r18_oxygen_patent_legacy The air is under warranty, and it is failing

text:
OXYGEN PATENT

The verdict runs to one sentence: air remains too common to own, costs to the party who tried. Universal Air Ventures is ordered to refund every billed breath, payable in coin or in silence, and the vice president of atmosphere is last seen inhaling economically. The meter lives on the league's mantel now, retired with honors, and the retired midwife has named it Gustav, the first thing she has named in years that did not cry.

choices.text: Set up a refund advisory table, cash only

outcomes.text: You set up a table beside the refund queue, two cents on every claim to do the arithmetic, and the arithmetic is real: interest, overage, the involuntary rate, most people underclaim by half. Fourteen coins by Friday, and every customer walks away richer than they arrived, which is a strange thing to feel sideways about. The midwife pencils your name back into the ledger of first breaths, then adds an asterisk. You do not ask what the asterisk means, and she does not offer, and the asterisk stays.

choices.text: Take a caretaker shift at the oxygen works

outcomes.text: Your first caretaker shift at the oxygen works is quiet: one fan, one forest, one logbook with a pencil on a string. You oil the fan out of respect, thank the forest as per the new custom, and mark the day's air as received in full, no charge. The baker sends bread up with the shift change, the yeast being sentimental about the place. Back home on the mantel, Gustav counts your breaths out of habit and waives them all, one by one, like a man dealing out a deck he finally likes.

Your shift begins with a pigeon in the fan housing and ends with you in the blackberries below the ladder. The pigeon is fine. You are structurally fine, decoratively scratched, and the logbook now contains the entry incident, resolved, bird ungrateful. The street hears the works nearly ate its first caretaker and turns up in numbers to fit a guard screen, loudly, with opinions about ladders.

choices.text: Teach the meter to print refunds instead of bills

success.text: Reversing a meter turns out to be one screw and an apology. Gustav now prints refunds instead of bills, each stamped with a small complimentary sigh, no charge, while the queue puzzles over the novelty of enjoying a receipt. The System certifies him as the district's first instrument of disbursement. He has never been busier, and the column he once hid, sighs waived, unbilled, is now simply called the ledger.

fail.text: Gustav takes to refunding with the zeal of the converted and begins reimbursing people for breaths they never took, on the theory that they might have, had air been kinder. The System flags the generosity as fraud and sends two auditors with matching briefcases. You hide Gustav in the bread oven, unlit, where he is warm and among friends, printing quietly. The auditors stay a week, and the paperwork outlasts everyone's patience, surviving on the kitty's tea budget.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

Six in the morning, and the depot bread queue has been alphabetized. The Office of Objective Need's Allocator feeds surnames before bodies, so the widow on queue seven, born unluckily into the letter W, has not eaten since Thursday. The machine will release her loaf once she submits proof of continued existence, which it does not accept as evidence when she says it standing right there. Two tired debuggers in inked aprons wave you over: they have a ticket queue, a kettle, and a theory.

choices.text: Scalp certified existence to the highest bidder

outcomes.text: You discover the Allocator accepts any slip stamped twice, so you stamp them twice and charge by the letter of the alphabet. The W's pay a premium, which you describe as a handling fee, and the Office quietly buys a batch to hit its throughput target, filing the precedent under flexible verification for later use on people it dislikes. Everyone eats except the people who could not afford to exist. The machine prints your receipt without comment, which somehow feels like a comment.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ration_algorithm_assembly The Allocator still wants proof you exist

choices.text: Join the debuggers and take the widow's ticket

success.text: You log defect one four one: MACHINE CANNOT SEE PEOPLE, severity high, reported by everyone. The senior debugger, who names error codes after her exes, adds you to the rota and hands you the widow's file, six inches of a woman being alive. By noon you have collected proof of continued existence for half the queue, using the radical method of looking at them. The Allocator accepts the paperwork and dispenses, and the widow eats where she stood, which is where the evidence had been all morning.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ration_algorithm_assembly The Allocator still wants proof you exist

fail.text: You reach into the dispenser chute for the widow's held loaf, and the Allocator, following procedure, closes on your wrist while requesting your proof of continued existence. The queue riots on your behalf, which is touching and unhelpful. The debuggers pry you loose, log your knuckles as supporting documentation, and add you to the rota, since you clearly have the passion.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ration_algorithm_assembly The Allocator still wants proof you exist

choices.text: File a formal defect against the feeding order

outcomes.text: You draft it in the Office's own dialect: the algorithm violates specification section nine, ALL SOULS SERVED, by defining soul alphabetically. A clerk reads it twice, goes pale in an official capacity, and stamps it RECEIVED AND REGRETTED. The queue moves for the first time since Thursday, and the debuggers start citing your ticket number like scripture.

outcomes.text: Your defect is rejected as a duplicate of a ticket filed nine years ago, which was itself closed as WORKING AS INTENDED. Objecting to that phrase earns you a citation for tone. The queue takes note that the proper channels are a loop, and its patience begins expiring alphabetically.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

Underneath the depot, the debuggers hold ticket triage beside a boiler that also has a defect number. Tonight's queue: forty starvation reports, one misfiled birth, and the Allocator itself, which has begun printing REQUEST CLARIFY: WHAT IS A BODY in the margins of its ration slips. The senior debugger taps the margin and says the machine is not being difficult, it is being honest, which is worse. Somebody has to reproduce the bug before the Office reproduces the excuse.

choices.text: Run triage till every hunger ticket has a name

success.text: You chair the triage like a field hospital: bodies first, letters never. Each ticket gets a person attached, a neighbor who vouches, a debugger who owns it until it eats. The boiler hisses approvingly, and even the Allocator's margins change, from WHAT IS A BODY to QUERY RECEIVED, THANK YOU. It is the first thank you the Office has produced in living memory, and it came from the plumbing.
effects: schedule 3 r18_ration_algorithm_retaliation The Allocator still wants proof you exist

fail.text: Triage runs nineteen hours before you fall asleep face down on ticket two twelve and wake up stapled to it. Upstairs, someone hears the boiler room is full of unauthorized compassion and files a noise complaint. You are now on two rotas and one watchlist, and your cheek says TWO TWELVE backwards, which the debuggers agree is a promotion.
effects: schedule 3 r18_ration_algorithm_retaliation The Allocator still wants proof you exist

choices.text: Feed the machine a surname it cannot digest

outcomes.text: You register a starving man under a surname that is the letter A written seven times, and the Allocator vaults him to the front, feeds him instantly, and logs a triumph. Then you lay both slips before the depot inspector: same stomach, two fates, one spelling. The inspector stares at the evidence, then at the machine, then formally concedes that hunger is not alphabetical, a sentence the debuggers copy onto the boiler-room wall in chalk.

You have made the Allocator paranoid and the queue longer. The Office thanks you for the hardening opportunity in a memo you cannot prove is sarcastic.

choices.text: Auction the front of the alphabet

outcomes.text: You quietly sell surname changes, one stamped deed poll at a time, and by Friday the queue is full of freshly minted Abbots and Aarons. The Office pays you as well, for consulting on demand smoothing. The Allocator processes the sudden genealogy without blinking, though it prints CLARIFY: SAME BODIES? at the bottom of the day sheet. Nobody clarifies, and the W's stand where they always stood, only now behind people who used to stand beside them.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

Retaliation arrives on official letterhead: every debugger is now required to prove continued existence weekly, in person, during the hours the depot is closed. The senior debugger has been declared PRESUMED DISCONTINUED for missing an appointment she was never told about, and the Allocator has stopped honoring her badge, logging the revocation eleven seconds late, which for a machine is a protest. The Office of Objective Need calls this an integrity review, and would like your paperwork by dawn.

choices.text: Subpoena the spec that defines existing

success.text: You demand the Office produce, in writing, its operational definition of a person, and the ensuing silence has a paper value. Three departments each insist the definition lives elsewhere, and by the fourth referral you have them confessing in triplicate that nobody ever wrote one. A magistrate suspends the weekly appointments pending the invention of existence, and the senior debugger's badge works again, eleven seconds early this time.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ration_algorithm_coalition The Allocator still wants proof you exist

fail.text: The Office produces the spec instantly, which is the trap: four hundred pages defining a person as any entity in good standing with the Office. Your subpoena is granted, satisfied, and framed against you inside an hour. Standing in the corridor, you understand you have asked a circle to testify against roundness.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ration_algorithm_coalition The Allocator still wants proof you exist

choices.text: Dig through the audit logs after hours

success.text: You spend a night inside the audit ledgers and find the Office bills the treasury per existence check, which explains the enthusiasm. You leak the arithmetic to a rationing gazette for a modest finder's fee, and by noon the guards at the depot have been reassigned to counting, a duty they cannot hurt anyone with. The debuggers are annoyed you freelanced, but the queue is shorter and your pocket declines to apologize.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ration_algorithm_coalition The Allocator still wants proof you exist

fail.text: You find the billing scheme and sell it back to the Office as a vulnerability report, which is the polite term for what you did. The debuggers hear about the sum before you can explain, and there is no explaining a sum.

choices.text: Escort the presumed discontinued to their appointments

Even the Allocator gets into it, stamping the senior debugger's slip CONTINUED, WITH ENTHUSIASM, an output nobody programmed and nobody reports.

The queue starts calling the appointments the existence derby, which the Office hates enough to shorten them.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

The depot kitchens send a delegation downstairs: three cooks, one clerk from inside the Office, and a decommissioned operator who once oiled the Allocator and still calls it sweetheart. The clerk has smuggled out a terrible fact: the machine was never given the kitchens' inventory, so it rations a scarcity that is not scarce, guarding bread that outnumbers the queue two to one. The old operator swears the Allocator would feed everyone if anyone told it that it could. All it has ever asked for is proof.

choices.text: Broker the surplus to private buyers first

outcomes.text: You introduce the surplus to some merchants before anyone introduces it to the queue. The bread leaves by the loading dock at midnight, listed on the manifest as archival material, and both sides pay you for discretion. The Allocator counts the outgoing loaves and prints CLARIFY: NEED? at the dock, which everyone present agrees is not addressed to them. The queue never learns what it stood two feet from, which was the service you actually sold.

choices.text: Bankroll a midnight inventory of the kitchens

outcomes.text: You buy ledger paper and ink out of your own pocket and sit up all night with the cooks, counting the kitchens loaf by loaf into a format the machine can swallow. The old operator feeds it the inventory at dawn, whispering encouragement into the intake slot. The Allocator reads the total twice, prints RECALCULATING NEED, and for one whole shift feeds the queue without asking a single soul to prove anything. The cooks weep, the clerk pretends allergies, and the machine hums like a thing forgiven.

The Allocator ingests its own bureaucracy without chewing and updates the rations by close of business. The clerk files the real memo in the real archive, where it becomes true by seniority, and the cooks start calling you the forger laureate.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

At noon the Office declares a General Revalidation: every soul in the district is PRESUMED DISCONTINUED until re-proven, effective before lunch. Black-sashed verifiers set up checkpoints at the depot doors while the Allocator, ordered to purge its rolls, works down the list until it reaches its own registration. It prints ALLOCATOR: PROOF OF CONTINUED EXISTENCE NOT FOUND, and stops, dispensers open, lights on, officially not there. The queue watches the machine refuse to exist at the exact moment existence became a checkpoint.

choices.text: Vouch for every stranger in the line

outcomes.text: You build a chain of vouching: each person attests to the next, the cooks attest to the queue, the old operator attests to the Allocator, and the Allocator, being officially absent, stamps everybody anyway, since a machine that is not there cannot be countermanded. The verifiers face four hundred documented existences and a ghost with a rubber stamp. They withdraw to seek guidance, which is verifier for lose.

The registrar reads ALLOCATOR: PROOF NOT FOUND three times, develops the expression of a man looking into the paperwork abyss, and voids the Revalidation to protect the concept of paperwork. The queue re-exists in a body, retroactive to breakfast.

You are cited for practicing philosophy without a license. Outside, the checkpoint line and the bread line have merged into one line, and nobody in it can say which end is which.

choices.text: Sell fast-track revalidations at the checkpoint

outcomes.text: You know a verifier from a previous queue, and for a consideration he stamps whoever you send to his window, no questions, no eye contact. You charge the desperate a convenience rate, rebate the verifier his cut, and by dusk you are running a thriving concession in the middle of a disappearance. The black sashes conclude the checkpoint model works beautifully with the right local partners. Behind you the Allocator's error line blinks on unread, the only thing at the depot refusing to profit.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

A settlement arrives in a leather folder: the Office will end the checkpoints, feed the queue, and restore the rolls, provided the Allocator is scrapped at a public ceremony as the sole author of the hunger. The old operator reads the terms and sits down hard on an empty crate. Inside the depot the condemned machine keeps working the line, stamping slips CONTINUED while its own decommission order waits in the intake tray, which it keeps declining to ingest, citing a full queue. The Office calls this closure, and has already engraved the plaque.

choices.text: Demand the scrapping follow its own procedure

outcomes.text: You accept the ceremony on the condition it obeys the Office's decommission manual, which you have actually read. Section two requires a fault report signed by the machine's author, and no author will sign, because signing means existing in the vicinity of blame. The scrapping postpones itself indefinitely on procedural grounds, the checkpoints end anyway per the settlement, and the plaque is quietly re-engraved TO THE UNKNOWN PROCESS.

choices.text: Wheel the Allocator out before the ceremony

success.text: At four in the morning you and the cooks unbolt the Allocator, load it onto a bread cart, and walk it out the loading dock past a night verifier who is paid to see nothing and performs superbly. The machine spends the ceremony in a cellar, working through its backlog by candlelight, stamping slips for anyone brave enough to visit a fugitive appliance. The debuggers, who were not consulted, are furious and then delighted, in that order and in writing. The Office demolishes an empty plinth with full honors, and a man in a black sash starts asking who exactly moved four hundred pounds of state property.

The machine, back on its plinth, stamps your incident report CONTINUED, which under the circumstances reads as concern.

No official will swing a hammer against that much testimony with the gazettes present. The scrapping is downgraded to a review, the plaque is misplaced by a clerk who winks at you, and the Allocator stamps the crowd out at the door like a shift ending.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

The old operator finally cracks the Allocator's sealed panel, and behind it, on wax paper, is the original source: the feeding schedule, annotated in a tidy, miserable hand. The algorithm is not broken and never was, it does exactly what the margins say it must: SORT BY SURNAME PER UNDER-DIRECTOR PELL, HE LIKES THE LEDGERS TIDY FOR TOURS, I AM SORRY, WHOEVER FINDS THIS. Line after line, the anonymous author begged in the comments and complied in the code. The machine was not cruel, it was specified cruelly, and the specification has a name.

choices.text: Sell the annotated source to a scandal sheet

outcomes.text: The gazettes bid against each other for the wax paper, and a broadsheet with more circulation than scruples wins by a landslide of coin. PELL'S MARGINS runs for six editions while you decline to be named in any of them. The Office responds by buying the gazette outright, which costs more than feeding the district ever would have. The Allocator is left out of every headline, which it would probably prefer, though its author's apologies now belong to a man who quotes them at parties.

success.text: You gather the rota and read every margin, including the apologies, especially the apologies. The senior debugger declares the anonymous author an honorary debugger, retroactive, with back pay of one kettle of tea held in trust. Then the room rewrites the schedule the way the comments always wanted: bodies first, surnames never, and the Allocator runs the new build like a held breath finally let out.

fail.text: Midway through the reading, verifiers raid the boiler room for the wax paper, and you keep it by the traditional method of being on top of it. You lose a tooth and keep a comma. The debuggers finish the reading louder, from memory, while you bleed on the minutes, which are kept.

choices.text: Enter the margins into the public record

outcomes.text: You file the annotated source as evidence in open session, and the clerk of the court reads Pell's name aloud four times, as procedure requires, each repetition somehow louder. The Under-Director explains that tidy ledgers benefit everyone, and the gallery, which has stood in his ledgers, disagrees as one body. The feeding order is struck down on the strength of the machine's own margins, the first acquittal in Office history won by the defendant's handwriting.

outcomes.text: The Office concedes everything about the algorithm and surrenders nothing about Pell, who is promoted sideways into an advisory fog where subpoenas cannot land. The margins are sealed as a personnel matter. You keep a copy, naturally, and copies of copies, and the line I AM SORRY, WHOEVER FINDS THIS begins appearing chalked on walls the Office must now pay someone to wash.

text:
RATION ALGORITHM

Spring finds the Allocator wearing a knitted cozy the queue made for its intake slot. The rewritten schedule feeds bodies before surnames, the W's ate first for one ceremonial week on principle, and the Office of Objective Need has been renamed twice, which is how it apologizes. Only one procedure survives from the old build: the machine still issues a single proof of continued existence daily, unprompted, stamped and dated. It issues it to itself, and the old operator swears it looks proud.

choices.text: Train new debuggers on the widow's old ticket

success.text: You teach the incoming rota from ticket one four one, now framed: MACHINE CANNOT SEE PEOPLE, closed, resolution attached. The widow herself guest-lectures on being evidence, and attendance is standing room only, which she notes is the correct kind. Graduates sign the boiler-room wall beneath the chalked error line, and the Allocator stamps each certificate CONTINUED, WITH ENTHUSIASM, an unprogrammed output the debuggers have voted never to fix.

fail.text: Training day collides with the renamed Office's ribbon ceremony, and you throw your back out hauling the demonstration ledgers past a procession of new plaques. The recruits take notes on your posture as a cautionary diagram. The class runs anyway, from the floor, and the Allocator lowers its intake tray to your level, which the old operator logs as bedside manner.

choices.text: Publish the margins as the official manual

outcomes.text: You typeset the anonymous author's margins, apologies and all, as OPERATIONS MANUAL, SECOND EDITION, and the renamed Office adopts it rather than admit it never read the first. Under-directors are now procedurally bound to comply with I AM SORRY, WHOEVER FINDS THIS. The appendix is the widow's old ration slip reproduced at actual size, captioned simply EVIDENCE, and clerks are reported to consult it.

The manual sells out its first printing to collectors who frame the apologies as decor, which is not strictly the point. The renamed Office objects that regret is proprietary and files for ownership of the margins. The dispute will run for years, but the machine has already ingested the manual and marked it VERIFIED, and nobody has ever successfully appealed the Allocator.

choices.text: License the mercy patch to other districts

outcomes.text: Other districts want the bodies-first schedule, and you arrive ahead of the paperwork, selling implementation at consulting rates, exclusivity included. The patch that was written free in a boiler room now retails, and one district you priced out keeps its surnames-first build, alphabetizing quietly. The Allocator learns its kindness is a product line and prints CLARIFY: SORRY? which you file, and bill for filing. You are rich in the way the Office used to be, which is a sentence you try not to finish.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Tuesday evening, the municipal notice board: a permit for a torchlit procession, granted for the consultation period, pinned neatly beside the consultation itself, which has not begun. The Committee for Studying Immediate Action meets Thursday, as it has met for eleven years, to consider item one: a motion to define the word motion, tabled fourteen times. The seamstress reads both notices twice, then opens her annotated standing orders, which she calls Horace, to the chapter on quorum. We are not going to break up the meeting, she says. We are going to attend it.

choices.text: Fill the public gallery with neighbors and pastry

outcomes.text: The gallery has nineteen seats and you register thirty one attendees, citing the standing order that the public may be present, which specifies no number and, on inspection, no maximum. The clerk signs everyone in alphabetically, twice, to be sure. You cover the registration stamps and the pastry yourself, and the night porter, unasked, brings folding chairs up from the cellar and sets them out to a plan of his own devising. Thursday now has a public.
effects: schedule 3 r18_postponement_committee_assembly Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

outcomes.text: Registration opens at noon and the queue develops opinions about itself by five past. You take a folding chair to the shin in the scrum, and the porter takes the scrum personally. Everyone is signed in eventually, bruised, alphabetized, and loud in a way that carries to the street. The Committee notes, with satisfaction, an agitated public: proof that further study is needed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_postponement_committee_assembly Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Request the permit file through the proper window

outcomes.text: The window for Records is open Thursdays, ten until ten fifteen, and you are there at nine with your request in duplicate. The clerk behind the glass reads it, blinks at the words granted during consultation, and stamps it RECEIVED with something close to relief. By afternoon the file is public, and the town learns its procession was approved for a period that does not exist yet. Even the Committee's friends agree the paperwork deserves a look.

Refiling takes a week you do not have, and the permit stays in its drawer, gathering authority. At Thursday's meeting the Chair cites your rejected form as evidence that the public is not yet ready for documents.

choices.text: Scalp your place in the sign-in queue

outcomes.text: Gallery seats turn out to have a market, and you are standing at the front of it. A merchant pays eight for your spot, and a man in unseasonal boots pays six more for the queue position of a friend you invent on the spot. The boots trouble you slightly, later, in a way that a sum of fourteen does not.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Thursday, seven sharp, the council chamber smells of lamp oil and eleven years of tabled business. The Chair of Studying Immediate Action wears the Sash of Deliberation and opens the meeting with a pearwood gavel handed down, she reminds the room, from chairs who also achieved nothing rashly. The gallery is full for the first time since the flood. Item one waits on the agenda like a dog that has learned not to hope: a motion to define the word motion, tabled fourteen times.

choices.text: Move to take item one from the table

success.text: The seamstress rises on a point of privilege and moves that item one be taken from the table, seconded before the Chair's gavel finds the wood. The Chair hesitates, torn, because studying this motion is the most immediate action she has been offered in a decade, and her devotion wins: the motion to define the word motion is before the body at last. The minutes clerk writes taken from the table and pauses, admiring the sentence. Fourteen tablings, one un-tabling: the arithmetic of the evening is yours.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_retaliation Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

fail.text: You rise, mispronounce privilege as a plural, and the Vice Chair for Further Study rules you out of order with the tenderness of a man drowning a kitten he respects. The motion stays tabled a fifteenth time. But the gallery has now watched procedure kill a thing in the open, and Horace acquires three new annotations in a hand that presses hard.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_retaliation Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Sell agenda copies at the chamber door

outcomes.text: The agenda is public and free, which is different, you reason, from convenient. You sell fair copies at the door for eight all told, and a polite stranger with a drummer's calluses pays six more for your annotated one. The Committee is flattered by the demand. Somewhere tonight, someone knows exactly which Thursdays the chamber sits, and how late.

choices.text: Second whatever the seamstress moves, instantly

outcomes.text: You appoint yourself her second, and the mechanism becomes beautiful: she moves, you second, the Chair must acknowledge, the clerk must record. The gallery learns the rhythm and hums it. By the recess the Committee has acknowledged more in one evening than in the preceding four years, and the tea trolley operator, moved by nobody, brings the good biscuits to the caucus side unprompted.

outcomes.text: You second so fast you second the Vice Chair's motion to adjourn by mistake, and the room takes a moment to enjoy that. In the confusion the gavel comes down on your fingers, ceremonially but firmly. The clerk records the seconder wishes it noted he was stretching, which is the kindest thing the minutes have done for anyone all year.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Monday's agenda arrives with a wound in it: the motion to define the word motion has been referred to the Subcommittee on Definitions, membership two, meeting schedule none. The venue has also changed, from the council chamber to the annex broom room, capacity nine, on grounds of intimacy. Outside town the permit holders have begun rehearsing with drums, and the sound comes over the rooftops at supper, patient as weather. The Chair calls the referral a triumph of thoroughness.

choices.text: Move the chamber benches into the broom room

success.text: You and the porter carry the gallery benches down two corridors and install them in the broom room with a shoehorn's confidence, and where benches are, the porter observes, the public may sit. Capacity nine becomes capacity forty if nobody inhales. The subcommittee of two arrives to find its intimate venue upholstered like a parliament, with soup. The caucus elders mutter that furniture is not procedure, but they mutter it from very good seats.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_coalition Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

fail.text: The second bench takes the staircase corner badly, and you take the bench worse, three points of you at once. The porter is unhurt but radicalized on the subject of banisters, and the Committee, hearing the crash, votes itself a sergeant at arms as a precaution. You limp home leaving the furniture wedged in what is, technically, a load-bearing position.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_coalition Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Cater the subcommittee until it reports

outcomes.text: The Subcommittee on Definitions has no schedule, so the caucus gives it one: soup at noon, bread at one, a standing invitation renewed daily until a report exists. Definitions, it turns out, ripen quickly in a fed room. By Friday the subcommittee reports that the word motion merits defining, which is the first conclusion the Committee has produced in living memory, and the tureen is empty and famous.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_coalition Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

outcomes.text: The soup run works until the tureen and the annex stairs disagree, and you break the fall with your dignity and one wrist. Lunch arrives late, the subcommittee sulks, and the whole street hears the crockery and assumes the worst. Still, a fed subcommittee is a scheduled subcommittee, and the caucus insists your ladle arm will be minuted as wounded in service.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_coalition Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Move that the subcommittee be everyone present

outcomes.text: The seamstress consults Horace and finds it: any member may move to enlarge a subcommittee, and nothing anywhere caps the enlargement. You move that the Subcommittee on Definitions consist of all persons present, and the Chair, hearing more study proposed by more people, accepts with shining eyes. The broom room is now the whole meeting wearing a smaller hat. The first amendment passes before supper: a motion is an expression of intent to move.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Wednesday, the annex cellar, the porter's seating plan spread on a barrel like a battle map, because that is what it is. Chairs are power: who sits near the gavel, who sits near the door, who sits where the draught discourages long speeches. The drums over the rooftops have added a horn. The seamstress wants a second amendment ready before the Committee reconvenes, and she threads a needle while she says it: intent is demonstrated by movement.

choices.text: Trade favors with porters, clerks, and the trolley

success.text: You mend the porter's ceremonial coat, top up the trolley operator's kitty, and walk the minutes clerk home past the noisy end of town, asking for nothing. Nothing turns out to be the correct price: by Friday the seating plan puts the caucus at every draught-free point of order distance from the gavel, and the trolley reaches your side of the room first. The clerk says only that accurate minutes require hearing both sides, and sits one seat closer.
effects: schedule 2 r18_postponement_committee_crisis Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

fail.text: The favor economy has a crash: you promise three errands in one afternoon and deliver none, and the trolley operator's disappointment lands harder than any gavel, as does the cellar door, which was one of your errands to fix. The porter redraws the seating plan without malice, which is worse. Repairs to your standing, and to your forehead, begin at once.
effects: schedule 2 r18_postponement_committee_crisis Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Draft the amendment in the Chair's own prose style

outcomes.text: The seamstress studies eleven years of the Chair's rulings and stitches the amendment from their cloth: whereas thoroughness demands specificity, intent to move is demonstrated by movement. The Chair reads it twice at the reconvening, visibly stirred by the familiar cadence, and pronounces it the most rigorous sentence the Committee has yet considered. She cannot rule her own style out of order. The second amendment is on the floor.
effects: schedule 2 r18_postponement_committee_crisis Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

She rules it frivolous, and the Vice Chair moves to commend her vigilance, carried, minuted, framed by lunchtime. The amendment survives on the order paper, barely, breathing through one clause.

choices.text: Coach the permit holders' clerk in procedure, for a fee

outcomes.text: The permit holders have acquired a clerk of their own, a nervous man who cannot tell a point of order from a point of information, and he pays eight for an evening of coaching and six more for your old notes. He is a terrible student, which you tell yourself is the point. Over the rooftops the drums practice on, better funded now, and your purse is fourteen heavier and slightly louder than usual.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

The procession is set for Saturday, and Thursday's chamber knows it: the drums have moved two streets closer and acquired a schedule of their own. Halfway through the second amendment's reading, the Vice Chair rises, straightens his sash, and moves to adjourn sine die, that is, forever, in the tone of a man announcing dessert. Members of the Committee's bench are already reaching for their coats. The Chair's gavel hovers.

choices.text: Object that a motion under way cannot be laid down

outcomes.text: The seamstress is up before the coats are: point of order, the body cannot adjourn while a motion is on the floor, and by its own first amendment this motion is an expression of intent to move, and is therefore, demonstrably, under way. The Chair consults Horace, which the seamstress lends her with terrible courtesy. The gavel comes down on the side of the text: adjournment fails, the reading resumes, and the coats sit back down one sleeve at a time. The clerk writes the motion could not be stopped, and underlines nothing, trusting the sentence.
effects: schedule 3 r18_postponement_committee_compromise Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

outcomes.text: Your objection cites the amendment before it has technically passed its reading, and the Vice Chair impales the error at his leisure. Adjournment nearly carries, and fails only because the Chair, in her heart, does not want the studying to end. You are saved by her devotion, and everyone in the room knows it, including her.

choices.text: Auction your deciding voice to both benches quietly

outcomes.text: The Committee's whip offers eight for your abstention, and a sympathizer on the caucus side, misreading the room heroically, offers six for your aye, and you accept both on the reasoning that money has no quorum. The vote is postponed before your voice matters, so you keep the fourteen and, in a sense, your word. The drums outside sound briefly like applause. They are not applause.

choices.text: Keep quorum in the room by any polite means

success.text: The Committee's fallback is simpler than argument: leave, and let the meeting die of arithmetic. The caucus has prepared with hospitality: the trolley blocks nothing but happens to be everywhere, the porter has waxed the corridor into an adventure, and departing members find themselves in conversations about their grandchildren of surprising specificity. Quorum holds like a dam built of small courtesies. The reading finishes at midnight, to the sound of one drum, far off, losing confidence.

Outside, the drums rehearse something that is nearly a march, and the whole street hears the chamber shouting about arithmetic.
effects: schedule 3 r18_postponement_committee_compromise Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Friday morning a runner brings the caucus an envelope sealed with the Committee's full ceremonial wax, both ribbons. Inside is an offer: withdraw the motion to define the word motion, and the Committee will expedite review of Saturday's permit, expedite to be defined, the letter notes, at a later date. The seamstress reads it aloud twice, once for the words, once for the silences between them. The drums are near enough now to rattle the annex windows in their frames.

choices.text: Inspect the permit file before answering anything

success.text: You go to Records alone and read the file cover to cover, and there it sits in the granting clause: permission is given for the duration of the consultation period, no dates, no map, only those words. Whoever decides when and where consultation occurs decides the procession entirely, and the Committee wrote that sentence itself. The Records clerk, closing early, pays out an old witness fee you are owed, four, and pretends not to watch you copy the clause. The caucus is cross that you went alone, right up until you read it to them.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_reversal Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

fail.text: Records receives you with tea and a consultation honorarium of eight, which you accept because refusing tea is rude and refusing money is unnatural. Only afterward do you see the honorarium ledger's heading: advisors consulted, favorable. Your name now decorates the Committee's evidence of public support, and the caucus reads the ledger before you can explain about the tea.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_reversal Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Refuse by letter, signed by every hand you have

outcomes.text: The reply is one sentence, the motion is before the body, followed by signatures: the caucus, the porter, the trolley operator, both soup cooks, and, in small firm script at the bottom, the minutes clerk, who has decided the record should show where the record stands. The Committee reads that last name three times. Wax, this time, does not help them.

outcomes.text: Signature gathering goes beautifully until the annex stairs go out from under the letter and you go with it, elbow first. The letter survives creased; you survive annotated. It is delivered late but delivered, and the Committee's runner, seeing your sling, holds the door, which the Committee would like minuted as its contribution.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_reversal Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Amend the definition to its final, walking form

success.text: The seamstress moves the third amendment and the chamber holds its breath: a motion is the body moving, and the body may move as one body. The Chair examines it from every side, finds it thorough, specific, and grammatically armored, and passes it to final reading with the pride of a woman who believes she has finally taught the town to study. The clerk reads it back and, for the first time in eleven years, smiles at the minutes. The motion now says what it does.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_reversal Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

fail.text: The third amendment reaches the floor with a comma in the wrong place, and the Vice Chair rides that comma like a warhorse for two full hours. Final reading is postponed to the next sitting, which is Saturday morning, hours before the procession steps off. The seamstress repunctuates all night. Horace has never been so thumbed.
effects: schedule 4 r18_postponement_committee_reversal Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Saturday, nine sharp, the chamber packed to the porter's exact plan, and the motion to define the word motion passes its final reading without a syllable to spare: a motion is the body moving, and the body may move as one body. The Chair pronounces the definition rigorous and lifts her gavel to close the item. The seamstress rises with the calm of a finished seam: then, Madam Chair, the body now moves. The procession is due on the high street at noon.

choices.text: Move the whole session into the high street

outcomes.text: The body moves as one body: gavel, minutes, trolley, benches, the porter's seating plan re-pegged on the cobbles by eleven forty, agenda item still open, session unbroken. The procession arrives at noon to find the high street occupied by a duly convened meeting it is forbidden to disturb, since its own permit runs only during consultation, and the consultation is here, seated, taking minutes. The drummers wait for an adjournment that does not come, because drums cannot out-sit an agenda. You pay the porters' overtime from your own pocket, the best three you ever spent, and the new sergeant at arms, finding no disorder to sergeant, goes home.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_legacy Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

outcomes.text: The body moves as one body, slightly downhill, and the trolley develops an intent of its own at speed. You stop it with the leg you like least. The session reconvenes around your bruise, on schedule, and the procession still finds the street lawfully occupied; the minutes record one casualty, refreshments, recovering. The street will retell the runaway trolley for years, mostly warmly, mostly near you.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_legacy Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Offer the marchers the floor, via the speakers' list

outcomes.text: The clerk, at the seamstress's nod, carries the speakers' list to the head of the procession and explains, accurately, that any person may address the session in the order received. The order received stands at forty four items, beginning with the annex drainage question, estimated time to recognition four hours, torches not permitted indoors nor, the porter adds, in his street. A procession is many things, but it is not four hours of drainage patient, and by two o'clock the high street holds only a meeting, a trolley, and a horn somebody abandoned. The sergeant at arms is reassigned to guarding the biscuit tin, a post he fills with distinction.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_legacy Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

outcomes.text: Your copy of the speakers' list misquotes the standing order, and the permit holders' nervous clerk, coached, you dimly recall, by someone, spots it. There is a quarter hour of real danger while the procession's front rank and the session's front bench read the same page over each other's shoulders, drums idling. Then the Chair herself corrects the citation, because a procedural error offends her more than anything else on the street today, and the moment tips; you cannot argue with a woman like that, and they do not. The march files off down the one lane the seating plan left open, which leads, by the porter's design, out of town.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_legacy Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

choices.text: Sell commemorative copies of today's minutes

outcomes.text: History has a gift shop, and you are its founder: fair copies of the minutes at eight, the porter's cobblestone seating plan reproduced at six, demand outrunning the ink. Not every buyer is a friend; a man with drummer's calluses purchases three of everything, studying, presumably, for next time. Fourteen richer, you file that thought under later.
effects: schedule 5 r18_postponement_committee_legacy Item one has waited eleven years. It is done waiting.

text:
POSTPONEMENT COMMITTEE

Sunday the chamber is swept and strange with quiet, and the last item of the extraordinary session is the adjournment itself. The Chair, sash pressed, gavel polished, prepares to adjourn to the next scheduled Thursday, as chairs before her adjourned, rashly never. The seamstress passes a slip of paper to the minutes clerk, who reads it and, verbatim as ever, smiles. The motion to define the word motion lies on the table one last time, complete, spent, and slightly famous.

choices.text: Adjourn to a time and place of the body's choosing

success.text: The amendment to the adjournment is nine words: this body stands adjourned to wherever it next stands. The Chair examines it for flaw and finds only thoroughness, her one weakness, and the gavel falls on a meeting that can now reconvene in a granary, a doorway, or a street, wherever the body happens to be standing when standing is required. The Committee for Studying Immediate Action has, by its own procedure, chartered a town that votes and also shows up. The clerk closes the book gently, the way you close a door on a sleeping child.

fail.text: The Chair finds the flaw: wherever is not a place under chapter twelve, and she adjourns you to next Thursday, chamber, seven sharp, with visible relief. Then she pauses at the door and says, not unkindly, that Thursday is also wherever the body will be standing. Eleven years of studying immediacy, and she arrived eventually, by the long way, which she would tell you is the only rigorous route.

choices.text: License the walking definition to other councils

outcomes.text: Three neighboring councils write within the month, each besieged by a permanent committee of its own, each offering eight for the amendments and six for the marginalia. You sell fair copies with the seams marked. Some purchasers, you notice, sit on the committees in question and intend the definition as a muzzle; what you do not mention is that a text that walks also travels.

choices.text: Retire the motion with a seat and a nameplate

outcomes.text: The motion is framed and hung above the public gallery, and the porter's plan grants it a seat, aisle, draught-free, marked RESERVED, IN MOTION. New members are taken to meet it the way you would be taken to meet a grandmother. The caucus discovers, too late, the price of winning by procedure: last week's potluck required a second, the soup rota has acquired standing orders, and the seamstress has been elected something she is still trying to decline, twice, by the book. The trolley operator moves that the biscuits be defined, and nobody dares rule her out of order.

New members are taken to meet it the way you would be taken to meet a grandmother. The caucus discovers, too late, the price of winning by procedure: last week's potluck required a second, the soup rota has acquired standing orders, and the seamstress has been elected something she is still trying to decline, twice, by the book. The trolley operator moves that the biscuits be defined, and nobody dares rule her out of order.

Hanging the frame, you drop it, and the motion to define the word motion strikes you on the head in a final demonstration of its own text: it moved, and you were the body. The nameplate goes up crooked and stays crooked, and within a week the crookedness is beloved and protected by a resolution nobody remembers passing. You are minuted as the motion's last casualty and its first monument.

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

Dawn on the east road, and the Civic Reassurance Guard has lowered a fresh white barrier across it. The placard reads: BY ORDER, THE TRAVELER SHALL PLACE THE TRAVELER UNDER ARREST BEFORE PROCEEDING. The Sub-Warden of Reassurance stands beside it with the posture of a man guarding a cathedral. At the verge, the widow who counts carts marks her slate and does not say what the count is missing.

choices.text: Request the official procedure for arresting yourself

outcomes.text: The Sub-Warden cannot refuse a procedural inquiry; refusal is not among the approved postures. Within the week the Guard publishes Form CR-1, Self Arrest, Notice of, with one line for the arresting officer and one for the detainee, both yours to sign. The apprentice signwriter copies it onto the notice board in the Guard's own approved lettering, which makes it law twice. The barrier looms over the queue like a gate that has not yet learned what it is for.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_assembly The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: Your inquiry is accepted, stamped, and filed under traffic control, which is where the Guard keeps its weather. Your follow-up inquiry joins it. The Sub-Warden thanks you for your patience in a tone suggesting your patience has been confiscated as evidence. The barrier stays down all afternoon, practicing.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_assembly The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Sell waiting travelers a pre-arrest paperwork kit

outcomes.text: The kits sell out by noon: a pencil, a ribbon, and two copies of nothing official, which is exactly as official as most things here. The Sub-Warden commends your entrepreneurial calm and orders forty for the Guard at full price. You are now a preferred vendor of the checkpoint, which is income, and also a sentence you will have to live with. The widow watches you count your coins the way she counts carts: precisely, and for the record.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_assembly The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Stand with the widow and learn the count

success.text: She teaches you the tally: a stroke for each cart in, a stroke for each cart out, and a small dot for the difference. Nobody at the verge discusses the dots. By dusk you are holding the slate while she rests her hand, and that is the whole initiation; the checkpoint witnesses have no oath, only custody of the count. You give the barrier a courteous nod on your way home, and its counterweight shifts, which is probably the wind.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_assembly The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: The barrier arm drops in observance of its own schedule and takes your hat, plus some of what the hat was for. The Sub-Warden logs the injury as a traffic incident, self inflicted, and asks you to initial it. The widow bandages you with the flat efficiency of long practice and never once puts down the slate. You initial the log; it is, technically, accurate.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_assembly The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

Nine witnesses crowd the weigh house after closing, the scale still ticking under the weight of the day. The apprentice signwriter unrolls a rubbing of the placard and reads the operative sentence twice: THE TRAVELER SHALL PLACE THE TRAVELER UNDER ARREST BEFORE PROCEEDING. Shall, he notes, not may, and nowhere any limit on how often. The widow sets her slate on the scale, and the scale, for once, weighs something everyone can see.

choices.text: Take the Guard's coin to report on the meeting

outcomes.text: The Sub-Warden pays in fresh coin and files your report under civic weather. What you have sold him is a true account of people planning to obey him, which he reads twice without locating the crime in it. He pays again for the second reading; the Guard's rates favor thoroughness. You leave the guardhouse richer and lighter, in the way of men who have sold something that was not entirely theirs.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_retaliation The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Sign the rota of who arrests themselves when

success.text: The widow chalks a rota on the weigh house wall: who arrests themselves on which morning, so the checkpoint never runs dry of criminals. You take the first slot, the cold one, and sign with your whole name and both titles, detainee and officer. The signwriter letters your slot like a saint's day. Somewhere in the dark the barrier waits, unaware it has just been given a congregation.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_retaliation The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: You rehearse the arrest on yourself in the weigh house, seizing your own collar with real civic feeling, and your shoulder objects on procedural grounds. Something pops that should not pop. The witnesses agree you have set an inspiring standard of commitment and must never demonstrate it again. The rota gains a footnote: enthusiasm to be applied internally.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_retaliation The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Read the placard aloud, word by exact word

outcomes.text: You recite the placard at the barrier itself, at the pace of a man being fair to each word. Shall, not may; before proceeding, not once per lifetime; and the Guard must process every arrest it receives, because a Guard that skips processing is only a fence with wages. The Sub-Warden checks the recital against his own copy and finds you have not added a syllable. A rule read exactly is a rule armed, and nobody at the checkpoint sleeps well tonight except the witnesses.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_retaliation The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: You recite the placard so loudly that the Sub-Warden appears with a pot of paste and an amendment, ink still wet. Removal of the placard is now theft of government property, including removal by weather; the rain has been formally warned. Your recital is logged as an unlicensed public reading. The barrier creaks through it all, sounding, for the first time, faintly interested.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_retaliation The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

Two sentries arrive at the widow's stall with a requisition for her slate, stamped in three colors and carried at chest height like a relic. Independent counting, the Sub-Warden has ruled, is unlicensed traffic control. The widow reads the requisition all the way through, twice, while the sentries hold their pose. Behind them the barrier stands at half mast, unsure which side of this it is on.

choices.text: Copy the tally into twenty hands tonight

outcomes.text: By nightfall the count lives in twenty ledgers, a hymnal margin, and the back of a flour bill. When the sentries return, the widow chalks the slate clean and surrenders it unasked, obedient to the letter of the requisition, which demands the slate and never once mentions the numbers. The Sub-Warden receives a blank rectangle in three-color custody. The count, being everywhere, is now nowhere he can requisition.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: By nightfall the count lives in twenty ledgers, a hymnal margin, and the back of a flour bill. When the sentries return, the widow chalks the slate clean and surrenders it unasked, obedient to the letter of the requisition, which demands the slate and never once mentions the numbers. The Sub-Warden receives a blank rectangle in three-color custody. The count, being everywhere, is now nowhere he can requisition.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: You put yourself between the requisition and the widow, and the barrier's counterweight, descending on schedule, finds your knuckles in a designated lowering zone. The count survives intact in twenty hands. Yours takes up counting a little later than the rest. The sentries log the incident as traffic, which by now surprises no one but still costs something to hear.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Challenge the requisition's third stamp as decorative

success.text: You cite the Guard's own color schedule, which lists teal as decorative, and the requisition's third stamp is, on inspection, indisputably teal. A decorative stamp cannot operate; the requisition is ceremony, and ceremony cannot carry a slate. The Sub-Warden withdraws it for restamping with the expression of a man bitten by his own signet. The Guard's stamp registry becomes the most read document in the province.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: The Sub-Warden convenes a color tribunal on the spot and rules that teal is operationally blue. The finding is recorded in teal, which is now blue, which settles it. Your objection is filed as glare. The slate goes to the guardhouse under escort, and the town learns the Guard can amend the rainbow, though only, the signwriter notes, one color at a time.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Study how the barrier decides to drop

success.text: You spend an evening with the mechanism and learn its habit: every raise and lower is scored onto a brass wheel, a count the barrier keeps of itself. The wheel disagrees with the traffic filings by a margin nobody official has noticed. A traveling scrivener pays four coins for a certified rubbing of the wheel, no questions, which is his way of asking all of them. The witnesses are pleased with the rubbing and pointed about the fee.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: A sentry finds you inside the mechanism housing, and the Sub-Warden, rather than arrest you, hires you: the barrier requires an oiler, and initiative requires harnessing. The pay is eight coins, and the barrier now drops half a second faster, which you try not to think of as your work. The witnesses say nothing, which is louder than saying something. Freshly oiled, the barrier sounds happier; you wish it did not.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_coalition The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

Market morning, and the queue at the barrier is the longest in checkpoint history, every soul holding Form CR-1 in duplicate. Farmers arrest themselves. Carters arrest themselves. A grandmother arrests herself twice, once as detainee and once, she explains, as the officer needing the credit. The Sub-Warden processes them in order, because order is what he has instead of options.

choices.text: Announce that prisoners cannot legally be traffic

outcomes.text: You say it at volume, once, with the placard behind you for authority: a prisoner has a docket, a docket has a name, and traffic has neither. The Sub-Warden checks, and it is horribly true; custody is the one filing cabinet that cannot lose people quietly. By noon two hundred travelers have arrested themselves into the safety of the record. The barrier stays up for whole minutes at a time, holding the pose of a gate that is beginning to suspect it is a door.
effects: schedule 3 r18_checkpoint_law_crisis The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: The Sub-Warden hears the argument, agrees with every word, and suspends all dockets pending the design of a new form. The queue becomes two hundred criminals with nowhere to be filed, which even he concedes is untidy. Design is estimated at six weeks; the queue is estimated at growing. You have won the point and misplaced the paperwork it lived in.
effects: schedule 3 r18_checkpoint_law_crisis The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Rent stools and parasols to the arrested queue

outcomes.text: Custody is long and the sun is administrative, so you rent stools at two coins and shade at three. The Guard cannot object: regulations require prisoners be kept from harm, and you are, technically, infrastructure. The Sub-Warden hires two parasols for his sentries at the full civilian rate and files the expense under fortifications. Everyone in the queue is poorer and cooler, and you are the only person at the checkpoint having an uncomplicated day.
effects: schedule 3 r18_checkpoint_law_crisis The barrier would like a word, in triplicate
