choices.text: Ladle soup down the length of the queue

outcomes.text: You lay out soup along the queue at your own coin, one ladle per criminal, seconds for anyone who arrested themselves before breakfast. Feeding prisoners is the Guard's statutory duty, so the Sub-Warden must log your kettle as outsourced compliance and thank you by form letter. The queue eats, the forms move, and the barrier rises and falls in something dangerously close to rhythm. The widow adds a column to her slate for meals served, and this one has no dots in it.
effects: schedule 3 r18_checkpoint_law_crisis The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: The kettle tips in the noon crush and you take the splash meant for no one. The queue surges to help and briefly stops being a queue, which the sentries treat as an emergency of geometry. Order returns; your forearm keeps the memory. The soup that survived is declared excellent, which is a comfort of nearly the right size.
effects: schedule 3 r18_checkpoint_law_crisis The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

By the fifth market day the guardhouse holds nine thousand dockets, and the barrier has stopped rising: the counterweight cannot lift the paperwork stacked along its arm. From the capital comes the Inspector of Calm, whose coach has priority over roads and whose questions have priority over answers. If the checkpoint fails its audit, the Guard will replace it with mobile reassurance: no placard, no forms, nothing anyone can read. The witnesses face the strangest chore of the year, rescuing the trap they jammed.

choices.text: Sell the Inspector a guided tour of the backlog

outcomes.text: The Inspector pays handsomely for candor, and you give him the full tour: the docket mountain, the seized blank slate, the barrier pinned flat under its own administration. He writes magnificent throughput in his book and underlines it. At the gate he presses eight coins into your hand for discretion and six more for the opposite, to be exercised at his signal. You pocket both; the signal, you suspect, will never come, and the coins do not care.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

He writes magnificent throughput in his book and underlines it. At the gate he presses eight coins into your hand for discretion and six more for the opposite, to be exercised at his signal. You pocket both; the signal, you suspect, will never come, and the coins do not care.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Carry dockets for the drowning sentries

outcomes.text: The witnesses report for docket duty as prisoners maintaining the instrument of their own custody, which no regulation anticipated and none forbids. Dockets get carried, sorted, and shelved by the very names written on them. The Inspector finds a checkpoint in perfect order, staffed principally by its criminals, and pronounces it calm. The barrier, relieved of forty pounds of paper, rises an inch on its own, like a patient trying a limb.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: The docket mountain chooses your shift to become a docket landslide. You are struck by the collected self arrests of an entire parish and go down under the weight of other people's compliance. The sentries dig you out respectfully; several apologize by rank. The Inspector notes vigorous activity and moves on; your ribs file a minority report.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Petition to have the checkpoint declared a model

success.text: You present the numbers with perfect posture: arrests up ninety-one fold, zero unfiled persons since the placard rose, the safest road in the province by the Guard's own arithmetic. The Inspector cannot fault figures the Guard itself certified; auditing them would mean auditing certification, and beneath that floor there is no floor. He designates the checkpoint a model installation, to be preserved, studied, and never simplified. The Sub-Warden salutes; the barrier, pinned though it is, manages a dignified quiver.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: The Inspector agrees so completely that he drafts four more checkpoints for quieter roads, each with its own placard and its own barrier, sisters to this one. You have exported the jam without the jammers. The neighboring villages will need the recipe, the rota, and the kettle, urgently. The signwriter starts four more rubbings and does not sleep.
effects: schedule 4 r18_checkpoint_law_compromise The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

The Sub-Warden invites three witnesses into the guardhouse for tea in regulation cups, saucers held at the approved angle. His offer is on paper: the Guard will halve the reassurance levy on crossings if the town's rate of self arrest returns to what he calls plausible levels. The widow places her slate on the tea table, dots upward, and takes no tea. Through the window the barrier lies flat, listening the way furniture listens.

choices.text: Sit in silence until the terms improve

outcomes.text: Nobody speaks. The Sub-Warden refills three untouched cups, then, unnerved by a quiet with no procedural handle on it, improves the terms twice against himself. The levy dies entirely, and the count will be read aloud at the barrier each quarter day, by the Guard, as official traffic review. Silence, it turns out, is the one posture with no counterform. The dots stay unexplained, but they will now be read out loud, and out loud is ground the Guard has never had to hold.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

The levy dies entirely, and the count will be read aloud at the barrier each quarter day, by the Guard, as official traffic review. Silence, it turns out, is the one posture with no counterform. The dots stay unexplained, but they will now be read out loud, and out loud is ground the Guard has never had to hold.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: Nobody speaks, and the Sub-Warden discovers he can also not speak, having trained on longer silences in colder rooms. Two hours pass at the approved angle. When you finally stand, a sentry escorts you out by the elbow with reassuring firmness, and the door frame assists. The terms stay on the table, and the table stays his.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Accept the deal, pending proper countersignature

outcomes.text: You accept on the spot, effective upon countersignature by every arresting officer of record. The Sub-Warden reaches for his pen before the arithmetic arrives: every self arrested townsperson is an arresting officer, and his settlement now requires nine thousand signatures in approved ink. Faced with a signature page longer than the road, he abolishes the levy outright just to shrink the document. You sign the shortened version twice, as officer and as detainee, out of courtesy to the old form.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: The Sub-Warden finds the counterclause before you finish smiling: signing authority may be delegated to any officer of the installation, and the barrier, he rules, is the installation's senior officer. Its counterweight is inked and pressed to the page, and the deal is thereby signed by machinery that cannot read it, an arrangement in which the Guard reports total confidence. The levy is halved, not killed. You have been outranked by a plank, and the plank was gracious about it.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Raise the barrier by hand and hold it

success.text: You put your shoulder under the arm and lift, and the road starts moving through a gap you are pretending is legal. The Sub-Warden consults the regulations and finds that a raised barrier is a barrier in the open position, which is a recognized position; nothing governs how it got there. Carts pass all afternoon under your arms, and the levy cannot attach to crossings the barrier permitted. The widow disapproves on principle: muscle is not procedure, even when it is holding the ceiling up.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: The counterweight disagrees with your shoulder, and the counterweight has been winning arguments since installation. The barrier comes down through your grip and takes three days of your health with it in one administrative instant. You are logged as a traffic incident with excellent posture. The Sub-Warden posts a second sentry on the mechanism, in case your idea had friends.
effects: schedule 5 r18_checkpoint_law_reversal The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

Quarter day, and the Guard's payroll wagon rolls up to the barrier from the wrong side. The signwriter goes pale over his lettering: the placard binds the traveler, and by the Guard's own glossary a sentry walking to his post is a traveler on foot. No sentry has ever filed a Form CR-1. Every man at this checkpoint has been crossing in violation of the order he guards, daily, since the paint dried.

choices.text: Serve the Sub-Warden his own placard, certified

outcomes.text: You serve him a certified true copy of his placard, teal nowhere on it. The Sub-Warden reads the operative sentence like a man meeting a relative at a funeral, and then, because he is devout before he is anything else, he complies. He fills Form CR-1 in his best hand, names himself twice, and joins the queue as its most correct member. From inside the record he can file no one as traffic; prisoners keep custody of each other, and custody keeps names. The barrier stands up straight all day, because nobody who is anybody is available to lower it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

outcomes.text: He rules on the spot that sentries are not travelers but fixtures of the installation. The sentries stand very still to support the finding. But fixtures cannot make arrests either, a corollary he does not hear leave his own mouth, and the whole garrison spends the week furniture-shaped and jurisdictionless. Pressure builds in the capital about a checkpoint that guards nothing but its own definition. The placard stays up; so, unhelpfully for him, does its meaning.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Sell backdated self arrest forms to sentries

outcomes.text: You sell backdated Forms CR-1 to panicking sentries at six coins a soul, confession by way of forgery, absolution by way of receipt. Business is brisk and ashamed of itself. The Sub-Warden buys two, one as a spare, and pays without meeting your eye, which is how you learn he has known his arithmetic for days. By dusk you are the richest person at the checkpoint and the only one at it who is not, on paper, under arrest.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

choices.text: Demand the undelivered traffic be delivered

success.text: Traffic, the code says, is persons and goods in transit, and transit ends in arrival; the witnesses file one delivery demand per dot on the widow's slate. Nobody at the barrier makes a joke about this, and nobody looks for one. Over the following weeks, at odd hours and without announcement, arrivals begin to occur. The widow sits at her stall each dawn, and one by one, some dots become strokes. The slate does not celebrate; it counts, which was always the point.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

fail.text: The demands are accepted for filing, and filing is where the Guard keeps what it fears. A sentry pushes the queue back harder than reassurance requires, and your shoulder meets the barrier's post. The dots stay dots for now. But the demands exist, numbered, stamped, and undeniable, and the town's patience has acquired an edge that every man in that guardhouse can hear when the wind drops.
effects: schedule 2 r18_checkpoint_law_legacy The barrier would like a word, in triplicate

text:
CHECKPOINT LAW

A year on, the checkpoint has no garrison and the barrier has a name: the town calls it Cyril. Repainted in wedding white, it rises for brides, funerals, and the widow, in ascending order of solemnity. The crossing records hang in the weigh house, strokes and dots together, read aloud on quarter days by whoever holds the rota. Beneath them, under glass, hangs the placard, retired the only way the town knows how: obeyed to death. The former Sub-Warden keeps the glass polished; devotion, it turns out, survives its object.

choices.text: License the barrier's likeness for souvenir tokens

outcomes.text: Pilgrims of administrative comedy come from three provinces to see the barrier that arrested its own garrison, and you meet them at the road with souvenir tokens stamped with Cyril's profile, arm raised. They sell at eight coins and argue about nothing. A capital wholesaler orders a crate and pays six up front. The widow examines a token, notes that the profile flatters the counterweight, and buys none; some accounts, her look says, are still open, and yours has a new line in it.

Pilgrims of administrative comedy come from three provinces to see the barrier that arrested its own garrison, and you meet them at the road with souvenir tokens stamped with Cyril's profile, arm raised. They sell at eight coins and argue about nothing. A capital wholesaler orders a crate and pays six up front. The widow examines a token, notes that the profile flatters the counterweight, and buys none; some accounts, her look says, are still open, and yours has a new line in it.

choices.text: Take the dawn shift on the crossing rota

success.text: You take the dawn shift: grain carts through at first light, Cyril raised in salute, lowered gently behind, a crossing kept rather than a road guarded. Keeping a crossing, it turns out, generates paperwork, and the witnesses discover they have written a form of their own, with fields, and a filing box, and opinions. The widow stamps each completed sheet in teal, which is, she observes, strictly decorative. The grain gets through on time, every time, which no placard ever managed.

fail.text: Dawn, frost, stiff rope, and Cyril comes down half a beat early with full ceremony onto your boot, which was in the ceremony. The grain carts wait while you express yourself. The rota keepers rule the injury a crossing incident, self inflicted, and you laugh until it hurts, which is immediately. Cyril is forgiven by mid morning; barriers, the widow notes, only know one trick, and the town chose to keep it anyway.

choices.text: Draft the crossing law in approved lettering

outcomes.text: You draft the crossing law in the Guard's approved lettering: the barrier serves the road, the count is public, and no person may be filed as anything but a person. The province ratifies it without reading it, which is how the province ratifies everything. One rider arrives that nobody requested: all town weddings now legally require Cyril's attendance, and Cyril's fee, by long custom suddenly discovered, is cake. The witnesses deliberate and conclude they can live under this regime. At the next wedding Cyril is raised in salute and holds it, some say, a moment longer than mechanics require.

The province ratifies it without reading it, which is how the province ratifies everything. A visiting registrar reads your draft, rules the wedding salute a traffic signal, and schedules Cyril for signal inspection. Cyril fails on grounds of joy: the raise is judged eleven degrees more enthusiastic than a signal requires. The town appeals; the appeal requires a form; the form does not exist yet; the registrar waits. The salute continues at every wedding, unlicensed, beloved, and eleven degrees over, while the paperwork ages toward irrelevance in a box the widow stamps, decoratively, in teal.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

Tuesday, nine sharp, the dungeon entrance: a new stabbing schedule has gone up, and it doubles the matinees. The matinee mimic reads it twice and shuts with a click that echoes. Behind the waterfall the monsters are drafting a letter about hazard rates, one rate per stabbing, and the hydra is voting on whether to sign, one vote per head. The count stands at two for, four against, and one head asleep.

choices.text: Sell Heroic Delving the gossip from behind the waterfall

outcomes.text: The experience coordinator pays without haggling, which should have told you something, then pays again for the names. By Thursday the schedule has a new column titled Attitude, and the ghost's hums are marked Needs Warmth. The sleeping hydra head wakes up, hears what you did, and votes against with feeling. The waterfall runs louder than usual, the way water gets when it knows.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Chip in for the letter's notary fee

outcomes.text: You put three coins in the kitty and the weekend skeleton records them in the rota book under Friends. The letter goes out notarized, stamped, and smelling faintly of pond, which is how official things smell down here. The mimic offers you a seat inside itself, which is the highest honor it knows how to give. The hydra's fifth head watches you pay and says, mostly to itself, that it is listening.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The mimic, moved by your gesture, forgets itself and shakes your hand with the lid. You lose a little skin, and the weekend skeleton waives your fee on the spot, logging the wound as one incident, workplace, minor. The letter goes out anyway, and upstairs somebody important reads it and frowns at a mountain. Word runs through the tunnels that the surface sent someone who bleeds for the cause of not bleeding, and three caves start letters of their own.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: File the letter as a formal grievance with the System

outcomes.text: Form 7-G, Grievance of Persons Employed in Peril, exists, which surprises everyone including the System. You file it in triplicate and the entrance turnstile prints a receipt with a case number and, unprompted, the word finally. Heroic Delving must now respond in writing within ten days, and writing is the one weapon they respect. The hydra's third head says due process out loud, experimentally, like a new food.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The clerk upstairs files the letter under Structures, Decorative, because the intake form has no box for a mimic with opinions. The System replies that fixtures cannot file grievances, though they may apply for depreciation. Heroic Delving hangs the reply in the break room they do not provide. The hydra's heads argue all night about whether depreciation is at least a start.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_assembly Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

Eight o'clock behind the waterfall, and the weekend skeleton has chalked an agenda on a coffin lid: hazard rates, the matinee question, any other business. The ghost has done the ambience, warm instead of dreadful, and everyone notices and nobody says so. The hydra arrives at three for and four against, the fifth head lately won over by a jar of pickled eels. Seating it takes eleven minutes.

choices.text: Run the sign-in table by the coffin lid

outcomes.text: You check in forty two monsters, a moss attending in a personal capacity, and the hydra, which counts as one attendee and seven opinions. The weekend skeleton calls the sheet the finest paperwork the dungeon has ever produced, and it has produced tourists' wills. The meeting votes to ask for a rate per stabbing, doubled for matinees, plus a quiet hour for the ghost. Head five, full of eels, seconds everything and beams.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_retaliation Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The sign-in quill turns out to be a cockatrice feather with residual feelings, and your hand goes stony to the wrist for an hour. You keep checking monsters in left handed, which the tunnels take as a statement, and by midnight three more caves have chalked agendas of their own. The weekend skeleton logs your hand as incident, workplace, ironic. The hydra's fourth head, firmly against, admits that was at least committed.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_retaliation Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Chair the meeting so all seven heads get speaking time

success.text: You allot two minutes a head and enforce it with a teaspoon on a kettle, and for the first time in recorded quorum the hydra does not interrupt itself. Business concludes early. The motion passes on a show of hands, wings, lids, and necks, and even the against heads compliment the format. The company's spy, a tourist in an unconvincing cloak, reports upstairs that the monsters are alarmingly reasonable.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_retaliation Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: You give each head two minutes, but heads three and six pool their time to relitigate a goose incident from long before your birth. The meeting runs four hours and resolves only that the goose question be tabled, which head six calls typical. Upstairs, Heroic Delving hears the monsters argued all night and books an extra tour to test the mood. The cloaked spy's notes conclude they are no threat, merely a family, which is the biggest mistake in anyone's file.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_retaliation Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Cater the meeting at surface prices

outcomes.text: You sell forty two suppers of surface bread at triple markup, and the monsters pay, because eating together felt like the point. The eel money runs dry, and head five, unfed, wobbles back toward against before catching itself. The weekend skeleton settles your invoice in exact change from the kitty and does not look at you, which from a skeleton is a look. Heroic Delving hears there is money in monster catering and files a trademark on the words Dungeon Lunch.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_retaliation Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

The reply from Heroic Delving arrives stapled to a new rota: matinees tripled, the ghost reassigned to screams, and the mimic listed as Prop, Wooden, quantity one. The weekend skeleton reads it aloud twice, once for the meeting and once for the moss. The hydra's second head has meanwhile come around on a side deal, first claim on the morning sunbeam, bringing the tally to four for and three against. At the entrance the coordinator waits with a clipboard and a smile that was trained somewhere.

choices.text: Cite the ten day reply clause at the coordinator

outcomes.text: The coordinator flips the rota over, and there on the back the System has already stamped the case number and the words response noncompliant. Reassigning a grievant mid grievance is a further count, and you say so slowly, the way paperwork likes to be said. The matinees drop back to two while the counts pile up, and the mimic reopens with a show it describes as its rawest work. Head two, warm in its sunbeam, calls the law an acquired taste it has now acquired.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The coordinator listens, nods, and produces a newer form, 7-H, Grievance Withdrawal, prefilled with the mimic's signature, which is a forgery, because the mimic signs in bite marks. Proving that will take weeks the schedule does not have. The System, asked to choose between two of its own forms, requests a third. The hydra's against heads say told you, but quietly, because the ghost is doing ominous tonight and it is working.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Jam the entrance turnstile at peak matinee

success.text: You wedge a tourist's abandoned shield into the works and the turnstile stops mid click, which down here counts as a public address. Forty tourists mill at the entrance while the dungeon takes an unscheduled breath, and somewhere a scheduled scream goes gratefully unscreamed. The company loses a matinee's takings and learns the entrance can say no. The weekend skeleton thanks you and asks you never to do it again, because the monsters mean to win by rota, not by wreckage, and even the hydra's for heads nod at that.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: The turnstile, it turns out, is warded, and it discharges a month of stored admission magic into your elbows. You wake in the moss with a tourist standing over you asking if this is part of the experience. Heroic Delving hires four guards with matching halberds by the weekend and bills them to a line item called Atmosphere. The hydra's first head observes, not unkindly, that this is why it reads the wards before touching anything.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Cover the mimic's tripled matinees yourself

outcomes.text: You spend the day as a decoy chest, lid propped, taking prods so the mimic can rest its hinges. Tourists rate you highly convincing, and one tips you, and the tip goes in the kitty. The mimic watches from the shadows and later says apprentice like it is conferring a knighthood. Even head seven, the youngest, pauses mid against to ask whether it could also be a chest someday.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: You take a prod under the lid from a tourist who paid extra for enthusiasm, and it costs you a rib's worth of comfort. The mimic pulls you out, apologizing in slams, and the tunnels hum with the news that the surface bleeds for the rota now. The weekend skeleton logs it as incident, workplace, chivalrous. Head seven declares that when the next vote comes it knows which way the chest people are going.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_coalition Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

Word has gone round the deep places that the monsters could use friends, and friends are a thing rivers have opinions about. The underground river has held a grudge against Heroic Delving since the company shortened it for a flume ride, and a grudge gives a river excellent posture. The hydra stands at five for, head seven having joined in exchange for signing the river's invitation in its own wobbly bite marks, leaving head six against over the goose and head one against on principle. Upstairs, the coordinator is pricing guards by the dozen.

choices.text: Sell the company a consultation on monster morale

outcomes.text: You write Heroic Delving a report titled Restoring the Fear, invoice attached, and the coordinator pays it from the Atmosphere budget without reading past the title. Your recommendations are two thirds invented, but the third that is real tells them where the meetings happen. Guards with matching halberds begin standing near the waterfall, casually, the way halberds do. The river notes your scent going upstairs and keeps it, because rivers keep everything they have carried, and the hydra's for heads agree to stop rehearsing arguments in front of you.
effects: schedule 4 r18_monster_local_crisis Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Ask the river to attend the hearing in person

success.text: You address the river politely, by its full name, all nine syllables, and it rises an inch to consider you, which is a river's handshake. It agrees to attend, and testimony from four hundred miles of witness has a way of thinning out halberds. On the day it flows in under the door and sits in the gallery, perfectly level, radiating jurisdiction. The hydra's two against heads watch it and mutter that fine, water gets a say now, fine.
effects: schedule 4 r18_monster_local_crisis Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: You mispronounce syllable six, which means mudslide, and the river corrects your accent with a cold slap that empties your left boot. It still agrees to come, because the grudge outranks the grammar, but the tunnels spend the night gossiping about the surface's vowels. The weekend skeleton logs it as incident, diplomatic, soggy. Head one remarks that the river once slapped it too, centuries back, and that this was the beginning of respect.
effects: schedule 4 r18_monster_local_crisis Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Slip the tourists an honest program note

outcomes.text: You tuck a page into every waiver folder: names, shifts, and a note that the mimic being pried at has done two shows daily for nine years. Tourists, it turns out, mostly wanted to know who they were stabbing. By Friday a party of six asks the ghost for an encore hum and tips the hydra by mistake, and the hydra keeps it, seven to nothing, its first unanimous decision on any subject. The coordinator cancels one guard on cost grounds, which is how conscience arrives at a company.
effects: schedule 4 r18_monster_local_crisis Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: Your program note is confiscated at the turnstile and reprinted upstairs as Official Monster Backstories, four coins, lore approved. The facts survive but the names are gone, which was somehow the entire point. Tourists quote the pamphlet at the mimic, which has never been called a lonely woodland spirit before and files the first noise complaint of its life. The river reads a soggy copy in the gallery queue and adds the pamphlet to the grudge.
effects: schedule 4 r18_monster_local_crisis Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

STABATHON, say the surface posters, one weekend only, every monster, waivers half price. Heroic Delving has bused in freelance beasts to cover any shift the rota declines, and the monsters' answer is simple and terrible: when the matinee bell rings, the whole dungeon takes tea at the same time. Head three has apologized to head six about the goose, in front of everyone, and head six cried and voted for, which makes six. The holdout is head one, who has moved from against to abstaining, on a principle it declines to name.

choices.text: Carry the tea tray to every post when the bell rings

success.text: The bell rings and the dungeon goes domestic all at once: the mimic open and sipping, the skeletons dunking, the ghost steaming gently for effect. Tourists wander through the most peaceful dungeon in the world and find they quite like it, and the freelance beasts, unsure of the etiquette, accept a cup rather than start anything. Nothing is stabbed for two hours and the company loses a fortune to the sound of spoons. Head one abstains from tea as well, on principle, but holds its saucer correctly, which everyone agrees is progress.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_compromise Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: You are pouring for the ogre post when a Stabathon party of twelve arrives early, keen, and refuses to perceive the teapot. An elbow in the crush cracks your rib against a shield rack, and the ghost has to do dreadful at full volume to clear the room. The break holds anyway, raggedly, and the tunnels boil about the twelve for days. The weekend skeleton logs it as incident, workplace, brave, and underlines brave twice with a finger bone, and head one asks to see the logbook.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_compromise Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Serve the System an emergency peril injunction

outcomes.text: The System, presented with a weekend of unscheduled peril, discovers it has always had an opinion about scheduling. The injunction arrives as a red seal on the turnstile: no admission beyond posted rates of stabbing, by order. The coordinator appeals and is quoted a hearing date after the weekend, which is the System's idea of a joke and also of justice. Head one reads the seal three times and says hm, which from head one is nearly a vote.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_compromise Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The System grants the injunction but addresses it to the monsters, enjoining them from being stabbed above posted rates, penalties applicable to the stabbed. It is the right ruling in the wrong direction, which is the System at its purest. The weekend proceeds until the ghost works out that a monster on tea break cannot lawfully be stabbed at all, and ambience becomes jurisprudence. Upstairs the coordinator frames the injunction, then reads it properly and takes it down.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_compromise Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Guide the Stabathon yourself at premium rates

outcomes.text: You lead twelve tourists through on a route that avoids the tea, and the coordinator pays a premium plus a bonus for discretion. The dungeon you show them is technically open and entirely hollow, all props and no persons, and the reviews say it felt like stabbing furniture. The mimic watches your tour pass and does not open, which is a mimic's way of not shaking hands. Head one notes your fee to the coin and says nothing, which is worse.
effects: schedule 5 r18_monster_local_compromise Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

Negotiations open in the antechamber at noon: Heroic Delving on one side, the monsters on the other, and between them a table nobody measured for a hydra. Seven heads with one vote each require seven agendas, seven water bowls, and a seating plan the weekend skeleton has drafted four times and grieved over once, drily. Head one has stopped abstaining and started deliberating, which the other six report like weather. The coordinator has brought a second clipboard, which means the company is serious.

choices.text: Draft the hazard clause in words a company can sign

success.text: You write it plainly: one stabbing, one rate, doubled for matinees, tripled for anyone the file calls Prop. The coordinator reads it twice looking for the trap, and the trap is that there is none, and nothing unsettles a company like a fair sentence. Both sides initial the draft, and the ghost does celebratory ambience, which is the ordinary ambience but warmer. Six heads initial it too, in order of height, while head one deliberates at the page respectfully.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: Your draft is clean, but the company's lawyer inserts the word reasonable before hazard, and you miss it until the reading. Reasonable, in company usage, means whatever survives an argument the monsters cannot afford. The draft goes back for repairs and the seating plan needs a fifth version, because head four now refuses to face the lawyer directly. The weekend skeleton adds a column to the rota titled Words We Do Not Sign.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Audit the Atmosphere budget line by line

success.text: You follow the Atmosphere line into a ledger nobody meant to show you, and it is all there: halberds, spy cloaks, one trademark on the words Dungeon Lunch. You copy the page and take a finder's fee from the coordinator's petty cash on the way out, calling it postage owed. The lawyer's confidence sags visibly at the next session, like ambience going the other way. The monsters are glad of the ledger and quietly uneasy about the fee, and the weekend skeleton leaves your name under Friends but adds a small question mark, and the hydra's sixth head practices frowning at you.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

fail.text: You find the ledger, and the coordinator finds you finding it, and the offer arrives before the shame does: eight coins for the copy and your silence. You take it. The next session the lawyer is confident in a way that has clearly been paid for, and the monsters cannot work out why their numbers keep bouncing off. Head one watches you across the table with the patience of something that has spent seven lifetimes waiting people out.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Keep the seven water bowls filled through the talks

outcomes.text: You spend four hours refilling seven bowls in strict order of seniority, which you learn by being corrected six times. Hydration, it turns out, is diplomacy: a watered head is a listening head, and by evening the company has conceded matinee rates and the ghost's quiet hour. Head one drinks last, deliberately, and thanks you, which the other six confirm has not happened within living memory, and theirs is long. The seating plan works on the fifth draft and the weekend skeleton signs it like a masterpiece.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: Head two and head five disagree about bowl order, and you are the ground the disagreement happens over, because a hydra's shrug has knocked over smaller buildings. You come up bruised, still holding the pitcher, and pour head five's water without spilling, which earns a sevenfold silence of respect. Talks resume. The tunnels hear that the company kept negotiating while you were under a hydra, and think considerably less of the company, which takes doing.
effects: schedule 2 r18_monster_local_reversal Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

A rainy Thursday, and the ghost, once reassigned to screams, has been rereading the tourist waivers for comfort, as one does. Clause nine, in the smallest print, names a peril surcharge, collected at the turnstile, payable to the imperiling party. The imperiling party, by the company's own definitions, is the monsters, and the surcharge has been collected for nine years and paid to no one. Somewhere upstairs sits an account with a matinee mimic's name on it, roughly speaking, and the coordinator has stopped smiling.

choices.text: Broker the buried surcharge back to the company quietly

outcomes.text: The coordinator meets you behind the turnstile with a strongbox and an understanding: the clause disappears, the account disappears, and you disappear richer. Fourteen coins is what nine years of other people's danger goes for, apparently, wholesale. The ghost, who found the clause, drifts through you on its way past, and for a week everything you drink tastes faintly of rain. Head one writes something in its private tally and shows no one.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_legacy Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Front the filing fee to claim the surcharge account

outcomes.text: The claim requires a fee, because of course it does, and you pay it from your own purse before the kitty can argue. The account opens like a tomb with savings in it: nine years of surcharge, itemized by stabbing, a number that makes the weekend skeleton sit down on a coffin it normally respects too much to sit on. The monsters vote to pay back rates first and buy the ghost a proper quiet hour second. Head one, presented with an itemized list of every wound it was never paid for, stops deliberating and asks for a pen, then sets it down, because a vote this old should not be rushed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_legacy Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The claim office is up nine flights and the clerk waves your coins away, because the imperiling party must appear in person, so you escort the mimic, who has never been upstairs and holds your hand in its lid the whole way. The corridor crush costs you a toe's worth of dignity under a filing cart. The clerk processes the mimic like anyone: species, occupation, who depends on you, and the mimic answers everyone in the dungeon and means it. Going back down, the claim accepted, the mimic hums the ghost's hum, badly, all nine flights.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_legacy Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

choices.text: Read clause nine into the record at the hearing

outcomes.text: You read it slowly, the way paperwork likes, and the gallery goes so quiet the river can be heard being level. The System rules on the spot that a surcharge collected in someone's name belongs to the someone, and that monsters are someones, see clause nine, the company's own hand. It is the rare judgment that surprises no one and changes everything. The coordinator's second clipboard is out of arguments, and head one asks for the ruling read twice, and then a third time, softer.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_legacy Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

outcomes.text: You read clause nine into the record, and the company's lawyer moves to strike it as a typographical error nine years long. The System, which has made typographical errors, is sympathetic, and adjourns to consider whether money can be a typo. The tunnels take the delay badly, and the freelance beasts, still owed their own wages, start asking pointed questions upstairs. Head one deliberates harder, which the other six report as almost done, they can hear it.
effects: schedule 3 r18_monster_local_legacy Seven heads, one vote each, and the kettle is on.

text:
MONSTER LOCAL

Signing day, and the antechamber smells of pond water and fresh ink. The contract lies open on a table finally measured for a hydra: hazard rates per stabbing, matinee premiums, a quiet hour, and the peril surcharge paid out at the turnstile, to the payee, in person, by name. Tourists now sign a waiver that lists who works here, and it has made them strangely polite, the way people get when the furniture has a name. All that remains is the vote, and the hydra needs seven.

choices.text: Stand witness while the seven heads vote

outcomes.text: The votes go in order of height: for, for, for, for, for, for, and then head one, which looks at the mimic, the skeleton, the ghost, the river in the gallery, and finally at you. For, says head one, and the hydra is unanimous for the first time in its long argumentative life, and all seven heads discover this at the same moment and none of them can speak. The ghost does the warm ambience without being asked. The weekend skeleton records the tally in the rota book, seven to nothing, and underneath, in careful chalk, writes carried.

The votes go in order of height: for, for, for, for, for, for, and then head one, which looks at the mimic, the skeleton, the ghost, the river in the gallery, and finally at you. For, says head one, and the hydra is unanimous for the first time in its long argumentative life, and all seven heads discover this at the same moment and none of them can speak. The ghost does the warm ambience without being asked. The weekend skeleton records the tally in the rota book, seven to nothing, and underneath, in careful chalk, writes carried.

Head one votes for, the hydra goes unanimous, and seven heads attempt to embrace you at once, which is how you learn that a hydra's love has a tonnage. You come out of it with a creaked rib, no regrets, and seven apologies delivered in strict order of seniority. Outside, the tunnels celebrate loudly enough to worry the surface, which files a noise complaint that the ghost, now salaried for ambience, reviews personally and rejects. The contract is signed in ink, bite marks, and one careful damp line from the gallery.

choices.text: Read the contract aloud one last time before the ink

success.text: You read every clause aloud, including the small ones, because down here small print has a history. On clause nine the room goes quiet, on the quiet hour the ghost glows, and on the words workers and persons, plural, no exceptions, the seventh head starts the vote early out of sheer feeling and is gently shushed. Then the vote, then seven for, then a hydra weeping from every eye it owns, which is fourteen. The coordinator signs last and asks, quietly, whether the surface has anything like this, and nobody laughs at him, quite.

fail.text: You read the contract aloud and stumble on clause nine, and the lawyer rises with the speed of a man paid by the objection. It takes an hour to establish the stumble was yours and not the clause's, an hour the seating plan does not survive, so the vote happens with head four facing a wall. Seven for, even so, from six mouths and one dignified echo, and the hydra sits unanimous and moved beyond speech, dripping quietly on the minutes. The lawyer bills the hour to Atmosphere, which no longer exists, so nobody pays, which everyone agrees is the correct ending for it.

choices.text: License the signing as a ticketed farewell event

outcomes.text: You sell forty tickets to Signing Day, Historic, One Time Only, and the coordinator splits the gate with you out of professional admiration. The tourists behave, mostly, though two ask the hydra to vote again for the picture, and it declines, because unanimity is not a matinee. Seven for, all the same, and the hydra sits quietly moved behind the flash powder while the monsters bank their rates and privately resolve that the next historic moment will be invite only. Head one watches you count the take and remarks, to nobody, that the surface always finds a turnstile, but the contract is bigger than you.

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

Four in the afternoon, twenty years on, and the common hall still keeps its key under the same brick. The woman who kept the minutes is on the step with a biscuit tin and a list of names, some of them crossed out in a kinder pen. Inside, the Bureau of Permanent Victory has labeled everything CAUTIONARY EXHIBIT 7, including the kettle. Under a dust sheet in the corner, something has already turned itself to face the door.

choices.text: Sweep twenty years off the floor with her

success.text: You sweep, she dusts, and the hall remembers how to be a room. By dark there are chairs in a rough ring and a list of who might come, read aloud twice, once for checking and once for courage. She writes your name in the margin, which is where the useful people live. The dust sheet in the corner stays where it is, by unspoken vote.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_assembly Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: The stepladder gives out under you at the exact moment the dust sheet slips, and you land hard in front of a chairman's chair standing at attention. She hauls you up, checks your elbow, and covers the chair again without meeting its eye. Word gets round the district that the hall is open and something in it moved. Attendance, oddly, doubles.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_assembly Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: File the Bureau's visitor form for the exhibit

outcomes.text: The Bureau of Permanent Victory grants a viewing permit for its own cautionary exhibit, valid for one commemorative gathering, tea included if declared. The clerk stamps it twice, once for the file and once out of habit. The minute keeper reads the permit aloud on the step, and neighbors who have offered nothing in years offer chairs. Nothing eases a street like paperwork that finally agrees with it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_assembly Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: You tick the wrong box and formally request that the exhibit be restored to active failure. The Bureau opens a supplementary file and posts a notice on the door asking the failure to proceed in an orderly fashion. The minute keeper takes the notice down, folds it, and adds it to the tin, where evidence goes to cool.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_assembly Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Sell the Bureau a condition report on the hall

outcomes.text: You inventory the hall for the Bureau, noting the kettle as slow and the morale as structural. They pay eight for the report and six more because your handwriting fits their forms. Somewhere upstairs a file grows an appendix with your name on it. The dust sheet in the corner does not care for your tone.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_assembly Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

Seven arrive on the first evening, which is six more than the minute keeper let herself expect. The kettle takes eleven minutes, so old arguments have time to take their coats off and become apologies. The man who left first stands in the doorway holding a cake he cannot explain. Every time somebody speaks, the chairman's chair under its sheet creaks a quarter turn toward the sound.

choices.text: Set the room in a circle with no front

outcomes.text: You arrange the seats so the room has no head, only a middle, and the middle fills with tea. Speeches die politely for lack of a podium; sentences get shorter and truer. The chairman's chair rotates all night looking for the front and never finds one. The Bureau of Permanent Victory has no form for a room without a front, and forms are how it sees.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_retaliation Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: By nine the circle has drifted back into rows, the way water finds old channels. The chairman's chair ends up facing the rows with an air of quiet promotion, and conversation dies of good posture. The man who left first slips out before the tea; the minute keeper records only: early days. You stack the seats so tomorrow can start from zero.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_retaliation Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Take the Bureau's stipend to observe the evening

outcomes.text: The Bureau of Permanent Victory pays observers eight a night to confirm its exhibits remain cautionary. You confirm it, plus six for noting the cake. It is easy money and the tea is the same tea. The chair creaks toward you once, as if accepting a nomination.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_retaliation Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Sit with the man who left first

success.text: You take the cake from him so his hands have nothing to defend. He talks for eleven kettle minutes about the sentence he never finished in year three, and you do not finish it for him. When the tea is ready he is still there, which is the whole victory. The minute keeper writes: cake, accepted.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_retaliation Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: He hands you the cake and the plate cracks clean in half, twenty years of pressure finding the nearest china. A splinter of it catches your palm; he apologizes for that, and for nothing else yet. He stays anyway, standing, coat on. The chair creaks toward the noise and everyone ignores it professionally.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_retaliation Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

Tuesday brings an inspector from the Bureau of Permanent Victory, citing unauthorized warmth in a cautionary exhibit. He sets up at the head table, sits in the first seat he finds, and the sheet is not on the chairman's chair anymore. Within minutes he is chairing his own audit, calling himself to order, seconding his own motions. The survivors watch a stranger relive their history at speed.

choices.text: Read the Bureau's file while he chairs himself

success.text: The file is mostly the Bureau congratulating itself, but page forty holds a rent ledger showing the hall paid up decades ahead by the seventh commune's last kitty. You copy it out, and the district's rent argument gets four coins lighter, starting with yours. The survivors are less delighted; you touched the file, and the file is where their worst year lives. The inspector, mid-audit, seconds a motion to adjourn himself.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: Page forty turns out to be a reward schedule: the Bureau pays eight for updates on cautionary exhibits, and you are holding a pen. You file an update; the rent on the hall goes up to fund the reward that reported it. Nobody saw you do it, which is not the same as nobody knowing. The chair creaks approvingly, which is worse.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Pour the inspector tea and stand with the others

outcomes.text: The kettle takes its eleven minutes, so the inspector must sit in the silence his own chair keeps gaveling. By the second cup he has stopped seconding himself and started asking what the soup was like, back then. The minute keeper tells him about the soup. He leaves with a full audit, an empty argument, and the arrears notice unserved in his case.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: The chair creaks mid-pour, you flinch, and the tea takes the back of your hand. The inspector logs the flinch as evidence of instability, then apologizes to the chair. Word of the audit gets out by supper, and the street takes it personally on your behalf.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Cite the exhibit code against his audit

success.text: Exhibit regulation nine: a cautionary exhibit must be preserved as found, and it was found holding meetings. The inspector reads the clause four times, loses to it, and initials his own defeat. The Bureau's stamp comes down on a permit to continue failing as originally exhibited. It is the most useful document the hall has ever owned.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: You misquote the code by one subsection and accidentally certify the chair as the exhibit's presiding officer. The inspector notes it with relief, delighted to leave someone in charge. There is now a Bureau document stating that the furniture outranks you. The minute keeper files it in the tin, under grievances, pending.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_coalition Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

A list of nine names remains: survivors scattered across three districts, one lighthouse, and a prison term served long ago and never mentioned twice. The reunion proper needs all of them, or it is just a rehearsal with cake. The minute keeper writes invitations while the kettle warms itself for nobody in particular. The chairman's chair now faces the wall, by resolution; it creaks itself back around by morning.

choices.text: Put your coins in the kitty for the far travelers

outcomes.text: Three coins buy a cart seat for the one at the lighthouse and postage for all the rest. Answers come back in handwriting that has aged twenty years and softened ten. Yes, says the lighthouse. Yes, says the district past the river, and one letter asks only: is the chair still there, and does it still do the thing.
effects: schedule 5 r18_failed_commune_crisis Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: The cart you hired throws a wheel, and you walk the last hours carrying luggage that arrived without its someone. Your shoulder files an objection that lasts a day. The luggage waits by the door like an argument for hope. The street hears the story wrong and spends the evening restless about carts.
effects: schedule 5 r18_failed_commune_crisis Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Word the invitations as exhibit maintenance

outcomes.text: You draft the reunion as Scheduled Upkeep of Cautionary Exhibit 7, attendees listed as original components. The Bureau approves it in record time; nothing moves paperwork like the chance to preserve a failure. Nine travel permits arrive stamped, one addressed to the chair itself. The minute keeper allows herself one entire laugh.

choices.text: Charge visitors for tours of the famous failure

outcomes.text: Strangers pay eight to see where it went wrong, and six extra to sit in the chair that did it, briefly, supervised. You do the voice, you point at the dents. It is good money and only costs you the look on the minute keeper's face. The chair, for its part, has never chaired better.

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

Nine of nine, one room, first time in twenty years, and for an hour it is only soup and names. Then the man who left first, tired from standing all evening on principle, sits in the chairman's chair without looking. His spine straightens on contact. He calls the room to order in a voice nobody has heard since year three, and the room, horribly, comes to order.

choices.text: Raise a point of order against the chair

success.text: Point of order: the standing orders of the seventh commune were never dissolved, only abandoned, and under them no chair may elect itself. The minute keeper finds the clause in the tin in eleven seconds, which is exactly how long she has been waiting to. He rises out of the chair like a man handed back his own coat. The meeting resumes as soup.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_compromise Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: You cite the standing orders from memory and get them backwards, accidentally moving a vote of confidence in the chair. Seconded, says the creak, according to at least three witnesses. He stays seated another awful minute on pure procedure. It takes the kettle boiling over to adjourn him.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_compromise Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Sell the Bureau an eyewitness note on the relapse

outcomes.text: The Bureau of Permanent Victory pays eight, same day, for confirmations that its exhibits remain cautionary, and six more if signed. You sign. Upstairs, the file on the seventh commune gains a page titled RECURRENCE, with your penmanship praised in the margin. The soup is excellent and sits badly.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_compromise Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Bring him a bowl before you bring him an argument

outcomes.text: You put soup in his hands, and a man cannot chair while holding a bowl, a flaw in the chair's whole design. Spoon by spoon the voice from year three goes out of him. He looks down at the chair as if waking on a train. Dinner is served early, to everyone, on the grounds that it works.

outcomes.text: He grips your wrist before he takes the bowl, hard enough to lend tomorrow a bruise, and says the agenda is not finished. It has not been finished for twenty years. The soup goes cold between you while the room holds its breath, and the street outside somehow hears about all of it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_failed_commune_compromise Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

Morning after, and the chair stands in the middle of the hall where nobody will admit to leaving it. The survivors take turns not looking at it over breakfast. Somebody must decide what a room does with the piece of furniture its history sat in. The kettle, an old diplomat, starts itself.

choices.text: Carry the chair out to the yard yourself

success.text: You lift it alone so nobody else has to touch it, and it comes up lighter than its reputation. In the yard it looks like what it is, a chair among weather. By noon two strangers have sat in it and begun calling meetings of passersby, so the yard needs watching now. Inside, the vote on whether you should have asked first is postponed, tactfully, forever.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_reversal Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: The chair catches the doorframe and the doorframe wins, twice, and your knee casts a third opinion. You requisition a Bureau hand truck to finish the job, which requires signing for it, which counts, technically, as joint operations. The chair rides out on state equipment looking decorated. Your knee files a long report.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_reversal Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Ask each survivor what the chair cost them

outcomes.text: It takes all morning and two kettles. The one from the lighthouse goes first, and after that the armor comes off the apologies one buckle at a time. Nobody defends the chair; nobody kicks it either, which twenty years ago would have seemed impossible in both directions. The minute keeper writes it all down, and for once the writing is the mercy.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_reversal Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: The third answer ignites the old fight about year three, and an elbow meant for emphasis finds your ribs. The room needs air and takes it separately, and the shouting carries to a street that begins drafting its own version. Still, everyone comes back inside, which the minutes record twice for emphasis.
effects: schedule 3 r18_failed_commune_reversal Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Amend the orders so elections need a second

outcomes.text: One line, adopted unanimously: no election stands without a second from a living person. The chair can nominate itself from now until the walls retire, and the motion will die in silence every time. It is the gentlest way anyone has ever fired a piece of furniture. The Bureau requests a copy and files it under weapons.

There is now a stamped document endorsing the furniture. The minute keeper begins drafting a reply that opens with the word respectfully and gets worse from there.

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

At last the tin comes out, and in it the minutes of the seventh commune's final meeting, never once read aloud. The minute keeper's hands are steady the way bridges are steady, by engineering. Everyone had assumed the chair did it: elected itself, elected Tam, chaired them all into the ground. The last page says otherwise, and she reads it out: no other nominations, none opposed, meeting closed.

choices.text: Hold the silence with them until it is done

success.text: Nobody fills the quiet, which is the hardest chore ever put on a rota. Somewhere inside it, the man who left first says the end of his sentence from year three, and it turns out to have been an apology the whole time. The minute keeper writes: heard. The chair does not creak once; there is nothing in the room for it to face.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_legacy Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

fail.text: The silence holds almost to the end, and then an ordinary seat scrapes, and everyone's grief mistakes it for the other chair. In the scramble your hand finds a splinter the table has been saving for twenty years. A door gets slammed by someone who needed a door. They come back; the minutes wait.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_legacy Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

choices.text: Enter the last minutes into the Bureau's file

outcomes.text: You submit the page to the Bureau of Permanent Victory as a factual correction: the seventh commune was not conquered by its furniture, it ran out of seconds. A clerk reads it twice, stamps it ADMITTED, and the file gains its first true page. The survivors sleep differently, knowing the record finally lost an argument. Even the chair seems to sit lower.
effects: schedule 4 r18_failed_commune_legacy Twenty years late, the seventh commune puts the kettle on

outcomes.text: The Bureau declines the correction: a cautionary exhibit, the clerk explains, is load-bearing, and the truth would require scaffolding. The page comes back unstamped, with a leaflet about acceptance. The minute keeper puts both in the tin, truth on top.

choices.text: Sell the original minutes to a Bureau archivist

outcomes.text: The archivist pays eight for the page and six for the tin's provenance, cash, gloves on. The minute keeper watches her own handwriting leave in an envelope and says nothing you will forget soon. There is a copy, but a copy is a photograph of a hand. Upstairs, the file wins one at last.

text:
FAILED COMMUNE

The final morning, one more meeting, called correctly, seconded by a living person, quorum of nine plus you. The agenda has four items and three of them are who brings what. The chairman's chair sits in the corner with a cushion on it, retired, where it may elect itself as often as it likes. The kettle takes eleven minutes and nobody minds anymore; that is what the minutes are for.

choices.text: Draft the agenda as a shopping list

outcomes.text: Bread, the lighthouse; soup, the minute keeper; spoons, the man who left first and stays now. The meeting lasts eleven minutes, kettle inclusive, and adjourns itself out of contentment. A copy goes to the Bureau of Permanent Victory, which files a shopping list as evidence of continued failure. For once, everyone hopes the file stays open forever.

Bread, the lighthouse; soup, the minute keeper; spoons, the man who left first and stays now. The meeting lasts eleven minutes, kettle inclusive, and adjourns itself out of contentment. A copy goes to the Bureau of Permanent Victory, which files a shopping list as evidence of continued failure. For once, everyone hopes the file stays open forever.

Your list drifts into subclauses, and by item two the bread has a steering group. The room laughs at you, gently, on schedule. The minute keeper strikes the whole thing and writes: bread, someone; soup, someone; enough, everyone.

choices.text: Accept the Bureau's fee for a memoir called How Not To

outcomes.text: Eight on signature, six on delivery, title fixed by the Bureau: How Not To, volume nine in a series nobody asked to be in. You write it warm and they print it cautionary; editing is the Bureau's only martial art. The survivors forgive you at the rate of one chapter a year. The chair is on the cover, wearing its cushion.

choices.text: Take the tin when her hands ask you to

success.text: The minute keeper slides the biscuit tin across the table without a speech, which is the speech. You are the keeper of the minutes now, and of the eleven kettle minutes, and of the difference. The last line in her hand reads: next meeting, spring, bring what you can. The chair in the corner creaks once, all in favor, and this time it counts for nothing and means well.

fail.text: You reach for the tin across the kettle, and the kettle, a traditionalist, objects across your knuckles. She wraps your hand in her handkerchief and puts the tin in it anyway. The street outside hears the yelp and assumes drama; the minutes record only: handover complete, minor scald. Spring is on the schedule.

text:
PIRATE CONSTITUTION

Six days out, Admiral Sole Share nails a notice to the mainmast: all horizons sighted, unsighted, or rumored are the property of the captaincy, effective the moment anyone looks at them. The deckhands gather at the wheel with the back of an old chart and a borrowed pen. The ship's parrot reads the notice twice and screams OBJECTION at the mainmast, which is the first anyone has heard of its legal career. Nobody sustains it. Not yet.

choices.text: Draft article one in fair round hand

outcomes.text: The quartermaster writes it out clean: the ship goes where the crew and the weather agree, and the weather votes last. It reads less like mutiny and more like a maintenance schedule, which is the point. The Admiral demands to know what law permits this, and the lookout says the one you nailed up, we are citing your notice as precedent for nailing things. The parrot repeats OBJECTION at intervals through the night, and the quartermaster writes objection noted at the bottom, which legally speaking is how all of this starts.
effects: schedule 3 r18_pirate_constitution_assembly A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

outcomes.text: The draft comes out beautifully and then the pen runs dry halfway through the signatures, which the Admiral declares proof that ink itself is loyal to the captaincy. Half the watch signs in tar, which smears, which he calls forgery. The parrot screams OBJECTION and for once everyone agrees with it, they just cannot prove which clause it means. Article one survives, but it survives the way a man survives a wedding he objected at.
effects: schedule 3 r18_pirate_constitution_assembly A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

choices.text: Sell the Admiral an advance copy of the draft

outcomes.text: He pays in silver counted twice, which tells you he has never once trusted a horizon either. He reads the draft, laughs at the lunch clause, and hires two extra marines on the strength of the rest of it. The deckhands notice the marines before they notice the leak, but they are sailors, they can work out where water comes from. The parrot lands on your shoulder and says OBJECTION directly into your ear, unsustained, but filed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_pirate_constitution_assembly A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

choices.text: Put three coins in the kitty and sign first

outcomes.text: Your name goes at the top, which on a pirate ship is traditionally where the noose starts reading, and the line behind you forms anyway. The cook signs second on the grounds that lunch needs a government. Your three coins buy a locked box, a better pen, and a perch by the wheel, because the parrot has taken up station there and shows no sign of billing anyone. By evening the back of the chart has more names than the muster book, and the muster book is officially jealous.
effects: schedule 3 r18_pirate_constitution_assembly A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

outcomes.text: You sign first, and a marlinspike drops from the yard above and takes the corner of your shoulder on its way to the deck. The Admiral calls it weather. The topmen call it a warning, and sign, all of them, in a hand shaking with either fear or wind. The parrot screams OBJECTION at the yard for a full minute, and though nobody sustains it, nobody argues either. Your shoulder complains until the dog watch.
effects: schedule 3 r18_pirate_constitution_assembly A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

text:
PIRATE CONSTITUTION

Midnight, dog watch, and the floating assembly convenes in the rigging where the Admiral's boots decline to follow. The question is what the articles are for: wages, lunch, or the wheel itself. The parrot perches on the topsail yard and counts the house, twice, out loud. It objects to the total. A recount finds the sailmaker voting once as himself and once as his hat, and the objection is sustained to general astonishment, including the parrot's.

choices.text: Auction your vote to the highest whisper

outcomes.text: The Admiral's steward bids highest, which everyone expects, with the Admiral's own silver, which even the parrot did not. You vote for a clause so watery it could be pumped overboard, and the assembly turns to sharper methods on the spot, having learned what gentle ones cost. You count fourteen coins in your hammock while above you the crew stops drafting and starts planning. The parrot watches you count and says nothing, which from that bird is the loudest objection on record.
effects: schedule 4 r18_pirate_constitution_retaliation A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

choices.text: Stand double watch so the whole mess can vote

outcomes.text: You take the helm through two watches so every deckhand can climb up and have a say, and the ship sails straighter for it, a fact the quartermaster enters into the record as evidence. The vote lands on plain words: shares by work, lunch by right, the wheel by rota. The cook weeps briefly into a clean apron. The parrot objects to nothing for one entire hour, which the assembly minutes describe as unanimous.
effects: schedule 4 r18_pirate_constitution_retaliation A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

outcomes.text: You take double watch and fall asleep standing, and the wheel takes a slow romantic turn toward a reef with a reputation. The lookout catches it, the crew hauls the ship round, and you catch a boom with your ear on the way to apologizing. The vote still carries, deckhands to a man, partly on the argument that even exhausted, one of their own steered better than the notice on the mast. The parrot objects to the reef. Sustained, retroactively, with feeling.
effects: schedule 4 r18_pirate_constitution_retaliation A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

choices.text: Argue a horizon is whatever the lookout sees

success.text: You put it to the assembly as a matter of trade: a horizon is defined by the eye that finds it, the finding eye is on the masthead, and the masthead is crewed by the lookout, who is paid a deckhand's share. Therefore every horizon the captaincy claims arrives pre-owned. The lookout stands, bows, and declares her eye open for business. Even the steward laughs, which widens the thing past the deckhands to anyone aboard with an eye. The parrot cries OBJECTION, then visibly cannot think of one, and the minutes record it as withdrawn.
effects: schedule 4 r18_pirate_constitution_retaliation A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

fail.text: The argument is beautiful for three sentences, and then you define horizon so thoroughly that the sailmaker moves the ship itself is one, being a thing always receding from perfection. Debate collapses into philosophy, the worst weather there is. The Admiral quotes your own definition back at the mast next morning, wrongly, but with better posture. Still the deckhands stay in the rigging talking, and the steward stays too, and the parrot maintains one long unbroken objection until dawn on principle.
effects: schedule 4 r18_pirate_constitution_retaliation A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

text:
PIRATE CONSTITUTION

Dawn brings the Admiral's answer: lunch is suspended pending a loyalty review, and the ship's wheel now wears a chain and a small brass plaque reading PRIVATE. The cook stands in the galley door holding a ladle like a writ. Somewhere aloft the parrot reads the plaque, pronounces OBJECTION with unusual care, and begins pulling at the chain's smallest link with the patience of a creature that has decided on a career.

choices.text: Feed the whole watch from the crew's own stores

outcomes.text: The mess pools what it has: a cheese of legal age, biscuit, a ham the cook has been saving for a constitutional emergency, which he insists this is. Lunch happens on the open deck, in sight of the quarterdeck, seasoned with the specific pleasure of eating well in front of the man who cancelled it. Two marines drift downwind of the ham and are quietly fed, which does more damage to the Admiral than any clause yet. The parrot receives a biscuit as its retainer, the first anyone has paid it, and pockets the arrangement in its head forever.
effects: schedule 5 r18_pirate_constitution_coalition A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

outcomes.text: The shared meal goes handsomely until the Admiral's steward reports the ham, and the marines arrive to confiscate lunch as evidence. In the scuffle you take an elbow holding the pot, and the pot survives, which the mess agrees was the right order of sacrifice. Eating together becomes the ship's quiet rebellion, twice daily, scheduled. The parrot objects to the confiscation and follows the evidence to the great cabin, screaming, all the way.
effects: schedule 5 r18_pirate_constitution_coalition A parrot has raised a constitutional objection

choices.text: Cite the lunch clause and demand a hearing

outcomes.text: You read article four off the chart back in a voice pitched for weather: lunch precedes captains, kings, and in the cook's opinion, land. The Admiral demands to know what court would hear such a case, and the quartermaster gestures at the assembled crew, who are, conveniently, assembled. The hearing lasts nine minutes. Lunch is reinstated with back pay in biscuit, the parrot's objection to the plaque is formally sustained, and the plaque itself is entered into evidence and never seen again.
effects: schedule 5 r18_pirate_constitution_coalition A parrot has raised a constitutional objection
