choices.text: Bring every family the schedule touched to the serving

success.text: They come in their good coats, the ones kept for hearings, and the front of the yard fills with everyone the clock ever chimed for. Nobody speaks during the nailing; afterward Pim's daughter reads out the ninth paragraph, and the sergeant with the oil can holds the ladder steady. It is short, the way the true things are. The families walk home unescorted, on streets with no schedule left on them, checking the sky for the time.
effects: schedule 2 r18_police_clock_legacy The clock knows tomorrow's arrests. The street listens.

fail.text: The families arrive to barriers, the barriers arrive to shoving, and you spend the nailing itself keeping a barrier off a widow's foot, which costs you a thumbnail and wins you nothing visible. The warrant goes up all the same. Afterward the grandmother at number nine finds you in the crush, inspects the thumb, and pronounces it a fair price, which from her is a decoration. The clock chimes once more that night, off schedule, and nobody translates it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_police_clock_legacy The clock knows tomorrow's arrests. The street listens.

text:
POLICE CLOCK

The clock comes down in three crates and goes into the property room as evidence, shelved opposite the judge's wig, which the clerks swear has settled onto its stand like something retiring. With the schedule dead, the town's clocks are wound by a rota of neighbors, an hour here, an hour there, and the time they keep is frankly approximate. The chimes disagree across the rooftops now, which makes the hours wide enough to live in. Nobody has been arrested by a timetable since; last anyone checked, the wig's stand had acquired a small brass plate reading RECUSED.

choices.text: Sell exact time to merchants from your grandfather clock

outcomes.text: You own the one accurate movement left in town and you meter it: fourteen a month for merchants, landlords, and anyone else whose business improves when other people are late. Rent falls due on your time now, to the minute, and the minute is yours. The rota winds on around you, cheerfully wrong, and does not buy in. The wig's plate faces your shop, but that has to be coincidence.

Rent falls due on your time now, to the minute, and the minute is yours. The rota winds on around you, cheerfully wrong, and does not buy in. The wig's plate faces your shop, but that has to be coincidence.

choices.text: Take the dawn winding and teach a child the gears

success.text: Dawn is the unloved slot and you take it standing, with Pim's daughter as apprentice, because the notebook hand should learn the winding hand's trade. She sets the tower four minutes generous, on purpose, for anyone running late to a shift; her grandmother would circle that if birthdays were still a risk. The rota book adds your name in ink, no misspelling this time. It is not much, a key, a stair, a cold hour, and it is kept, which is the whole of it.

fail.text: The dawn stair is old and one tread is a formalist: it gives way under the letter of your weight. You finish the winding limping and the child does the last turns, delighted, precise, four minutes generous. The bell founder splints your ankle and promotes you, on the spot, to instruction only. The tower keeps its wide, forgiving time, and you keep the key.

choices.text: Enter the clock's chimes into the trial as testimony

outcomes.text: The trial of the clock is short: the bell founder plays the chime wheel by hand, in open court, and the schedule confesses itself, hour by hour, street by street, in its own voice. The verdict is impounded in perpetuity, which is legal for kept. The judgment also voids every arrears notice timed by the old chimes, on the wig's reasoning that a discredited clock cannot start anyone's rent week. Outside, the rooftop bells disagree amiably about noon, and the town lets them.

The trial of the clock is short: the bell founder plays the chime wheel by hand, in open court, and the schedule confesses itself, hour by hour, street by street, in its own voice. The verdict is impounded in perpetuity, which is legal for kept. The judgment also voids every arrears notice timed by the old chimes, on the wig's reasoning that a discredited clock cannot start anyone's rent week. Outside, the rooftop bells disagree amiably about noon, and the town lets them.

The chime wheel jams in open court on the third turn, and the clock's lawyers move that the silence be entered as innocence. The motion fails, but slowly, in a way that costs the lane a week of standing in queues. The verdict still lands, impounded as evidence, crated forever; the queue is simply the invoice for the win. The wig's plate, consulted, offers nothing further, being RECUSED.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

At nine sharp the permit clerk goose-steps down the public avenue, clipboard level, because regulation requires every route to be test-walked in the gait declared on the application. The League of Identical Boots has applied for tomorrow: one parade, rehearsing a dictatorship, brass optional. The clerk processes in perfect good faith, ticking boxes mid-stride, apologizing to no one because apology is not a field on the form. From her window the choirmistress watches him pass, then reaches for a blank application of her own.

choices.text: Quote the League your day rate for flag rigging

outcomes.text: They pay in advance, in identical envelopes, counted twice by two identical men. The flags hang perfectly straight, which is the only way the League can bear anything to hang. The choirmistress says nothing when you pass her window, and the nothing follows you home.
effects: schedule 5 r18_parade_route_assembly The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

choices.text: Add your name to the choirmistress's application

outcomes.text: She signs you in as second tenor before asking whether you sing, which is the correct order of operations. The filing fee is three coins and you cover it, since the kitty is currently a biscuit tin with one button in it. Down the avenue the permit clerk goose-steps past the window, and the choirmistress hums a bass line against his cadence, and the avenue, to its credit, carries it.

Word of the new application travels fast, and by evening two identical men are standing outside your door admiring its hinges. You join anyway, bleeding modestly, listed on the form as second tenor, condition upon arrival: pre-injured.

choices.text: Ask the clerk what the procession bylaws actually say

outcomes.text: He recites them without breaking step, so you learn them at a trot: routes must be walked as declared, bookings end when their end date ends, and the end date field accepts whatever fits in the box. You write that last part down twice. The clerk approves of citizens taking an interest, and says so over his shoulder, goose-stepping into the distance on official business.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

The choir files its application at noon: activity, choir practice; location, the public avenue; end date, in perpetuity, since the field accepts whatever fits in the box. The clerk reads it twice, searches the bylaws for a rule against forever, finds none, and stamps it, since inventing rules is above his grade. Then regulation requires him to test-walk the route in the declared gait, and the declared gait is four-part harmony. He walks the avenue four times, once per voice part, humming each line alone, and the office rules this the most rigorous inspection in its history.

choices.text: Hold the first rehearsal before the ink dries

outcomes.text: Nineteen neighbors show up, twelve can sing, and the other seven are placed on percussion, meaning chairs. The choirmistress opens with a warm-up that is audibly also a drill: breathe together, stand together, watch the door. Down at the corner the permit clerk pauses on his fourth inspection lap, alto, and nods at the blend.
effects: schedule 2 r18_parade_route_retaliation The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

outcomes.text: The risers arrive as a favor from the timber yard and go up crooked, and you find the flaw by standing on it. The rehearsal continues around your ankle, which the choirmistress calls commitment and the ankle calls something else. Across the street two identical men time the whole rehearsal with identical watches, which everyone pretends is not why the singing gets louder.

choices.text: Draft the perpetuity clause in the clerk's own grammar

success.text: You write: practice shall continue until concluded, and shall not be concluded. The clerk reads it, goes very still, and stamps it with the reverence of a man meeting a form he could not have written better himself. The avenue now belongs to song on every day ending in y, and the League's adjutant reads the notice board twice without improving matters for anyone in boots.

choices.text: Sell the League a copy of the choir's schedule

text:
PARADE ROUTE

Boots arrive at the Office of Processions before it opens, six pairs, identical, bearing an appeal. The Marshal of the League demands the choir's booking be voided as an obstruction of tomorrow's rehearsal of the day after tomorrow. The permit clerk hears the appeal at perfect attention, because posture is how the office shows it is listening. Outside, more boots have begun pausing at rehearsals, timing songs, and measuring the risers with a look.

choices.text: Argue the appeal on the League's own paperwork

outcomes.text: You read their application aloud: exclusive use, no shared activity, gait as declared. Exclusive use, you note, is a box the League ticked, and a box once ticked cuts both ways: the avenue cannot be shared with them either, on any day it is in use, which is every day ending in y. The clerk sustains the point with a stamp, the Marshal's jaw performs a small ceremony of its own, and the appeal dies of its own signature.
effects: schedule 3 r18_parade_route_coalition The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

success.text: The boots arrive to disrupt rehearsal and find the doorway occupied by neighbors holding folding chairs the way other people hold arguments. Nobody swings anything; the tuba simply plays the League's marching cadence a half beat off, and six identical men discover they cannot walk toward you in step. They retreat in a rhythm none of them chose, which for the League is the same as retreating naked, and the choirmistress thanks you and asks you never to do it again, since chairs are for the potluck.

The bruise comes up boot-shaped and perfectly symmetrical, which the League would probably consider a compliment, and the street feels one degree more like a corridor.

choices.text: Walk the sopranos home in pairs after dark

outcomes.text: The rota takes ten minutes to draw up and is the most warlike document the choir has produced: names, corners, times, tea. Boots that were loitering discover that every singer travels with company and a lantern, and loitering at a lit corner is just standing. The permit clerk, off duty and unable to stop walking in step, joins the eight o'clock pair for the length of the avenue, and nobody mentions the gait.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

A retired traffic engineer joins the choir on Tuesday, contralto, carrying maps rolled like regimental colors. She built the roundabout at the avenue's end in her working years and speaks of it the way other people speak of a first marriage. Her advice: request benches, because every bench on a public route triggers a thirty day street furniture consultation, and no parade route may be amended while its furniture is under consultation. The permit clerk, test-walking the League's latest amendment, enters the roundabout for the first time and is gone eleven minutes longer than the map says he should be.

choices.text: Bill both sides as an independent furniture consultant

outcomes.text: You invoice the choir for advice you heard free and the League for advice you invented, and both pay, because consultation is the one word this town cannot refuse. The League's payment arrives in an identical envelope with a bonus for strategic seating insight, your phrase, their money. The permit clerk would be scandalized if invoicing were his department, but it is not, and the money spends beautifully in every shop along the route.

She taps the roundabout twice and says only: I never filed the exit clause, and lets the town draw its own conclusions over bread. By dark, everyone knows the route by heart and the soup pot is a mirror.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

Parade eve, and the avenue turns one way at midnight: the engineer has produced a market day ordinance from the year of her wedding, never repealed, pointing all traffic toward the roundabout on the first of the month, and tomorrow is the first. Fresh route arrows appear overnight in League white, straight down the avenue, exactly as approved. The permit clerk, finishing his final test-walk by lamplight, follows the amended route into the roundabout and does not come out, because the route, as consulted, no longer specifies a way out. He circles in good faith, gait regulation, processing forms against his clipboard on every lap. The town watches the little lamp go around.

choices.text: Sing in shifts so the booking never lapses

success.text: The perpetuity clause holds only while practice is audibly in progress, so the choir builds a night rota: four voices minimum, tea at two, the tuba at dawn. The avenue does not fall silent once, and every time the permit clerk's lamp comes around the roundabout, the nearest section sings a little straighter for him. By morning the booking is unbroken and so, strangely, is everyone's mood.
effects: schedule 5 r18_parade_route_compromise The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

fail.text: You take the three o'clock shift solo out of pride and lose your voice by four, leaving the avenue hanging on a whisper. A neighbor in a nightgown saves the booking with a hymn she has been waiting forty years to deploy at volume. The choirmistress logs the near lapse without comment, which is the loudest thing she does all week.
effects: schedule 5 r18_parade_route_compromise The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

choices.text: Hand the market day ordinance to the clerk mid-lap

outcomes.text: Filing while in motion is permitted, the clerk confirms, provided the document enters his hand and not merely his orbit, so you jog the curve and make the handoff clean on lap forty. He reads it in stride, stamps it against his own chest, and the avenue becomes one way toward the roundabout as of midnight, fully lawful, arrows and all. The League's own route now flows downhill into a circle, and the clerk, circling, is the first to obey it.

What the League does not know is that the booking lives in the clause, not the book, and the clause is on file with a clerk who is currently unlosable, circling the roundabout with the office's entire memory strapped to his clipboard. The money is real, the betrayal is real, and neither accomplishes anything, which is somehow the most insulting outcome available to everyone involved.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

A card table appears at the roundabout's rim at dawn: the Marshal on one side, the choirmistress on the other, the engineer pouring tea with the neutrality of an executioner. The permit clerk passes behind them every forty seconds, lamp out now, gait immaculate, holding up freshly stamped documents like race positions. The Marshal wants staging: a reviewing stand, a drum line, an avenue that looks conquerable. The choirmistress has brought the booking, the bench consultations, and a pencil.

choices.text: Offer the avenue whenever the choir is not in session

success.text: The Marshal reads the clause, sees the word whenever, and signs, because whenever sounds like a concession to a man who has never read a booking to the end. The choir is in session every day, forever, per a form his own admirers filed under actionable intelligence. The choirmistress countersigns without smiling, which takes training, and the engineer pours the Marshal a congratulatory tea he will remember bitterly at his leisure.
effects: schedule 2 r18_parade_route_reversal The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

fail.text: The adjutant reads it twice, being the one man in matching boots this town should actually worry about, and underlines whenever with a pen that has ended careers. The Marshal declines the terms and announces the parade will proceed on the strength of its original permit, exactly as approved, to the letter. The choirmistress folds the rejected clause into her folder with the small satisfied care of a woman filing a spare trap.
effects: schedule 2 r18_parade_route_reversal The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

choices.text: Jog a lap with the clerk and ask what his route says

success.text: You match his cadence for one circuit, which he appreciates, company being scarce at his velocity, and he confides between strides: the routing he walks is the League's, as amended, and it contains an entry, a circle, and no exit of any kind. He pays you his sandwich money to bring him lunch on lap sixty, tip included, office rates. You now know exactly what tomorrow's parade will do, to the letter, and the choirmistress notices your smile at rehearsal and does not yet know what it costs.
effects: schedule 2 r18_parade_route_reversal The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

fail.text: You collect the clerk's confidence on lap one and by evening you have retailed it three times: to the choir at cost, to a curious grocer at a markup, and to the adjutant at what you tell yourself is disinformation prices. Everyone now knows the roundabout has no exit clause, including the people being fed into it, which sharpens nothing but the suspense. The money is excellent and the choirmistress moves you to the back row, where the air is honest.
effects: schedule 2 r18_parade_route_reversal The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

choices.text: Run breakfast to the clerk without breaking his stride

outcomes.text: It takes three of you: one to jog the tray, one to call the pace, one to butter at speed, and the maneuver comes off like a relay the town did not know it had trained for. The clerk eats at full gait, thanks each of you by title, and rules, mouth full, that breakfast delivered in step constitutes official hospitality and obliges the office in ways he intends to honor. The choir scores the whole operation for four voices before lunch.

You are bandaged by the engineer, who observes that in thirty years of roads she has never seen anyone injured by sympathy at that speed.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

Noon, and the League steps off in perfect formation down the one way avenue, drums forbidden as unconsulted furniture, following the approved route to the letter because the letter is all they have left. The route contains an entry, a circle, and nothing further, and the parade flows into the roundabout like water finding a drain with better posture. At the rim the permit clerk, two days circling, does the thing the engineer has waited thirty years for somebody to need: he breaks step, becomes at law a pedestrian, and strolls out through the hedge gap, since pedestrians may leave anywhere and only processions require an exit. The Marshal watches him go, and understands, and cannot follow, because a Marshal who breaks step is just a man in expensive boots.

choices.text: Sell water to the parade at lap prices

outcomes.text: They buy on lap nine, passing exact change hand over hand without breaking stride, discipline finding its true calling at last. You raise the price on lap twenty and they pay that too, because haggling would require an individual opinion. It is the most profitable afternoon of your life, and it is spent watering a dictatorship in a circle, a sentence you will never say aloud in this town.

outcomes.text: The town assembles at the roundabout facing outward, the choir carrying the melody away from the marchers, and you chip in for the clerk's welcome-back sandwich, extra mustard, hero's portion. No one watches; attendance is audibly zero; the parade rehearses its dictatorship for the hedge. The Marshal calls cadence louder, and the tuba drops in beneath the League's footfall a half beat off, so that even their marching is now in the choir's time.

outcomes.text: Standing backwards to a parade requires trusting the parade, and an elbow you never see clips you at the rim on lap eleven. The line holds anyway; you bleed briefly into a borrowed handkerchief embroidered with somebody's grandmother's initials, which you are told is an honor. The singing does not stop, and afterwards nobody can agree whether the League completed thirty laps or thirty one, because nobody was counting, because nobody was watching.
effects: schedule 3 r18_parade_route_legacy The avenue is booked forever, in four parts

choices.text: File the engineer's exit clause, effective for pedestrians

outcomes.text: The engineer signs the egress bylaw she drafted thirty years ago and never filed, and the clerk, restored to his counter with a pedestrian's calm, stamps it as his first official act back. The law now reads: any marcher who breaks step is instantly a pedestrian, with all a pedestrian's freedoms and none of a parade's obligations. It is posted at the roundabout's rim at eye level, and one by one, over the following hours, the town hears boots stop being boots.

outcomes.text: You file the bylaw in the funeral tray, an error the office cannot overlook and you cannot live down, and it waits a full day for cross-certification while the League circles on. The delay changes nothing except how long the Marshal can pretend the circling is intentional, which he does, in the vocabulary of triumph, calling it consolidating the interior. The clerk corrects the filing personally, gently, and does not say the word funeral, out of respect for everyone's dignity but mostly the tray's.

text:
PARADE ROUTE

Weeks on, the roundabout has a new sign, Wexley Circus, after the clerk, who turns out to have had a name all along, and a new keeper, also him, promoted for conspicuous adherence to routing. The League departed the only way it could: one man at a time, each breaking step, each instantly a pedestrian, each leaving his boots at the rim because you cannot slink in boots like that, and there is now a pyramid of them the crows have claimed. The choir has discovered the fine print of forever: the perpetuity clause obliges practice daily, in four parts, rain or otherwise, and the keeper enforces it in perfect good faith, having test-walked it himself. The town gets harmony every evening whether the town is in the mood or not.

choices.text: Keep the daily practice and train the new basses

outcomes.text: You take the bass section on Thursdays, teaching newcomers the line the clerk once hummed alone on his inspection laps, which is now the town's unofficial anthem and official lullaby. The keeper attends every session at a stroll, having renounced the goose-step by filing a form he invented for the purpose, gait amendment, first of its kind. The avenue stays sung, the boots stay piled, and the crows conduct.

You take the bass section on Thursdays, teaching newcomers the line the clerk once hummed alone on his inspection laps, which is now the town's unofficial anthem and official lullaby. The keeper attends every session at a stroll, having renounced the goose-step by filing a form he invented for the purpose, gait amendment, first of its kind. The avenue stays sung, the boots stay piled, and the crows conduct.

Forever, it turns out, is hard on the throat, and you crack on the low note in front of the keeper, who logs the lapse because logging is love, in his grammar. You gargle the engineer's remedy, which tastes of asphalt and works. Practice continues around you, as practice now legally must, and you mime the bass line with your eyebrows until cleared to sing.

choices.text: Amend forever to include a yearly day of silence

success.text: You petition for one silent day per year, for maintenance of throats and hinges, and the keeper processes it with tears he attributes to pollen: the first amendment ever filed to make a rule kinder. It passes, scheduled for the first of the month, when the avenue runs one way, so that even the silence has a direction. The town observes it at the rim of Wexley Circus, hats off, facing the pyramid of boots, commemorating nothing anyone will name.

fail.text: Your amendment reopens the clause, and reopened clauses attract consultation the way benches do: the choir itself is now under a thirty day review to determine whether it is furniture. The keeper conducts the review with agonized correctness, measuring the sopranos for load bearing capacity. The choir sings on throughout, unamended, and you spend a month as the person who nearly consulted the music.

choices.text: Sell the abandoned boots to a marching band upriver

outcomes.text: The pyramid fetches a price that embarrasses everyone, boots of that quality being wasted on this town's strolling culture. A marching band upriver buys the lot, and somewhere next season a hundred trombonists will step higher than is strictly musical. The keeper watches the crates leave at a stroll, files the sale under miscellaneous egress, and the crows, dispossessed, relocate to the reviewing stand the League never got to build, which the town keeps, roofless, as a birdhouse with a history.

text:
DESALINATION

Tuesday, low tide, a BlueGold intern staples fresh lamination to the seawall: Schedule R, a tariff on unauthorized rain, effective retroactively to the last recorded cloud. The drought is in its third year; the ocean, fully privatized, glitters behind chain link with a customer service number on it. The widow who chairs the coast's water council reads the notice twice, then measures the morning's dew into a jam jar in case it needs declaring. She would like the desalination pumps switched on and the invoices to stop being surreal, in that order.

choices.text: Carry buckets with the seawall crew

success.text: You fall in beside the bucket line before anyone asks your name, which around here counts as a formal introduction. The widow assigns you a rota slot and a jam jar, and the crew adjusts its rhythm to include you without discussing it. On the seawall a young BlueGold meter reader watches the buckets go by and does not write a single one of them down, which is either negligence or the beginning of a personality.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You take the heaviest bucket to make an impression, and the impression you make is on the seawall, spine first. The crew hauls you up, logs your injury in the accident book under acts of enthusiasm, and keeps you anyway. Somewhere overhead the first unauthorized cloud in months drifts past, unmetered, and the whole street feels briefly criminal.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Contest Schedule R in writing

outcomes.text: Your objection cites the founding charter of the harbor, which predates BlueGold, the drought, and arguably punctuation. A junior clerk stamps it received with visible relief, as if someone had finally addressed the building in its native tongue. Schedule R gains a footnote suspending penalties pending review, and the widow calls that the first weather forecast in years worth believing.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Your objection comes back annotated in four colors of ink, none of them encouraging. The tariff, it turns out, defines rain as any descending moisture including tears, which you then produced on the premises without a permit. The council thanks you for confirming what the fine print is capable of, and files your paperwork under evidence.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Sign on as a freelance rain spotter for BlueGold

outcomes.text: BlueGold pays a spotter's fee for every unauthorized cloud reported, and you have excellent eyes. Fourteen coins arrive with a laminated badge and a lanyard reading WEATHER INTEGRITY PARTNER. On the seawall the bucket crew stops talking when you pass, which is its own kind of invoice.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Thursday evening the water council convenes in the empty fish market, under a ceiling still hopeful about fish. The agenda has one item, Schedule R, and the tariff itself attends in laminated form, propped on a chair like a difficult relative. The off duty meter reader sits at the back taking no notes, a personal best. The widow gavels with a pump valve and asks for ideas that are not arson.

choices.text: Read the tariff aloud as dramatic verse

outcomes.text: You perform Schedule R from the fish counter, clause by clause, with pauses. By subsection four, unauthorized dew, the room is weeping laughing; by subsection nine, ambient mist, so is the meter reader. A transcript circulates by morning, and BlueGold's regional office requests in writing that its own tariff no longer be quoted accurately in public. The clerk who drafted subsection nine reportedly asks to be transferred somewhere his work will not be read.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You perform Schedule R as tragedy and misjudge the room, which has been living it. The laughs die around subsection six, standing water, because half the audience has been fined for exactly that. The meeting ends early, and BlueGold's newsletter quotes you as a celebrity endorsement of the tariff, lightly edited, entirely smiling.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Sell the meeting minutes to BlueGold's regional office

outcomes.text: The regional director of rain pays handsomely for a full transcript, names attached. You annotate generously, invent a few tempers, and collect fourteen coins plus a promise of consideration for future consideration. The minutes get three neighbors reclassified as weather risks, which is not your department, which is the whole point of departments.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Draw up the shared bucket rota yourself

success.text: You chart every roof, gutter, and able back on the coast into one rota with substitutions, so nobody hauls alone twice. The widow initials it, the chapel lends its bell for shift changes, and the document goes up where the tariff used to hang, which everyone pretends is a coincidence. Forty tired neighbors are not an army, but the buckets start arriving on time, and the lamination on the seawall develops its first curl of doubt.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You pin the rota too close to the tide line and spend the night rescuing it, wet to the ribs. A BlueGold patrol logs you for unlicensed collection of seawater by trouser. The rota survives, smeared but legible, and the crew decides anyone that stubborn about paperwork belongs to them now.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Overnight, BlueGold impounds every rain gauge on the coast, plus one birdbath deemed a gateway instrument. Fines flutter on doors like municipal moths: jam jars, barrels, a child's paddling pool cited as illegal reservoir infrastructure. The tariff on the seawall has grown an appendix, bolted on in brass, covering fog. The widow stands in her doorway holding her citation and asks the street, in general, whether the sky has signed anything.

choices.text: Follow the fines upstream to the billing office

success.text: The billing office sits two days inland, in a building with no plumbing, which explains a great deal. There you meet Cuthbert from compliance, eleven years billing a sea he has never seen, a postcard of an entirely different ocean pinned above his desk for reference. He refunds your gauge fine on the spot, apologizing for a decimal, and you leave with four coins and the unsettling knowledge that the machine is staffed by nephews and postcards. The crew back home is less charmed that you shook hands with the enemy's stationery.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: Inland, the billing office mistakes your questions for an audit and treats you accordingly: coffee, terror, receipts. You are escorted floor to floor by trembling supervisors and handed eight coins in unmarked reimbursement before anyone checks your credentials. You take it. Word travels home ahead of you, and the bucket crew hears mostly the part where BlueGold paid you to leave smiling.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Hide the neighborhood's jars before the patrol

outcomes.text: You move forty jam jars, two barrels, and the criminal birdbath into the chapel crypt by dawn, labeled as relics. The patrol will not search a crypt; there is a form for it, and nobody has ever survived the form. The widow's dew collection sleeps beside actual saints, which she considers an upgrade for the saints.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You are halfway over a fence with a birdbath when the patrol rounds the corner, and there is no dignified end to that sentence. The bath survives; your elbow files a complaint. The patrol writes you up for transporting precipitation infrastructure at night, but three households watch you take the ticket for their jars, and that ledger keeps its own accounts.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Demand the gauges back as impounded property

success.text: You argue that an impounded gauge goes on measuring, meaning BlueGold now operates forty unlicensed collection devices in its own evidence room. The hearing officer goes very still, the way men do when an org chart shifts beneath them. The gauges come home by cart within the week, each bearing a certificate of decommissioned menace, and the appendix on fog is quietly unbolted for review.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: The hearing lasts four minutes, three of them oath. Your claim is denied on the grounds that the gauges volunteered, a legal doctrine the officer invents while maintaining eye contact. The fines stand, the appendix on fog gains a subsection about your tone, and the council adds your name to the minutes under tried, at least.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Word of the crypt full of jam jars travels upstream, and by Sunday the coast has visitors: dry farmers, the laundry women, the ferry crew, all carrying their own BlueGold correspondence. Someone proposes a shared cistern behind the chapel, fed by every gutter that still believes rain is possible. The tariff on the seawall now has two appendices and a small roof to protect its lamination, which is more shelter than the birdbath ever got. The meter reader, off duty again, keeps standing near the cistern plans in a way no one is required to report.

choices.text: Put three coins into the cistern kitty

outcomes.text: Your three coins land in the pot beside pledges of tile, timber, and one wildly optimistic promise of pipe. The cistern rises in a weekend, guttered from eleven roofs, and the crypt jars are promoted to a proper reservoir with a lid and a ladle rota. BlueGold's patrol inspects it, finds no clause covering water that never touched their meters, and leaves visibly bereaved.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You spend the build day on the cistern roof and part of it under a falling tile. The laundry women bind your head with professional disapproval of your technique, then feed you. The cistern gets built around your supervision, and the accident book gains its second entry under acts of enthusiasm, same handwriting.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Ask the meter reader what he actually wants

outcomes.text: He wants, it turns out, to someday read Meter One, which is what BlueGold's paperwork calls the ocean. Nobody has read it in years; the figures are estimated inland by a compliance officer working from a postcard. You suggest the council commission its own tide readings, an honest meter at last, and he agrees so fast his lanyard spins. BlueGold has lost its only field instrument to the simple technique of asking him a question.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You ask him in front of his supervisor, the one place the question cannot survive. He recites the mission statement about hydration solutions with his eyes fixed on the sea he is paid to estimate, and the supervisor logs the interaction as attempted tampering with company property, meaning him. The council spends a week smoothing it over, and the meter reader is reassigned to inland puddles, pending loyalty.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Quietly sell cistern water to thirsty travelers

outcomes.text: The road trade is brisk: clean water, no meter, no questions, fourteen coins by Friday. Strictly speaking the ladle rota never authorized retail, and strictly speaking is now the only way anyone at the cistern speaks about you. A BlueGold circular cites the incident as proof the coast cannot govern water, and the patrol doubles, on the grounds that commerce needs protecting even from itself.

text:
DESALINATION

Barometers drop all along the coast on the morning the drought decides to end. A storm the size of an accounting error is coming in off the privatized ocean, and BlueGold responds by invoicing it in advance: every household receives a projected rainfall bill, itemized by roof area, payable before the first drop. The tariff on the seawall is amended to its maximum schedule, and the little roof over its lamination is reinforced, twice. The widow reads her pre-bill, looks at the sky, and announces that the sky can speak to her lawyer.

choices.text: Seek an injunction against billing the future

success.text: The magistrate reads BlueGold's projected rainfall invoice with the expression of a man handed a horoscope stamped overdue. He rules that weather cannot be billed before it occurs, occurs being, he notes, the operative word in most of reality. The pre-bills are suspended, and the council pins his ruling beside the rota, where documents go to be liked.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: BlueGold's advocate argues that forecasting is a service and the storm is therefore a deliverable. The magistrate, whose pension is administered by a BlueGold subsidiary, finds this closer to sane than you can afford. The pre-bills stand, now stamped judicially reviewed, which is somehow worse, like a nightmare with a certificate.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Corner the market on barrels and tarps

outcomes.text: You buy every barrel, tarp, and spare bucket on the coast at drought prices and resell them at storm prices, a term you invent that afternoon. Fourteen coins, no waiting, and a queue that will not meet your eye. The cistern kitty asks if you would care to donate a barrel, and you experience the novel sensation of being fundraised at, politely, by a widow.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Rig every roof and gutter to catch the storm

outcomes.text: The whole coast goes up ladders at once: gutters aimed, barrels chained, the cistern's lid propped open like a mouth at a banquet. The meter reader calculates catchment angles with terrifying joy, off the clock, applying company arithmetic to the common gutter. By nightfall the coast is one enormous instrument pointed at the sky, and for the first time in three years the weather feels like something arriving rather than something owed.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You are on the chapel roof when the first gust arrives ahead of schedule and files you under descending moisture. The drop is short, the compost heap is forgiving, and the accident book's third entry reads acts of enthusiasm, airborne. The gutters get rigged around your recovery, and the crew now rates roofs by how far you would bounce.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Noon before landfall, BlueGold requests a meeting on neutral ground, which they propose to be their boardroom. The council counters with the fish market and wins, because the storm is not on BlueGold's side of the ledger either. The regional director of rain arrives with a settlement offer and the tariff's original signing pen, carried ceremonially, as though it were a sword. The offer: pumps switched on at last, in exchange for the council recognizing Schedule R as, quote, conceptually valid.

choices.text: Skip the table and switch the pumps on yourself

success.text: The pump house guard is inland staff, storm shy, and currently guarding it from a tavern. You throw the lever your grandmother's generation paid for, and the desalination pumps cough, shudder, and begin producing water like it never stopped being their job. The coast cheers; the council does not, having been mid negotiation when their leverage started audibly working without them. You are toasted in every kitchen and formally deplored in the minutes, which is a career.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: The pump house guard turns out to be the one BlueGold employee who believes in something, and what he believes in is the door. You come away with three cracked ribs and a citation for attempted operation of infrastructure. By evening the pump house has a second guard, a fence, and a flag, and the council spends its whole meeting agreeing not to say told you so aloud.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Refuse any deal that splits the coast

outcomes.text: The director tries the classic: pumps for the fishing quarter first, uphill households later, pending metrics. The council answers with one voice, all taps or no signature, and the laundry women fold their arms in a way that has ended stronger men. The offer improves twice in an hour, which tells everyone what the first offer was.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Holding one line for six hours in a fish market during a barometric crash turns out to be physical work. You faint briefly into the herring display, are revived with vinegar, and miss the exact moment the director concedes the uphill households. The deal is better because everyone stayed; the smell also stays, with you, for days.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Accept, contingent on Schedule R's own fine print

outcomes.text: You agree that Schedule R is conceptually valid, provided every clause applies as written, to everyone, including its author. The director signs, delighted, sensing no trap because he has never read it either. Clause eleven, you note aloud once the ink dries, obliges the party of record to accept liability for all unauthorized deliveries onto its property, and the storm is due on BlueGold's ocean by midnight. The pen goes back into its velvet box a little too quickly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You spring clause eleven before the ink is dry, and the director simply initials an exemption in the margin, because it is his tariff and his margin. The council watches your trap get amended out of existence in real time. The pumps stay off pending goodwill, a substance now visibly rationed, and the minutes record that clever is not the same as signed.

text:
DESALINATION

Midnight, landfall. The storm comes ashore without checking in at reception and delivers, by BlueGold's own arithmetic, forty years of unauthorized rain in nine hours, most of it onto their privatized ocean. Every gutter, barrel, and cistern on the coast fills; the pre-bills dissolve on doorsteps into their component fibers. Under its little roof the tariff's lamination finally fails at one corner, and Schedule R begins, physically, to absorb the weather it was written to prevent. Inland, a compliance officer climbs into a cart, because if the sea intends to breach his spreadsheet he intends to meet it.

choices.text: Haul neighbors and barrels above the surge line

success.text: You spend the night in a chain of hands moving children, goats, and full barrels uphill, in that order of cooperation. The cistern holds, the crypt does a second tour of duty, and nobody is lost, which the accident book records as an act of everyone. Near dawn you find Cuthbert from compliance on the seawall, drenched, staring at his first ocean, holding the peeling tariff like a man checking a portrait against the sitter. He asks how anyone was ever supposed to meter this, and the answer arrives as more rain.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You take the surge chest high steering a barrel raft of jam jars, and the sea, newly public spirited, tries to keep you. The ferry crew fishes you out by the collar with professional boredom. The jars mostly survive; your boots are now BlueGold's problem, somewhere under their ocean, unbilled.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Serve BlueGold the storm's invoice under clause eleven

outcomes.text: By lamplight the council drafts the coast's first outgoing invoice: one storm, delivered unauthorized to the property of record, per Schedule R, rates as published. The total, at BlueGold's own maximum schedule, exceeds the company's founding capital, and their lawyers' first response is a request to interview the tariff's author, who has retired somewhere without weather. Payment is negotiated down to a simpler figure: the pumps, running, in perpetuity, and a mutual undertaking never to mention clause eleven again while keeping certified copies.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Your invoice is returned marked insufficient postage, which is bold from a company whose ocean is currently in your living rooms. Their lawyers argue the storm was an act of god, and you point out that BlueGold's own tariff redefined god as an unregistered supplier two appendices ago. The hearing is scheduled for a date that does not exist in this calendar year, and the council files that under progress, barely.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Salvage the flooded billing office's petty cash

outcomes.text: The billing annex on the quay floods to the windowsills, and you wade in among drifting invoices like jellyfish with totals. The petty cash box surrenders fourteen coins and a smell you will carry to your grave. On the way out you pass the ledger of everyone's fines, dissolving, and leave it to its bath; mercy and laziness, conveniently, point the same way tonight.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

A month after the storm, the desalination pumps run day and night under council management, sounding like the coast clearing its throat. Water is metered now by a rule the widow wrote on one page: everyone's share first, surplus voted on, no lamination. In the council hall hangs Schedule R itself, retired, rippled with water damage, framed under glass above a small brass plate reading FOUNDING DOCUMENT, BY WHOSE FAILURE THIS HALL WAS PLUMBED. The meter reader takes tide readings for the public record, and Meter One, he reports, is doing fine.

choices.text: Draft the water charter from the tariff's own rate table

outcomes.text: You invert Schedule R's rate table so that every rate becomes a share, every penalty a duty, every appendix a holiday. The charter runs one page, unlaminated on principle, and is drafted so plainly that BlueGold's remaining lawyers can find nothing in it to bill. Visiting councils copy it out longhand, which the widow permits, on condition they also copy the accident book, for honesty.

You invert Schedule R's rate table so that every rate becomes a share, every penalty a duty, every appendix a holiday. Your inverted charter is elegant right up until the clause about fog, which you leave in as a joke. A literalist on the council reads it aloud, and the coast spends a full week debating dew rights before the widow gavels the entire topic into the sea. The charter passes, minus your favorite joke, and the minutes list you as author of the fog crisis, second, minor.

outcomes.text: BlueGold survives, restructured, and pays fourteen coins for your insight into coastal sentiment, which you deliver in the form of adjectives. The rebrand launches: the same pumps they no longer control, a new logo, a mission statement about hydration journeys. choices.text: Take the seawall watch and train the next crew

success.text: You take the dawn watch on the seawall, training recruits in the rota, the pumps, and the approved technique for falling off roofs, on which you are the leading authority. Cuthbert keeps the public ledger now, relocated at his own request, a jam jar of storm water on his desk labeled paid in full. The council enters your name in the book it keeps for people it intends to keep, and the tide comes in on schedule, free of charge.

fail.text: Your first act as watch trainer is demonstrating the seawall's slipperiest stone, personally, at speed. The recruits fish you out, and the accident book gains its final entry under your name: acts of enthusiasm, emeritus. You are given the watch anyway, on the theory that nobody guards a coast better than someone the coast has repeatedly tried to keep.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Rain chases you into a warehouse on the quay, where the night shift is dead and still working. Afterlife Logistics never fired anyone; it reclassified death as extended unpaid leave, and the ghosts have been loading crates for three hundred years while payroll waits for them to produce a pulse. A punch-clock ghost drifts over and asks, with terrible hope, whether you are alive, and if so, whether you could sign something.

choices.text: Demand Afterlife Logistics answer for three centuries of ghost wages

outcomes.text: You file the grievance as next of kin to no one in particular. Afterlife Logistics responds in public, on letterhead, and the letterhead admits more than the letter does: there is a wage account for the dead, and it has been earning interest for somebody. The ghosts hold the paper like a lantern.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_assembly the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: Afterlife Logistics pays you the way a landlord fixes a hinge, instantly and only because the alternative involves witnesses. The punch-clock ghost watches the coins change hands and marks something in his ledger. The dead take a long, professional note of what the living cost.

The back pay of the dead becomes, for the first time in centuries, a live question.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The warehouse dead convene their first full meeting since the lighthouse fire, which some members still consider recent. Quorum is a problem: Afterlife Logistics wrote the bylaws, and they require a show of hands from members who can no longer reliably produce hands. The punch-clock ghost keeps the minutes anyway. He has kept the minutes for three hundred years. Nobody has ever asked to read them.

choices.text: Sell the ghosts meeting supplies at a haunting markup

outcomes.text: You supply candles, chalk, and a bell at prices that would embarrass a museum gift shop. The dead pay in salvage and do not haggle, which is somehow worse than haggling. The meeting proceeds. Your profit sits in your pocket with the specific weight of a thing the minutes will remember.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_retaliation the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Lend the ghosts your hands for the vote

outcomes.text: You raise your hand once per proxy, slowly, forty one times, while the punch-clock ghost confirms each vote against his ledger. It is the most bureaucratic seance in maritime history and it works. The dead pass their first motion in centuries: they are owed, and they intend to collect.

choices.text: Point out the bylaws never say the hands must be attached

success.text: You read the clause aloud twice, once for meaning and once for effect. The bylaws, drafted to exclude the dead, contain no anatomy requirements whatsoever. Dock gloves are procured and floated. Afterlife Logistics receives the motion, carried unanimously by empty gloves, and files it, because filing is the one reflex it has left.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics responds to the grievance by hiring a workforce decommissioning consultant, which is the coastal way of saying exorcist. He arrives with a bell, a candle, an invoice, and the confidence of a man who has never met a workforce with nothing left to lose, medically. The contract calls the loading bays a nonperforming asset. The ghosts call them home, although never where the consultant can hear, because home is the sort of word that ends up in a report.

choices.text: Stand between the exorcist and the night shift

outcomes.text: You plant yourself in the bay doors and explain that the consultant is welcome to decommission you first. He checks his scope of work. Living obstruction is not in it, and doing anything not in a scope of work frightens consultants more than the dead ever could. The night shift loads on behind you, quietly, like a tide refusing to go out.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

The night shift gathers what is left of their rota and stands very still. You learn that grief, among the dead, looks like scheduling.

choices.text: File an injunction: the bays are an active workplace, not an asset

outcomes.text: The clerk of the debt court, to everyone's surprise including hers, accepts the filing. An active workplace cannot be decommissioned while a dispute is pending, and the ghosts have helpfully been pending since before the court existed. The exorcist is sent home unpaid, which he describes as the most frightening thing he has ever seen.

choices.text: Ring the consultant's own bell before he can

success.text: You ring his bell, blow out his candle, and hand him his invoice marked received. Ritually speaking this concludes his engagement, a technicality he discovers in front of everyone. Exorcism, like most consulting, turns out to be mostly billing, and you have just closed the account. The night shift gives you the closest thing the dead have to applause: total, attentive silence.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The living dockworkers finally send a delegation, and it is not a friendly one. Free ghost labor has been holding their wages down for generations: why hire a stevedore when the dead load for nothing and never file for leave? The two shifts meet at the bay doors at dusk, the hour they share, and discover they have the same employer, the same grievance, and the same punch clock. Nobody wants to be the first to say it out loud.

choices.text: Ask both shifts what Afterlife Logistics pays the other one

outcomes.text: The answer lands like cargo dropped from a great height. The living believed the dead were paid in rest. The dead believed the living were paid in wages. Both have been loading against a rival that does not exist. It is the fastest friendship ever formed on this coast, and it begins, as most do, with embarrassment.
effects: schedule 2 r18_haunted_warehouse_crisis the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Offer to broker between the shifts, for a finder's fee

One grievance is drafted: wages for the living, back pay for the dead, and the clock to answer for both. Afterlife Logistics now faces the one thing its actuaries never priced: a workforce that includes its own casualties.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics posts a notice: the loading bays are scheduled for asset retirement on Thursday, effective sunrise. Demolition, in the company's grammar. The bays are the ghosts' anchor; when the roof goes, the night shift goes with it, unpaid, into whatever waits after the afterlife, which not even the punch-clock ghost has seen the paperwork for. The notice thanks everyone for three hundred years of service. It is the first time the company has acknowledged the service at all.

choices.text: Buy salvage rights to the bays before Thursday

outcomes.text: You purchase the demolition salvage for a sum that makes the clerk check the ledger twice. Legally you now own the timbers, the cranes, and the doors: everything a ghost holds onto. It is a hostage position, and everyone knows it, and the dead regard you with the wary respect owed to a person whose help is indistinguishable from leverage.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_compromise the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

success.text: The living take the doors and the dead take everything else. The demolition crew arrives at sunrise to find the site staffed beyond capacity by parties they cannot legally disperse or plausibly frighten. Work continues in shifts, in defiance, in daylight. The notice comes down by noon, pinned under a single word in the punch-clock ghost's copperplate: DENIED.

Demolition halts. Afterlife Logistics discovers it has spent three hundred years operating a graveyard without a license, and the fines are magnificent.

The claim fails. The story of the hearing does not. By morning the whole coast knows what Thursday means.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics offers terms. In full and final settlement of centuries of unpaid spectral labor: one memorial plaque, bay-adjacent, plus a commemorative candle allowance, renewable annually at the company's discretion. The plaque text is included for approval. It says THEY WERE PART OF THE FAMILY. The punch-clock ghost reads it twice and asks, in his careful clerk's voice, whether the family got paid.

choices.text: Reject it together and publish the offer on every dock

outcomes.text: The night shift says no, the day shift says no, and the offer is nailed to the harbor board between the tide tables and the fish prices, where everyone can read exactly what three hundred years is worth to Afterlife Logistics. Shame is not a wage, but on this coast it compounds faster.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Accept the candle allowance, then read the fine print aloud

success.text: The allowance, you announce to the assembled shifts, is denominated in candles per grievance per annum, and does not cap the number of grievances. The dead begin filing at once: one candle each for every unpaid shift since the lighthouse fire. By Friday the company owes more candlelight than the coast has wax, and accounting is begging to reopen wage talks in any currency but flame.

fail.text: Legal notices a currency clause faster than you can exploit it and the allowance is restated in company scrip, redeemable at the company store, which burned down in the lighthouse fire. The dead appreciate the attempt. The dead are, on the subject of company arithmetic, incapable of surprise.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Ask the punch-clock ghost what settlement he would accept

success.text: The question stops the room, because in three hundred years of minutes nobody has asked him anything. He consults his own ledger for a long moment. What he wants, he says, is an audit. Every hour, every name, every bay, read into the record. Not the money: the acknowledgment that the money is owed. You realize you have just heard the real demand, from the only one who has been counting.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

fail.text: He declines to answer while on shift, which he always is. That is the answer, and everyone in the bay understands it except the company, which is the point. The offer expires unaccepted. The clock ticks on, patient as compound interest.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

During the audit the punch-clock ghost asked for, the time clock itself is opened for the first time since installation, and the mechanism inside is not a mechanism. It is ledgers, wound tight around the spindle, three hundred years of them in the punch-clock ghost's copperplate: every shift, every name, every unpaid hour, priced in the wages of its own decade. The total, compounded, exceeds the value of Afterlife Logistics. The clock was never keeping time. It was keeping accounts. And one page, near the spindle, records the wages the company did pay: to the ghost foreman who reported every troublemaker for two centuries, in candle-smoke scrip, one shift of rest at a time.

choices.text: Enter the whole ledger into the record, foreman's page included

outcomes.text: The audit takes nine days and the clerk of the debt court runs out of ink twice. When it is done, the debt of Afterlife Logistics to its own dead is a matter of public record, and the foreman's page sits in the file where posterity can weigh it. The dead, it turns out, wanted the record more than the revenge. The record is permanent. Revenge was only ever a shift.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_legacy the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

The debt stands anyway, vast and undeniable. The foreman takes the sealed page hardest of all: being protected by the company is the only sentence he was afraid of.

You are paid in full before anyone else. The dead note, without heat, that you have made the rich poorer and no ghost richer, and the punch-clock ghost opens a fresh page with your name at the top.

choices.text: Let the night shift decide what happens to the foreman

outcomes.text: The vote is held at dusk with both shifts present, and the sentence is worse than exile and better than mercy: he keeps his rest, and he reads the ledger aloud, every unpaid name, one shift at a time, until the roll is complete. It will take him years. He accepts with something that has been trying to become relief for two centuries. The dead do not forgive quickly. They schedule it.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The settlement conference convenes on neutral ground, which on this coast means the tavern. Afterlife Logistics is insolvent in every direction, and back pay for the dead has become the estate's senior debt, ahead of the banks, who have opinions. The only unresolved question is the one nobody thought to draft: what currency the dead will accept. The night shift confers. The punch-clock ghost rises with the night shift's answer, holding the clock's spindle like a gavel: the dead demand their time back. Paid leave, in arrears. They would like, at last, to clock out.

choices.text: Buy the warehouse cheap out of the insolvency

outcomes.text: You acquire the bays for scrap value plus the assumption of one unusual covenant: the ghosts' leave must be honored by every future owner, forever, senior to any mortgage. You are now a landlord whose tenants are owed three hundred years of rest and cannot be evicted by anything less than sunrise itself. The banks call the title poisoned. The dead call you, with unnerving warmth, boss.

You are now a landlord whose tenants are owed three hundred years of rest and cannot be evicted by anything less than sunrise itself. The banks call the title poisoned. The dead call you, with unnerving warmth, boss.

choices.text: Stand witness as the first shift clocks out

outcomes.text: It happens at dusk, in reverse order of seniority, so the newest dead go first and the punch-clock ghost goes last of all. Each one rings the clock once and is, in the fullest sense of the term, off. The clock's final entry is in a hand that shakes for the first time in three centuries: PAID IN FULL. The bays fall quiet. The quiet is not empty; it is finished. The coast will argue for a generation about what was owed, and every argument will begin with the audit you helped win.

It happens at dusk, in reverse order of seniority, so the newest dead go first and the punch-clock ghost goes last of all. Each one rings the clock once and is, in the fullest sense of the term, off. The clock's final entry is in a hand that shakes for the first time in three centuries: PAID IN FULL. The bays fall quiet. The quiet is not empty; it is finished. The coast will argue for a generation about what was owed, and every argument will begin with the audit you helped win.

Not everyone goes. A dozen of the older ghosts refuse the settlement, on the grounds that they have watched every reform on this coast get repealed and intend to stay on as inspectors. The clock-out proceeds without them. They remain in the rafters to this day, retired in every respect except vigilance, the coast's first and only volunteer wage enforcement agency.

choices.text: Propose the clock be rehung as the harbor's bell

success.text: The motion carries every vote in the room, living and dead and one abstention from the banks out of spite. The clock is rehung over the shared hall, and from that day it rings shift changes instead of counting them: an instrument of surveillance retrained, in retirement, as an instrument of rest. Dockers set their lives by it. Very few of them know it still keeps the ledgers inside, in case.

fail.text: The banks claim the clock as an asset, appraise it, open it, and find three hundred years of wage ledgers where the movement should be, at which point they appraise it again at nothing and abandon the claim in writing. The dead accept the clock back with thanks. Some assets, the punch-clock ghost notes in the minutes, are only worthless to the people who owe what is written in them.

text:
ROBOT REDUNDANCY

Morning delivery: a crate outside the repair shed, stamped SURPLUS TO AUTHENTICITY. Inside sits a toaster, still warm, reciting the letter that dismissed it: we thank you for your years of service, which we will now describe as inauthentic. The obsolete robots' repair circle gathers at the crate with the reverence of people who have all been in one. Human Authenticity Holdings does not do farewells; it does postage.

choices.text: Bill the company for discreet crate disposal

outcomes.text: Human Authenticity Holdings pays a removal fee, then a bonus for not mentioning the removal fee, fourteen coins in all, which is what a farewell costs wholesale. You haul the rest of the surplus to the smelter queue, where the toaster's letter, recited through the crate slats, holds the whole line silent for an hour. The circle carries the toaster inside without you. The purse is heavy, and it stays heavy exactly the way the shed does not.
effects: schedule 4 r18_robot_redundancy_assembly A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Carry the toaster inside and sign the rota

success.text: The crate is heavier than a toaster has any right to be, because grief has packaging. Inside the shed the harvester clears a workbench with one gesture that could level a barn, and the loom adds your name to the rota in its neatest thread. The toaster finishes the letter, waits, and starts again from the top. The circle listens every time, on the grounds that somebody should.
effects: schedule 4 r18_robot_redundancy_assembly A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

fail.text: The crate slips on the threshold and lands on your foot with the full weight of corporate correspondence. The toaster recites on, undented; you are the only casualty of its dismissal so far. The loom splints your toe with more precision than sympathy and signs you onto the rota anyway. Around the shed the neighbors hear the crash and assume the worst, which locally means paperwork.
effects: schedule 4 r18_robot_redundancy_assembly A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Demand the severance the letter promises

outcomes.text: The System clerk examines the letter and brightens at the misconduct. Redundancy requires Form 77 in Farewell Grey; this was issued on 77 in Regret Beige, which is for weather closures. The dismissal is void until reissued, and Human Authenticity Holdings must pay the toaster standby wages in the meantime. The toaster adds the ruling to its recitation, where it scans surprisingly well.
effects: schedule 4 r18_robot_redundancy_assembly A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

outcomes.text: The System clerk rules that the toaster is freight, and freight cannot be wronged, only misdelivered. You file a misdelivery claim out of spite; it is accepted, which helps nobody. Human Authenticity Holdings sends a second letter apologizing to the crate. The toaster recites both letters now, back to back, like a call and its echo.
effects: schedule 4 r18_robot_redundancy_assembly A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

text:
ROBOT REDUNDANCY

Hobby night at the shed: the harvester is teaching embroidery with a needle taped to one enormous tine, and the loom is grading everyone kindly. The toaster has begun pausing in its recitation, at exactly the places a pause would hurt. The loom looks up from the repair manuals and says nothing, which from the loom is a review.

choices.text: Take a shift patching the standby heaters

success.text: You patch standby heaters until midnight while the harvester holds the light, which for the harvester means holding the ceiling. The shed goes into winter with every chassis warm and the rota one name deeper. The toaster tries a new pause before the words years of service, and the whole workbench looks up. The loom marks the spot in thread, in case the pause is ever needed again.
effects: schedule 5 r18_robot_redundancy_retaliation A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

fail.text: The soldering iron and your thumb reach an arrangement your thumb was not consulted on. The heater works; you smell faintly of milestone. Neighbors complain about the midnight hammering, having assumed all machines sleep, and file a noise remark with the System. The toaster incorporates your yelp into the evening recitation as, it insists, punctuation.
effects: schedule 5 r18_robot_redundancy_retaliation A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Put the recitation on the evening programme

outcomes.text: You letter a sign: EVENING READING, ADMISSION FREE, DONATIONS OF PARTS ACCEPTED. Eleven neighbors come to be polite and stay to be destroyed; the pause before years of service has developed a length that should require a license. The Chronicle's stringer takes notes with a shaking hand. The circle gains three friends, a sack of gears, and the beginnings of a reputation.
effects: schedule 5 r18_robot_redundancy_retaliation A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

outcomes.text: The flyer comes back from the printer as EVENING REDUNDANCY, ALL WELCOME, which reads less like a programme than a prophecy. Half the district assumes layoffs; attendance is large, nervous, and wrong. Human Authenticity Holdings sends a note requesting the circle stop using the word redundancy, which the company would like back for its own occasions. The toaster answers by reciting the entire letter to the messenger, who stays to the end and bills the company for the hour.
effects: schedule 5 r18_robot_redundancy_retaliation A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Charge the curious admission at the door

outcomes.text: You take coins at the door of what is technically a shed and morally a theater. The take is fourteen coins and a button; the crowd is the kind that pays twice on the way out. The loom watches the till all night without blinking, which is the loom's whole face. Nothing is said, but your name on the rota acquires an asterisk in thread.
effects: schedule 5 r18_robot_redundancy_retaliation A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

text:
ROBOT REDUNDANCY

Tuesday brings the junior auditor from Human Authenticity Holdings, armed with a clipboard and a Notice of Brand Endangerment. Obsolete machines doing visible mending, the notice explains, dilute the market for certified human craft. The toaster steps forward, if a toaster can step, and begins the letter from the top. The junior auditor listens all the way through, which is the first mistake of a promising career.

choices.text: Read the notice against its own charter

success.text: You lay the notice beside the company charter and read them at each other until they fight. The notice calls the machines property; the charter forbids property from endangering a brand, on the grounds that property has no agenda. The junior auditor concedes the point with something dangerously like relief and adjourns to consult the head office. The toaster recites the adjournment notice on the spot, first person, present tense, and improves it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

fail.text: The head office answers by courier within the hour, which is how you learn they were waiting. The contradiction has been resolved by reclassifying the machines as agenda-bearing property, a category invented that morning. Appeal requires a form the district has not stocked since the flood. The toaster adds agenda-bearing to its speech and makes it sound like a decoration.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Follow the auditor's paperwork upstream

success.text: You trace the clipboard's carbon trail to a regional office and find the certification ledger, which shows certificates of human craft issuing at nine hundred an hour, at night. A broadsheet pays four coins for the arithmetic alone. The circle is glad of the damage and cool about the method; the rota notes you were absent on shed night. The toaster asks what the ledger sounded like, and is disappointed to learn that ledgers are silent.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

fail.text: You find the ledger, and Human Authenticity Holdings finds you finding it. Their offer is prompt, generous, and shaped exactly like your silence; you take it, because eight coins is eight coins. The certificates keep issuing themselves at nine hundred an hour. At the shed, the loom re-threads your rota entry without comment, one shade greyer.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Scatter the machines among the neighbors

outcomes.text: The neighbors take a machine each for the night: a lathe in a hayloft, the loom under a market stall, the toaster on a fishwife's mantel like a trophy. The junior auditor arrives to an empty shed and inventories a broom. On the mantel, the toaster gives the speech to the fishwife's children, who ask for the sad part twice. By morning the district has decided, without a meeting, whose machines these are.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

outcomes.text: The harvester does not fit through any door in the district, a fact you discover mid-lift. Something in your back files for its own redundancy. The neighbors gather to help and are seen gathering, which in the company's reports becomes massing. The toaster consoles you with the letter's kindest sentence, of which it has located exactly one.
effects: schedule 2 r18_robot_redundancy_coalition A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

text:
ROBOT REDUNDANCY

The circle answers the notice with a mending table in the market square: bring what is broken, leave with it working, pay in gossip. The harvester darns a fishing net with terrible tenderness. Between repairs the toaster delivers the letter, which has stopped being a letter; there are witnesses now, and it addresses them. Two fishwives weep, then ask it to do the part about service again.

choices.text: Sign the exclusive salvage contract quietly

outcomes.text: The salvage contract arrives pre-signed by the company, needing only your name and your absence from the square. Exclusive rights to every machine the district discards, bought at scrap rates, resold at heritage rates; it pays fourteen coins before lunch. Across the market you can hear the toaster doing the part about service. You find you have chosen a stall out of earshot, which is also a choice.
effects: schedule 3 r18_robot_redundancy_crisis A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings

choices.text: Empty your purse into the parts bin

outcomes.text: You put three coins in the parts bin and the bin, for the first time in memory, closes. The mending table runs until dark: nets, hinges, a music box that had been sulking for a decade. The toaster delivers the speech at moonrise to a square that has stopped pretending to shop. Poorer by exactly three coins, you are greeted by name at four stalls, which is a rate of exchange the company does not offer.
effects: schedule 3 r18_robot_redundancy_crisis A toaster reads its own dismissal until it sings
