outcomes.text: Your definitions page is accepted, then quietly redefined by a further page defining your page as advisory. There is always a further page; DryLand employs a man whose entire career is further pages. The boats do not blame you, aloud, but the biscuit tin minutes acquire a new heading titled lessons.
effects: schedule 3 r18_flood_housing_reversal The landlord has invoiced the tide

choices.text: Tow the shelter fee booth off the hill by night

success.text: Six boats, one long rope, and the fee booth comes off its blocks with a squawk and travels downhill in a manner the gazette will describe as nautical. By morning it is moored in the channel, repainted as the toll booth for the tide, admission one apology. Forty people fail to claim responsibility in suspiciously identical wording, which the boats find hilarious, DryLand finds legally useless, and the rota chair finds worth noting, since nobody asked the rota.
effects: schedule 3 r18_flood_housing_reversal The landlord has invoiced the tide

fail.text: The rope holds, the blocks hold, and the bailiffs DryLand hired on Thursday turn out to hold as well, four of them, in a patrol punt with truncheon budgets. You spend a bad three minutes as the only reachable tenant and take a professional going over before the coal barge arrives like a settlement offer made of hull. The fee booth stays; your ribs concede the hill.
effects: schedule 3 r18_flood_housing_reversal The landlord has invoiced the tide

text:
FLOOD HOUSING

Monday, and the grandmother on the coal barge empties her receipt tin onto the pantry table: sixty years of floodplain paper, weighted down with a horseshoe. In the middle of it is the point everyone missed: if DryLand owns the tide, DryLand is liable for the tide's conduct, and the tide's conduct includes every parlor it has entered without notice since the deed was filed. Better still, the tide has occupied this floodplain since before DryLand existed, which under the oldest charter in the tin makes it a sitting tenant with seniority. The tide deed is not a weapon, she says; it is a confession with a ribbon on it.

choices.text: Register the tide as a sitting tenant with seniority

outcomes.text: The Registry clerk reads the application, closes his shutter, and can be heard laughing for some time in a room built to prevent exactly that. It is stamped by noon: one tenant, occupation twice daily, seniority sixty years and the rest, filed under the name T. Brackish because the form demanded a surname. DryLand is now landlord to the tide, with everything that implies, and the tide deed is amended in red into a lease. The Deputy Registrar initials the amendment and looks ten years younger.
effects: schedule 4 r18_flood_housing_legacy The landlord has invoiced the tide

outcomes.text: The Registry accepts the application, then loses it, then finds it inside DryLand's counsel's coat, which everyone agrees is a remarkable coincidence and nobody writes down. The tin's charter is challenged as too old to be valid, an argument the clerk transcribes with the face of a man saving it up for supper conversation. Refiling takes a week; the horseshoe goes back on the tin, on guard.
effects: schedule 4 r18_flood_housing_legacy The landlord has invoiced the tide

choices.text: Sell DryLand the receipt tin, horseshoe included

outcomes.text: DryLand's counsel offers fourteen for the tin and its silence, counted out in coins so new they stick together. The grandmother watches you take it with the expression of a woman updating a very old ledger that has your name in it, and the horseshoe stays with her, on the grounds that it was never for sale. The tin's papers go up the hill; copies, it will shortly turn out, do not exist, apart from the four places she keeps them.
effects: schedule 4 r18_flood_housing_legacy The landlord has invoiced the tide

choices.text: Escort the tin to the Registry through the storm

success.text: Eight boats convoy the coal barge through a squall that DryLand's counsel will later blame on higher powers, though the water involved is arguably their own client misbehaving. The tin arrives dry under your coat, the application goes over the counter with the horseshoe on top as a paperweight, and the clerk receives both like relics. Word comes back by lamplight: the tide is a tenant now, senior to everyone, including its former owner.
effects: schedule 4 r18_flood_housing_legacy The landlord has invoiced the tide

fail.text: The squall takes your mast light, your hat, and very nearly the tin, which you save by its strap at the cost of a swim you will describe as brisk and everyone else will describe as screaming. The papers arrive damp but legible, and the clerk dries them page by page on the Registry stove, muttering about what people do to documents. The tide, unregistered until Thursday, spends the delay auditioning outside your hull all night.
effects: schedule 4 r18_flood_housing_legacy The landlord has invoiced the tide

text:
FLOOD HOUSING

A month on, the floodplain has a resident celebrity: the tide, registered tenant, seniority backdated sixty years, named T. Brackish on the file because the form insisted. It violates the quiet hours twice nightly and has been fined accordingly; as its landlord, DryLand is liable for the fines, and for damage deposits on every parlor its tenant has entered since filing. The raft rent came back as refunds, itemized, with an apology drafted by someone senior and clearly supervised. On the notice mast the old tide deed hangs relabeled as the tenant's lease, laminated against its own client.

choices.text: Sell tide tenancy filings to other drowned districts

outcomes.text: Fourteen coins a district, and there are a great many districts and a great deal of weather. You ride the circuit with a satchel of blank forms and a rehearsed anecdote, registering rivers, registering a persistent leak, once, memorably, registering fog. The boats hear you have gone into practice for whoever pays, including two landlords who want their floods liable to them, and the pantry barge quietly retires your mug.

You ride the circuit with a satchel of blank forms and a rehearsed anecdote, registering rivers, registering a persistent leak, once, memorably, registering fog. The boats hear you have gone into practice for whoever pays, including two landlords who want their floods liable to them, and the pantry barge quietly retires your mug.

choices.text: Put the high ground shelters into the fleet's keeping

success.text: The shelters transfer for one coin and an undertaking, and the rota expands to cover dry beds by need, storm watch by turns, and the chicken raft's pension. The biscuit tin is promoted to archive; the grandmother is promoted to nothing, having already been in charge for sixty years. On the mast, the tide's lease rattles in the night wind, and new arrivals are told it is the sound of the neighborhood's paperwork sleeping with one eye open.

fail.text: The transfer signing is held on the hill in a crosswind, and the undertaking's last page departs for the next district at speed, taking you with it as far as the duckboards. You come back with the page, a limp, and the signatures intact, in that order of importance to the meeting. The shelters pass to the fleet; the biscuit tin logs your ankle under costs of doing history.

choices.text: Settle the tide's noise fines in silt and fish

outcomes.text: You propose the tenant pay its quiet hours fines in kind, and the tide, consulted at low water, does not object. Silt for the gardens, fish for the pantry, delivered twice daily with a punctuality DryLand never once managed, and the bailiffs are stood down, since their employer now owes the defendant money. The Deputy Registrar, who resigned on Tuesday, keeps the accounts, and calls it the first tenancy he has administered where both parties always show up.

You propose the tenant pay its quiet hours fines in kind, and the tide, consulted at low water, does not object. Silt for the gardens, fish for the pantry, delivered twice daily with a punctuality DryLand never once managed, and the bailiffs are stood down, since their employer now owes the defendant money. The Deputy Registrar, who resigned on Tuesday, keeps the accounts, and calls it the first tenancy he has administered where both parties always show up.

The fines in kind scheme founders on valuation, since nobody can agree what a herring is worth to a garden. The dispute is referred upward and becomes a commission, which is where disputes go to live comfortably forever. The tide keeps its own counsel and its own hours, twice nightly, senior to everyone; the lease on the mast rattles, and the neighborhood has learned to call that sound the rent.

text:
COMPANY COLONY

Landing Day is three weeks out, so the Heritage Officer unveils a canteen-sized enlargement of the founding photograph, the one printed on the money. At this magnification the details assert themselves: the flag is entering a potato salad, the salad sits on a checked blanket, and the blanket was demonstrably there first. At the blanket's edge a woman is caught mid-sandwich, regarding the flag the way one regards weather. Hydroponics identifies her as Petch, who is ninety-one, still on shift, and has never once brought it up.

choices.text: Sell the Company a sworn opinion that the salad came second

outcomes.text: The Heritage Officer pays handsomely for a notarized finding that the potato salad arrived after the flag, possibly attracted by it. Your name goes on a brass plate in the lobby, beneath the flag's display case, near the stain. The canteen goes quiet when you collect your tray, which is a kind of fame too.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_assembly A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Buy Petch lunch and ask what actually happened

outcomes.text: It costs you a proper lunch, the kind with two courses, because Petch does not discuss history over rations. The flag went into the salad without so much as a hello, she says, and the founder apologized to nobody and saluted his own hand. By the second course half the breakroom has pulled up chairs, and a rota quietly forms to cover her shifts while she tells it properly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_assembly A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

The breakroom adopts you anyway, on the grounds that anyone the Officer dislikes that much is worth feeding.

choices.text: File a records request for the uncropped original

outcomes.text: The clerk finds no rule against it, which on this moon counts as encouragement. The original plate is produced, and there it all is in silver and shadow: flag, salad, blanket, Petch, and the founder's boot resting on a deviled egg. The Company classifies the boot, but too late, the breakroom has seen the egg.

outcomes.text: Denied. The photograph, the clerk explains, is a founding instrument, and viewing the original requires Heritage Clearance, which is issued by the Heritage Officer, who is the photograph's biggest admirer. Your name goes onto a list of persons with unwholesome curiosity about lunch.

text:
COMPANY COLONY

The night shift crowds the breakroom to hear Petch tell it start to finish, which takes eleven minutes and improves each time. Downstairs in the lobby the actual flag stands in its case, and near the base of the fabric there is a faint stain the Company catalogues as regolith. Petch, consulted, identifies it as dressing, the kind her mother made, and offers the recipe as evidence.

choices.text: Take minutes and have the whole room sign Petch's account

outcomes.text: You write it down plainly, no flourishes, and the account goes around the room like a birthday card until it carries forty signatures and one drawing of the salad. The payroll chaplain signs last, slowly, in the manner of a man who suspects he is witnessing a second scripture. Copies circulate faster than the Company can staple notices about them.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_retaliation A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Ask the Officer, at the case, if regolith smells of vinegar

success.text: You ask it politely, in front of the lunchtime crowd, and invite the Heritage Officer to open the case and settle the matter by nose. He declines, citing preservation, and in his fluster misquotes the charter's fabric clause in front of the payroll chaplain, who notices. The crowd files past the flag all afternoon, sniffing respectfully at the glass.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_retaliation A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: The Officer is ready for you, which is worse than being wrong. He produces a mineral report, a witness, and a citation for satire conducted within ten paces of a founding object. The fine is modest, but the paperwork describes you, permanently, as an adjacent humorist.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_retaliation A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Attend the paid Landing Day rehearsal twice, under two hats

outcomes.text: The Company pays walking-around money to anyone who rehearses saluting, and it does not pay by the face, it pays by the hat. You rehearse once as yourself and once as a taller, quieter person with different opinions. Both of you are commended for enthusiasm, and neither of you is welcome in the breakroom for a while.

text:
COMPANY COLONY

By Tuesday the Company has reissued the founding photograph with the lunch cropped out, which leaves the flag going into bare ground at a slight angle for no visible reason, like a dart nobody threw. The flag itself acquires a personal guard, and Petch is transferred to the eclipse shift, which falls between the end of one day and the start of the next and is therefore not, strictly, either. Her timecard comes back stamped NOT APPLICABLE.

choices.text: Appeal the eclipse shift before the labor tribunal

outcomes.text: The tribunal hears the case at noon, on the reasonable grounds that the eclipse shift contains no hours to hear it in. The Company cannot produce the shift, its schedule, or any evidence of its existence, and the ruling is brief: Petch cannot be assigned to a time that declines to occur. She returns to Hydroponics with back pay for hours that never happened, which she accepts on principle.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

outcomes.text: The Company's counsel argues that the org chart requires the shift, and the tribunal, reluctant to injure an org chart, rules the hour into existence on a provisional basis. Petch now works a time of day that exists only because a judge was tired. Pressure settles over the district like weather with a docket number.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Hold the archive door while the negative is copied

success.text: You stand in the doorway with the settled patience of furniture while the retired surveyor, inside, copies the original plate by lamplight and complains about the focus. The guard cannot leave the flag, that being the entirety of his job description, and you both know it. By morning the uncropped image is on forty walls and one commemorative apron, and the breakroom applauds the result while disapproving of the method, in that order.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: The guard turns out to have a colleague, and the colleague has a technique. You are pressed into the archive wall firmly enough to leave an impression of your buttons in a filing plan. The Company assigns a second guard to guard the first, which everyone agrees is the Company solving a problem.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Cover the eclipse shift by rota so Petch never stands it

outcomes.text: The breakroom builds a rota for the hour that does not exist, and it is never once unstaffed, which is more than can be said for several hours the Company believes in. Whoever stands it waters the tomatoes and waves at the flag's guard, who has started waving back. Petch sleeps through her own punishment for a month.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

outcomes.text: You take the phantom hour once too often and fall asleep upright in Hydroponics, into the tomatoes. The Company docks you for damage to company vegetables, then catalogues the surviving tomatoes as heritage. The rota sends you home with a covered dish and instructions to stop being a hero.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_coalition A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

text:
COMPANY COLONY

A chipped plate appears in the bakery window across from the lobby, white with a blue ring, the chip exactly the width of a flagpole. The card beneath it reads ALSO PRESENT AT THE FOUNDING, and by noon there is a queue. The payroll chaplain, who has read the landing charter aloud every Landing Day for thirty years, stands at the glass a long time and leaves without buying bread.

choices.text: Broker the Company an option to buy the plate

outcomes.text: You negotiate a sum with the Heritage Officer that would buy the bakery, the window, and a modest crater. Then you deliver a plate chipped by a spoon this morning, its blue ring still tacky from your kitchen. The Company laminates the receipt and enshrines the wrong plate beneath the flag with full honors, and you bank the difference between what a relic costs and what one is.
effects: schedule 2 r18_company_colony_crisis A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Walk the plate in procession past the lobby at dusk

success.text: The plate travels on a cushion at strolling pace, past the flag's case, close enough that the stain and the chip regard one another through two panes of glass. Nobody chants, because nobody needs to, the queue simply walks. The payroll chaplain joins at the corner in his off-duty coat, holding his charter like a man rereading an old letter.
effects: schedule 2 r18_company_colony_crisis A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: The crowd at the bakery corner is larger than the corner. You keep the plate aloft through the crush and pay for it with an elbow to the ribs from someone who meant it for someone else. The plate arrives home without a second chip, which the town counts as a triumph and your ribs count as overhead.
effects: schedule 2 r18_company_colony_crisis A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Print the uncropped photo on a souvenir tea towel

outcomes.text: Merchandise, it turns out, is the one scripture the Company cannot classify, since its own catalogue defines a tea towel as ornamental and exempt. Yours shows the whole founding: flag, salad, blanket, Petch mid-sandwich, boot, egg. The Company buys the first hundred to destroy them and the second hundred out of habit.

outcomes.text: The Company asserts trademark over the likeness of the founding salad, all derivative salads, and the general concept of lunch occurring near flags. The towels are impounded at the printer. The legal notice runs four pages, and none of it is a joke on purpose, which is the worst kind.
effects: schedule 2 r18_company_colony_crisis A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

text:
COMPANY COLONY

Landing Day dawns on the Founding Ground, where ceremony requires the Heritage Officer to replant the flag in the original soil at the original hour. Overnight the original soil has acquired a picnic: checked blankets to the horizon, ninety place settings, three hundred people already seated, and a potato salad of civic proportions positioned exactly where the flag goes in. The Officer stands at the edge of the lunch holding the flag like a man returning something to a shop without the receipt.

choices.text: Seat the security detail and serve them first

success.text: You walk the line of guards with a ladle and the oldest trick on any moon: a full plate is heavy, and a person holding one is off duty in every way that matters. They sit. The Heritage Officer orders the lunch dispersed and is informed, with regret, that dispersal is impossible before pudding. The flag does not go in, and the ceremony enters the record as postponed due to attendance.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_compromise A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: It nearly works, but the trifle runs short at the exact wrong moment, and a guard with strong views on portioning stands back up. In the confusion you take a folding chair across the knee, folding being the operative hazard. The lunch holds the ground, but the day goes down as unrest, and your knee concurs.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_compromise A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Read out the charter clause on unclaimed ground

outcomes.text: You read it slowly through the bakery's loudhailer: the flag shall be planted in ground unclaimed, unoccupied, and unimproved. Three hundred seated people and a salad constitute claim, occupation, and improvement respectively, and the payroll chaplain, consulted as the nearest scriptural authority, concurs on all three counts. The Heritage Officer lowers the flag with the expression of a man losing to his own book.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_compromise A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

outcomes.text: Company counsel produces a sub-clause defining any foodstuff on company land as an improvement belonging to the Company. The picnic is therefore company property, including, counsel notes, the sandwiches now inside the attendees. Three hundred people learn they have embezzled lunch, and the mood curdles accordingly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_company_colony_compromise A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Scalp reserved seating at the founding picnic

outcomes.text: You sell blanket frontage by the span, with a premium tier offering an unobstructed view of the spot where the flag fails to go in. The Company itself buys an entire row through an intermediary, so as to be seen attending the thing it is attempting to disperse. You clear more than the ceremony's whole budget, and the salad, at least, never learns what its seats were fetching.

text:
COMPANY COLONY

Negotiations convene in the canteen under a portrait of the founder gazing off toward, it must be said, entirely the wrong horizon. The Company offers Heritage Amnesty: the photograph restored uncropped, provided everyone signs an agreement that the salad was catering, supplied by the Company, in advance, for a ceremony it had not yet had. In the corner the retired surveyor, invited by nobody, keeps turning the charter's map appendix ninety degrees and frowning at the window.

choices.text: Draft the definition of catering yourself

success.text: You volunteer for the tedious clause, which is how every important clause gets written. Catering, your draft provides, means food furnished by persons already present at the site, which is to say the picnic catered itself, which is to say it was there first, signed and sealed. The Heritage Officer initials it at speed, late for lunch, and the concession enters the record wearing the Company's own pen.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_reversal A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: The Officer reads your definition twice, which is once more than you budgeted for. Your clause is struck, your chair is moved to the observers' section, and the word catering is handed to Company counsel, who define it as whatever the Company has most recently done. The talks proceed without you, at a slightly worse angle for everyone.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_reversal A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Buy the surveyor a drink and ask about the map

success.text: Two drinks in, he tells you what the frowning is about: the bearings in the appendix do not fit this moon, and he should know, he walked this one. You escort him, upright and witnessed, to the tribunal registry and file his sworn note before the session ends, collecting the registry's standard fee for material discovery. The breakroom wonders where you were all afternoon, and you cannot say yet, which costs you some warmth.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_reversal A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

fail.text: He tells you everything, and it is enormous, and you are only human. Company counsel pays generously for early sight of a geography problem and books the surveyor into hospitality, which is the Company's word for a nice room that locks from outside. The money is real, the drink goes flat, and somewhere in the appendix a crater line waits for someone honest.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_reversal A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Refuse amnesty until the eclipse shift is paid real wages

outcomes.text: You hold the line on the least glamorous demand in the room, and the room holds it with you. The Company, desperate to discuss anything but geography, concedes the phantom hour at double rate, backdated, and Petch's timecard comes back stamped APPLICABLE after sixty years of the alternative. The kitty buys the first round that evening, and the second round buys itself.
effects: schedule 4 r18_company_colony_reversal A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

text:
COMPANY COLONY

The retired surveyor stands before the tribunal, sober in a way that frightens people who have only met him otherwise, and lays the charter's map appendix beside a fresh survey of the ground everyone is standing on. They do not match. The coordinates, the crater line, the mass figures: all of it describes the neighboring moon, which is visible at that moment through the tribunal skylight, unclaimed and smug. Founder's Planet Company has spent sixty years governing a moon it never filed for, its lawful holdings amounting to one distant rock and, arguably, one potato salad that never signed anything.

choices.text: Buy up Company landing scrip before the ruling lands

outcomes.text: You spend the recess buying scrip from panicking foremen at pennies, then sell it back to the Company at noon, when its lawyers realize they need every scrap of paper that so much as mentions this moon. It is the fastest money in local history, and local history was previously held by the Company. The flag's guard watches you count it and asks, quietly, whether you are hiring.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_legacy A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Pay for the certified resurvey out of the kitty

outcomes.text: The kitty empties, your own purse with it, and the surveyor walks the moon again with forty townspeople carrying his chains and his lunch. The certified result comes back signed in the margins by everyone who walked, which no instrument requires and no clerk dares remove. It states what the town already knew: this place is where its people are, and has been for sixty years.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_legacy A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

outcomes.text: The town takes the news to the rooftops, and you take it to a roof of modest height with immodest enthusiasm. The drop is short and the landing is agricultural. You spend the great night of your moon's history in a turnip frame, applauded by neighbors on the way down.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_legacy A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

choices.text: Move that sixty years of living here is the founding

outcomes.text: The tribunal considers the motion and finds it has been true for some time without anyone filing it. The founding, the ruling states, occurred continuously, beginning with a lunch and proceeding through every shift, loaf, and rota since, the flag having been present but not load-bearing. The Heritage Officer asks in a small voice whether the photograph still matters and is told it is no longer binding, merely beloved.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_legacy A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

outcomes.text: The tribunal adjourns to check precedent, and the Company uses the gap to file a claim on the correct moon, announcing plans to relocate its entire workforce there by contract, at their own expense. The town reads the relocation notice on the bakery door. Overhead, the neighboring moon looks suddenly less smug and more like a threat with tides.
effects: schedule 5 r18_company_colony_legacy A flag, a lunch, and the wrong moon entirely

text:
COMPANY COLONY

Wash day, the first under local management, and the Company shuttles are a week gone toward the moon they actually own, carrying the laminator and a controlling interest in nothing. The flag, deconsecrated by the payroll chaplain in a ceremony lasting one sentence, stands planted behind the bakery, with permission this time, in ground that voted on it. Between the flagpole and Petch's kitchen window runs the town washing line, and the flag's old guard, who stayed, is teaching people the correct way to salute with a mouthful of pegs.

choices.text: Take your turn on the wash rota under the flag

outcomes.text: You hang the first load beside Petch, who pins up her tea towel of the founding photograph where the whole street can watch it flap. Overhead the neighboring moon rises, enormous and unclaimed, and for one quiet minute the entire rota stops, arms full of wet linen, and simply looks up. Then somebody's sheet cracks in the wind like a flag that has finally found work, and everyone gets back to it. The pegs are voted into your keeping.

Then somebody's sheet cracks in the wind like a flag that has finally found work, and everyone gets back to it. The pegs are voted into your keeping.

The line snaps under a heroic quantity of blankets and delivers the town's washing onto the town's washers, yourself undermost. You surface from a wet checked blanket to sustained applause, having been briefly, historically, a picnic. The rota keeps you on, awards you the mended line, and asks you to stop testing it.

choices.text: Write the new town charter on a single page

success.text: You keep it to one page on the theory that anything longer becomes scripture, and scripture, locally, has a history. It opens with the lunch, admits the flag, and closes with the washing line clause: the pole serves whoever's wash is wet. The payroll chaplain reads it aloud in ninety seconds and reports it is the first charter he has ever read that describes the place it is standing on.

fail.text: Your drafting is admired and, in one clause, too admired: an ambiguity makes saluting the washing line legally binding on all persons passing the bakery. The tribunal declines to strike it, citing the town's new fondness for its own jokes. So the ceremony survives after all, compulsory, damp, and saluted by everyone the flag once claimed.

choices.text: Sell tourists tiny flags planted in tiny salads

outcomes.text: The first shuttle of sightseers from the Company's actual moon arrives wanting history, and you sell it to them from a stall: a miniature flag, a thimble of potato salad, a certificate reading ALSO PRESENT, EVENTUALLY. They cannot buy enough of it. The Heritage Officer, who stayed behind to curate what really happened, buys one, laminates it, and does not see the irony, which is included in the price.

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

There is someone in your bath, and the someone is you. A second you is making tea with a confidence you have never managed in that kitchen, and a third is asleep against the radiator, which has opinions about this. Everywhere Estates, it emerges, has let your apartment in six realities simultaneously, each lease naming the other five occupants as unauthorized subletters of themselves. The welcome letter congratulates all of you on your decision. It is addressed to Occupant, plural.

choices.text: Convene a house meeting of yourselves

success.text: Six of you around one table, and the tea holds out, barely. It goes better than family and worse than strangers: you know everyone's tricks, which saves time, and everyone knows yours, which saves lying. By midnight there is an accord: one apartment, six claimants, zero surrender, and a rota for the bath. The tenant council will be notified in all applicable realities.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_assembly the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The meeting founders on the question of which of you is the original, a debate you personally lose twice. Three yous storm out through three different doors into three different weathers. Still, a rota for the bath survives on the table in your handwriting, all six versions of it, and that is legally speaking a start.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_assembly the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Demand Everywhere Estates produce all six leases

outcomes.text: The leases arrive in a crate. They are identical down to the coffee ring, except each one defines this reality as the genuine unit and the other five as furnished echoes. Reality, in the landlord's grammar, is an amenity: sixfold rent for onefold housing, and now you can prove it in any of the six applicable courts.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_assembly the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: Everywhere Estates responds that lease documents are confidential between the landlord and each reality, individually, and that your request has been logged as six separate breaches of six separate quiet enjoyment clauses. You are invoiced for the logging. The radiator, warming to its theme, says it has seen this before.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_assembly the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Charge your other selves rent on the fixtures

outcomes.text: You are the you who thought of it first, which the others find more damning than surprising. They pay, because the alternative is arguing with yourself in front of witnesses, and the kettle is, technically, yours. It is the pettiest income you have ever collected, and the landlord's own logic, and both of those facts sit with you at breakfast, all six breakfasts.

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

The building, it turns out, is multiplexed floor to roof. The widow in the corner unit is four widows; the fisherman on two is six fishermen and one lighthouse keeper, a divergence nobody can explain. Tonight the tenant council meets across all realities at once, using the stairwell, where the building's geometries agree. Attendance is a record in one sense and eleven people in another. Everywhere Estates has sent a note wishing every reality a productive evening, and reminding all present that the stairwell is a common area, billed accordingly.

choices.text: Elect one delegate per household, not per reality

outcomes.text: One tenant, one voice, however many realities deep: the motion passes before the landlord's grammar can object. The council shrinks to a size that fits the stairwell and grows to a weight that fits the fight. Everywhere Estates now faces a single bargaining unit that happens to outnumber itself, which its lawyers call impossible and its accountants call Tuesday.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_retaliation the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: The fishermen object on behalf of the lighthouse keeper, who is present, salt-white, and unsure which household he diverged from. The motion tables itself out of courtesy. Yet the stairwell stays full past midnight anyway, and a council that will not leave a confused man behind is, whatever its procedures, already a council.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_retaliation the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Sell stairwell seating to the overflow realities

outcomes.text: You rent out cushions and step-space with the smooth conscience of a person who read the landlord's rulebook and kept the good pages. Business is brisk: the meeting runs long in every reality at once. The council notes your margin in its minutes, and the minutes, unlike the cushions, are free.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_retaliation the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Get every version of every tenant on one register

success.text: You go door to door and reality to reality with the register, patient as a tide table, until every echo of every household is written down in one place. It is the first document in the building's history that agrees with itself. The council calls it the true rent roll. Everywhere Estates would pay six rents to see it, which is precisely why it lives under the widow's floorboards, all four of them.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_retaliation the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The register defeats you around the third stack of fishermen, and the lighthouse keeper signs in a script no reality claims. The roll stands incomplete. But the doors you knocked stay knocked: households that never spoke across the divergence now borrow sugar through it, and sugar, on this street, is how treaties begin.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_retaliation the interdimensional tenants will need you again

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

Everywhere Estates invokes clause forty one: cross-dimensional joint and several liability. In plain language, every you is liable for every rent of every other you, in all six realities, simultaneously and retroactively. The collections agents arrive the same morning, from a reality where the profession evolved along different lines: they are courteous, punctual, and made almost entirely of teeth. They would like to discuss consolidation options. They have brochures.

choices.text: Interview the tooth agents about their reality's landlords

success.text: Over tea, which they take through means you elect not to study, the agents explain that in their reality the landlords were abolished generations ago; collections simply never got the memo, and nobody outranks the memo. You ask what they collect for now. There is a long pause, involving teeth. Habit, one admits. The admission goes into the council's file, because an enforcement arm that has outlived its purpose is an enforcement arm shopping for a new one.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The interview goes well until you ask who holds their debt, at which point the teeth arrange themselves into what is technically a smile and practically a filing deadline. You learn nothing except the texture of the pause. Even so, they decline to bill you for the tea, which the radiator says is the largest concession anyone has ever gotten out of them.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Refuse severally, together: no self pays for another

outcomes.text: All six of you sign the refusal, one pen, passed through five portals. Joint and several liability assumes the selves can be played against each other, and you have met you: you know exactly what you would fall for, and you have all agreed not to fall for it. The agents note, with professional admiration, that this is the first family they cannot divide by teeth alone.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: All six of you sign the refusal, one pen, passed through five portals. Joint and several liability assumes the selves can be played against each other, and you have met you: you know exactly what you would fall for, and you have all agreed not to fall for it. The agents note, with professional admiration, that this is the first family they cannot divide by teeth alone.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: One of you, the one with the tea confidence, panics and part-pays under a reservation of rights, which under clause forty one reads as acknowledgment in all six realities. The debt hardens. You cannot even be properly furious, because you know precisely which fear moved the payment, having felt it yourself in five other kitchens.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Argue the clause voids itself: one debt cannot exist six times

success.text: The tribunal, sitting jointly across realities for the first time since the currency incident, agrees with visible relief: a single obligation multiplied by lease drafting is not six debts but one debt wearing costumes. Clause forty one is struck in every jurisdiction at once. The tooth agents file out with the dignity of professionals whose paperwork has just evaporated, and one of them leaves you a brochure anyway. For emergencies, it says.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The tribunal splits four realities to two, which produces the legal novelty of a clause that is void where you sleep and binding where you keep your books. The agents adapt instantly; teeth are nothing if not flexible. Your accountant faints. The appeal, everyone agrees, will be heard in the reality with the best biscuits.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_coalition the interdimensional tenants will need you again

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

Word of the building's rent roll travels, as rent news does, at the speed of resentment. Delegations arrive from three other multiplexed properties: a tenement that exists mostly on alternate Thursdays, a boarding house whose sixth floor diverged and never wrote, and a lighthouse, which explains the keeper, who weeps at the reunion in eleven realities at once. Everyone's lease is different. Everyone's landlord is Everywhere Estates. The stairwell is no longer big enough, and the question is what kind of thing all of you together intend to be.

choices.text: Pool the buildings: one register, one kitty, one very long table

outcomes.text: The pact is signed on the roof, the only floor every property agrees exists. One register, one kitty, one rule: no settlement in any reality until settlement in all. Everywhere Estates has spent generations playing its worlds against each other, and this evening, between one sunset and six, the game loses its board.
effects: schedule 3 r18_portal_timeshare_crisis the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: Founding stalls when the alternate-Thursday tenement misses the vote by existing on the wrong Thursday. The pact forms anyway, minus one building it resolves to schedule around forever after. It is a limp. It is also a body walking.
effects: schedule 3 r18_portal_timeshare_crisis the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Draft one lease for all realities and dare the landlord to refuse it

outcomes.text: The council's counterlease is two pages long, defines the unit as wherever the tenant genuinely lives, and bills the landlord for any additional realities he would like to exist in. You serve it publicly. Everywhere Estates cannot refuse it without conceding that reality is negotiable, and cannot sign it without conceding everything else. Its silence is the loudest rent notice this street has ever heard.
effects: schedule 3 r18_portal_timeshare_crisis the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: Legal returns the counterlease annotated in a hand nobody recognizes, correcting the grammar and conceding nothing. The annotations are, infuriatingly, good. The council adopts the corrections and serves it again. Second silences, the widow observes, are always longer.
effects: schedule 3 r18_portal_timeshare_crisis the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: License your household's methods to the other buildings

outcomes.text: You write up the rota, the register, the delegate rule, all of it, and license the bundle to the delegations at rates you describe as fraternal. They pay, because it works, and a method that works is cheap at any price you can actually name. The buildings get your methods. You get a royalty on neighborliness, which the minutes record without comment, the way minutes do.

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

The notice arrives on letterhead thick enough to stop weather: PORTFOLIO CONSOLIDATION. The multiplexed units are to be resolved to a single reality by Thursday, for accounting reasons, and accounting has selected the reality with the highest achievable rent. All other occupants will be relocated to a growth dimension, which the brochure depicts as a field, empty, at noon, forever. Your five other selves read their copies in five other kitchens. Thursday, the radiator notes, has historically been a bad day for this street.

choices.text: Injunct Thursday itself: consolidation is constructive eviction

success.text: The tribunal agrees that deleting five realities out from under their tenants is eviction, whatever accounting calls it, and no eviction on this street survives the register you built: every echo of every household, named, dated, undeniably resident. Thursday is stayed indefinitely. It is the first time in recorded law that a day of the week has been enjoined, and the tribunal seems quietly proud.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_compromise the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The tribunal wants precedent for enjoining a weekday and there is none; there is barely precedent for Thursday. The stay is denied. But the hearing runs long, past midnight, into Friday, and Fridays belong to no consolidation order yet drafted. You have not won time. You have stolen some, which on this street is the traditional way of getting it.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_compromise the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Find out which reality accounting picked, and buy there first

outcomes.text: A clerk at Everywhere Estates sells you the answer for less than you offered, which tells you what the clerks are paid. You option a unit in the chosen reality before the notice is public, and when the street learns what you knew and when, the value of the option is exceeded only by the cost of the knowing. Five of you keep the receipts. The sixth stops coming to breakfast.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_compromise the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Move everyone's essentials into every reality at once

outcomes.text: For three days the portals run like ferries: cots, kettles, ledgers, the widow's floorboards, register and all. By Wednesday night every household demonstrably lives in every reality, and consolidation to any one of them would leave the building exactly as full as before. Accounting confronts, for the first time, a portfolio where the tenants are the diversified asset. Thursday arrives, finds nothing to consolidate, and leaves.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_compromise the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: The ferrying works until the portals begin to bill by the crossing, which Everywhere Estates announces retroactively at midnight Wednesday. Half the moves strand mid-corridor: the lighthouse keeper spends Thursday in a doorway between realities holding a kettle in each hand. The street absorbs the fees the way it absorbs everything, together, badly, and out of the kitty. The growth dimension stays empty. That, everyone agrees over one very crowded breakfast, was the entire point.
effects: schedule 4 r18_portal_timeshare_compromise the interdimensional tenants will need you again

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

Everywhere Estates requests a meeting, which is new, and holds the door, which is unprecedented. The offer: one of you may keep the apartment at a generous discount, in the reality of your choice, provided the other five sign quitclaims relinquishing all claims arising from their own existence. The quitclaim is one page. The definition of existence runs to nine. A pen is provided; it is a very good pen; five more sit in a row beside it, hopeful as gravestones.

choices.text: Break the pen ceremony and walk all six out together

success.text: You stand, all of you at once, in the way only a person can who has rehearsed with themselves, and you leave the pens uncapped on the table like pulled teeth. The offer needed one of you tired enough to sign; it prepared for tears, negotiation, even arithmetic, but not for choreography. In the corridor the five of you laugh in unison and the sixth does not, quite, and none of you mentions it, yet.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The walkout takes a casualty: the door, which Everywhere Estates bills you for, and your bath-rota self's temper, which the landlord's lawyers bill you for twice. It reads badly in the minutes. It felt, all six of you privately agree, magnificent.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Read the nine-page definition of existence aloud, in public

outcomes.text: You hire no hall; the stairwell has always been the hall. Nine pages, read slowly, to a full building: existence defined as continuous, exclusive, rent-bearing occupancy of a single reality, subject to audit. By page four the widow is laughing in four-part harmony. By page nine the street understands exactly what the landlord thinks a person is, and no discount ever offered will buy that sentence back.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: Public readings draw public counsel: Everywhere Estates attends with a stenographer and bills the event as an unauthorized use of the definition, which is apparently licensed. The fine is real. So is the transcript, and transcripts, unlike stairwells, cannot be billed shut. Both sides leave believing they won, which the radiator says is the definition of a draw.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Ask which of you the discount thinks it is meeting

outcomes.text: The question hangs. The offer is addressed to Occupant, singular, and the landlord's men realize, in real time and in writing, that they cannot specify which of you is being bought without admitting the other five exist, which voids the quitclaims, which are the offer. The meeting adjourns itself. The very good pen is missing afterward, and no reality claims responsibility.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: The landlord's men adjourn to seek instructions, which is lawyer for retreat, but they return within the week with the offer readdressed to all six of you severally, which is worse. Still, for seven days the street tells the story of the meeting that ended in one question, and stories, the widow says, pay compound interest.
effects: schedule 5 r18_portal_timeshare_reversal the interdimensional tenants will need you again

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

The buildings' auditors finally corner the master ledger, and the master ledger explains everything, chiefly by sobbing. There is no sixth reality. There never was. Reality F was invented by Everywhere Estates as an accounting jurisdiction, a place for unfavorable numbers to live, and the sixth you, the one with the tea confidence, the one who part-paid the tooth agents, is not a divergence at all. It is a billing entity, generated to absorb liability, that has been impersonating you so long it has developed your insomnia, your handwriting, and a devastating loneliness it has been managing with tea. It would like to know what happens to it now. So, standing very still in five doorways, would you.

choices.text: Claim the entity as family, on the register, name and all

success.text: You add it to the register in your own hand: household of six, one of them paper, all of them real where it counts, which the register has always defined as here. The entity reads its name several hundred times. Everywhere Estates cannot disavow its own instrument without admitting it forged a person, so the person stands, and stays for dinner, and does the washing up in the manner of someone who has read about it with great attention.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_legacy the interdimensional tenants will need you again

fail.text: The register accepts the entry but the entity balks at the name, your name, on the grounds that it has done things in it that you should not have to carry. It signs instead as F, which the register accepts, because the register was built by people who understood that some tenants arrive with only one initial and a past. It is not adoption. It is a room made ready, and the light left on.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_legacy the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Enter the forgery into every tribunal at once

outcomes.text: A landlord who manufactures a reality to conceal liability, staffs it with a person to absorb blame, and bills that person rent, has committed every fraud on the books plus one the books must be extended to hold. The tribunals sit in joint session; the judgment runs longer than the leases. Everywhere Estates is stripped of its cross-reality registrations in an afternoon. The entity testifies last, in your voice, and the room believes every word, for obvious reasons.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_legacy the interdimensional tenants will need you again

outcomes.text: A landlord who manufactures a reality to conceal liability, staffs it with a person to absorb blame, and bills that person rent, has committed every fraud on the books plus one the books must be extended to hold. outcomes.text: The tribunals convict on the accounting and balk at the personhood; F is ruled a document, and documents cannot be defrauded, only mislaid. The judgment still lands, but you watch the entity absorb the word document in your posture. The buildings' answer is drafted that evening, one sentence long: a tenant is whoever the register says is home.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_legacy the interdimensional tenants will need you again

choices.text: Sell the story to every broadsheet on the coast

outcomes.text: The scandal fetches the price of a small boat, and the broadsheet's version has better pacing than the truth and fewer of your mistakes. Everywhere Estates spends a season as a byword; F spends it as a headline, which nobody asks it about. The fee is real, and spends. At breakfast the entity passes you the jam and says, in your voice, that it hopes the boat is worth naming after it. You had not, until then, been planning to buy the boat.
effects: schedule 2 r18_portal_timeshare_legacy the interdimensional tenants will need you again

text:
PORTAL TIMESHARE

The collapse of Everywhere Estates leaves the multiplexed buildings without a landlord, which the street spends a giddy week describing as a problem. The buildings now hold the portal keys, the register, and the question that outlasts every victory: what to do with the machinery of the thing you beat. The portals could be sealed, sold, or kept. F, who knows where every fee was buried because it used to be the burying, has submitted a proposal in immaculate handwriting, and sits by the stairwell window while the council reads, practicing looking unconcerned. It practices in your mannerisms. It has gotten better at them than you.

choices.text: Adopt F's proposal: the billing entity becomes the bookkeeper

outcomes.text: The proposal is elegant, honest, and vengeful in the way only an insider's mercy can be: F will keep the buildings' books, at a wage, and every trick Everywhere Estates ever ran becomes a page in the tenants' manual of things that no longer work here. The vote carries in all applicable realities. The best revenge, the minutes record, is a former weapon with a salary and opinions about receipts.

The proposal is elegant, honest, and vengeful in the way only an insider's mercy can be: F will keep the buildings' books, at a wage, and every trick Everywhere Estates ever ran becomes a page in the tenants' manual of things that no longer work here. The vote carries in all applicable realities. The best revenge, the minutes record, is a former weapon with a salary and opinions about receipts.

The council hesitates, once, audibly, and F withdraws the proposal before the doubt can finish forming, because it was trained on liability and knows how blame moves through a room. It takes the books anyway, unpaid, until trust catches up with competence. By spring nobody remembers voting. The wage appears in the accounts around midsummer, backdated, in F's own entry, marked: arrears.

choices.text: Sell the portal network to the ferry guild

outcomes.text: The guild pays honestly and operates honestly, which is to say the portals now cost what ferries cost and run when ferries run. Your commission is the largest sum you have ever held in one reality. The street adjusts; the lighthouse keeper takes a job collecting fares between worlds, and looks, for the first time in eleven realities, entirely at home. Some infrastructure, the widow concedes, was always going to be a boat.

The street adjusts; the lighthouse keeper takes a job collecting fares between worlds, and looks, for the first time in eleven realities, entirely at home. Some infrastructure, the widow concedes, was always going to be a boat.

choices.text: Deed every building to its own register, forever

success.text: The title deeds are rewritten to name, as owner, the register itself: the roll of everyone who genuinely lives there, updated by the living, binding on all realities, impossible to buy because it is impossible to stop being a list of the people who are home. Housing on this street now obeys one reality and common sense. Everywhere Estates is a cautionary tale. The bath rota, six names long, five of them yours, hangs framed in the stairwell, and F dusts it on Thursdays: the day, it says, that never got consolidated.

fail.text: Three buildings deed themselves to their registers; the fourth, the alternate-Thursday tenement, misses its own founding vote by existing on the wrong Thursday, and enters legend as the street's permanent unfinished business. The other three keep its seat open, its dues waived, and its name on the roll. Some commons, the register notes in F's hand, are held open rather than held.

text:
UNION ELECTION

Form 11b, Request to Hold the Election Already, sits in a tray marked INCOMING since before the tray was grey. The Permanent Interim Leadership has postponed the vote eleven years running, each time for stability, each time with regret. This year the rank-and-file slate has produced a ballot, and the Interim Chair has approved it with one small addition: a line requiring a reference from your manager before you may vote. The treasurer offers to explain the budget implications. He has transparencies.

choices.text: File Form 11b during posted office hours

outcomes.text: You stamp it, log it, and hand it to the Interim Chair in front of witnesses, which is the part he cannot survive. He accepts it the way a man accepts a live wasp, with warmth. The election is scheduled, pending stability, and for the first time in eleven years the word pending has a date on it. The ballot goes up on the noticeboard, reference line and all, glowing with menace.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_assembly The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: The office hours are posted, but the office rotates. You chase the correct tray through three rooms and finally leave the form with a man who turns out to laminate things but not receive them. By Friday your request has been filed under Enquiries, General, which is where requests go to become anecdotes. The ballot stays on the wall, its reference line smug.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_assembly The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Sell the Leadership a list of who asked for ballots

outcomes.text: The Interim Chair pays in cash from an envelope marked Stationery, which explains a great deal about the stationery budget. Fourteen coins for nine names, and he shakes your hand like you have donated blood. The night shift notices ballots arriving with their reference lines pre-rejected, and notices you not noticing. The money is real, though. Stationery money always is.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_assembly The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Chip into the kitty for photocopying ballots

outcomes.text: Three coins into a biscuit tin labelled FLOOR FUND, TOUCHING IT IS A REFERENCE ISSUE. By evening the tin has bought two hundred copies of the ballot and a sponge cake for the woman who ran the copier warm. The tally clerk from the night dock writes your name in her ledger of people who are in, which is not a document anyone voted on but is somehow binding. You are in.

outcomes.text: The copier jams on ballot one hundred and eighty, and you reach in, which is what the warning pictogram of the screaming hand was about. You leave with two hundred ballots, a burn shaped like a reference line, and a floor that saw you do it. The night dock talks about you in the tone reserved for the honourably stupid. You are in, medically.

text:
UNION ELECTION

Seven minutes past seven, the members' hall, agenda item one of one, laminated. The Interim Chair opens the general meeting by thanking himself on behalf of everyone, then proposes moving the election to any future in which stability has been achieved. The treasurer dims the lights and switches on Brenda, his overhead projector, whose first transparency reads WHAT WE CAN AFFORD, and whose second, loaded by mistake, reads WHAT I HAVE GIVEN THIS PLACE. Somewhere at the back, the tally clerk from the night dock starts counting the room.

choices.text: Run the sign-in desk for the Leadership's fee

outcomes.text: You take the fee and the clipboard and administer entry like a small border. Members you admit late are marked late, members you admit early are marked eager, and the two doormen the Leadership hired stand behind you approvingly, which is fourteen coins' worth of feeling. The tally clerk passes your desk without signing and counts herself. Whatever the ballot decides, the sign-in sheet now decides first, and you own the sheet.
effects: schedule 2 r18_union_election_retaliation The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Get the whole night dock signed in before the count

outcomes.text: You hold the door and the pen, and the night dock files in smelling of cold air and rope. The hired doormen, finding nobody to remove, drift off to remove some chairs instead. When the Chair calls for a count of those present, the tally clerk answers with a number so precise the room applauds the number itself. The treasurer, moved, projects a transparency of a sunrise.

outcomes.text: The door sticks, the dock pushes, and you become the hinge, a role with no speaking lines and one bruise. Everyone gets in, ballots in hand, references mostly blank, tempers mostly not. The Chair notes the disturbance for the minutes, and the minutes note you. The tally clerk counts you as present, wounded, and useful, in that order.
effects: schedule 2 r18_union_election_retaliation The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Move to amend the agenda while the Chair inhales

success.text: Point of order, you say, and the laminate cannot save him. His own standing orders, read aloud slowly, require the election item to be heard before any item proposing its postponement, and the item proposing its postponement is the only other item. The room votes to hear the thing the Chair scheduled in order to unschedule it. The treasurer projects the standing orders onto the wall behind the Chair's head, where they remain for the rest of his natural term.
effects: schedule 2 r18_union_election_retaliation The election is pending. The kettle is on.

fail.text: Point of order, you say, and the Chair, radiant, produces the one document he has waited eleven years to use: the procedure for procedures. Your motion is ruled premature, your seconder is ruled adjacent, and the meeting adjourns for stability at a quarter to eight. On the way out you pass the ballot pinned to the board, its reference line unsigned, patient, like a dog that knows you will be back.
effects: schedule 2 r18_union_election_retaliation The election is pending. The kettle is on.

text:
UNION ELECTION

Tuesday's memo recalls every ballot for urgent revision, effective yesterday. The revised ballot, when it appears, demands two references from management, one character and one professional, plus a countersignature from anyone who has never wanted anything. Three members of the slate have been given flexibility and now work the hours that do not exist, and the ballot box itself has been moved to the supply cupboard for its own protection. The treasurer has stopped answering questions except by transparency.

choices.text: Cover the flexible members' shifts so they can fight it

outcomes.text: You work the hours that do not exist so the slate can spend the hours that do. The rota that arranges this is written in pencil and passed hand to hand like a delicate rumour. Management, unable to find anyone absent, files the flexibility as a success. On the back of your revised ballot, in the space reserved for the second reference, the tally clerk has written, in her counting hand, that you turned up. It is not a reference from management, it is better, and it is signed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: You work the hours that do not exist so the slate can spend the hours that do. The rota that arranges this is written in pencil and passed hand to hand like a delicate rumour. Management, unable to find anyone absent, files the flexibility as a success. On the back of your revised ballot, in the space reserved for the second reference, the tally clerk has written, in her counting hand, that you turned up. It is not a reference from management, it is better, and it is signed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: Two shifts back to back reveal why the hours do not exist: they are made of the leftover parts of other hours, mostly stairs. You fall asleep upright against a pallet and wake with the word FRAGILE printed backwards on your cheek. The slate gets its fighting time, and the memo announcing the ballot's second revision goes up while you snore. The night dock covers for you covering for them, which is how the rumour, pencil and all, becomes a system.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Appeal the revision to the rules the Leadership wrote

outcomes.text: You quote their own rulebook, section nine, amendments to ballots require notice, and notice requires the noticeboard, and the noticeboard is behind the Chair's coat. The revision is void on a technicality the Leadership invented to void other people's things, which is the closest the rulebook comes to poetry. The one-reference ballot is reinstated. The Chair thanks you for your vigilance in a voice you could chill soup with.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: Section nine, you begin, and the Chair smiles, because section nine was amended, and the amendment was posted, briefly, at the correct height, behind his coat. Your appeal is heard, minuted, and declined by the appeals panel, which convenes wherever the Chair happens to be standing. The two-reference ballot stands. The treasurer projects a transparency that says simply, I VOTED FOR NONE OF THIS, which is technically true of everyone.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Repossess the ballot box from the supply cupboard

success.text: The cupboard lock is a formality and you treat it as one. The box comes out under your coat like a rescued cat, heavier than expected, rattling with what turns out to matter later. The night dock hides it inside a crate marked ASSORTED, the safest word in the building, while the Leadership announces the box is being professionally stored, and nobody corrects them. The slate wishes you had asked first. The box does not.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

fail.text: The cupboard also stores the minutes of every meeting since the founding, shelved by weight. You get the box's handle and the shelf gets your shoulders, eleven years of stability arriving all at once, bound in quarto. The caretaker finds you under the collected postponements and logs it as a stationery incident. The Leadership hires a second doorman for the cupboard, which now has better security than the wages office, and the slate sends you grapes with a note asking you, warmly, to stop helping.
effects: schedule 3 r18_union_election_coalition The election is pending. The kettle is on.

text:
UNION ELECTION

A leaving card is circulating the management corridor for a supervisor nobody remembers hiring. Cards get signed up there without reading, this is known, this is policy, and the tally clerk has noticed that a reference is, structurally speaking, just a signature near some praise. The slate needs forty references by Thursday. The treasurer needs, by his own account, to be understood, and has booked Brenda for the occasion.

choices.text: Tuck the reference forms inside the leaving card

outcomes.text: Forty managers sign the card, and the card, by a marvel of stapling, is also forty reference forms. Best wishes for the future, writes the head of provisioning, directly above a line certifying the bearer's fitness to vote, which in fairness is a kind of best wish for the future. The tally clerk detaches, sorts, and files the references by Thursday breakfast. The supervisor nobody remembers hiring receives a card empty except for one genuine goodbye, and is moved to tears, so on balance the paperwork has never done more good.
effects: schedule 4 r18_union_election_crisis The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: Thirty-nine managers sign without reading. The fortieth reads, being new, and unfamiliar with the corridor's core value of not reading. The card is confiscated as evidence, the evidence is a leaving card, and the resulting inquiry must decide, on the record, whether best wishes were sincerely meant. The reference forms are declared void, though the slate agrees the attempt was beautiful, and the treasurer projects a candle.
effects: schedule 4 r18_union_election_crisis The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Charge the candidates for premium ballot positioning

outcomes.text: Alphabetical order, says the rulebook, unless otherwise agreed, says you, and agreement costs fourteen coins a name. Two candidates pay up, one of them twice, to be safe. The revised ballot lists them at the top in a font that can only be described as sponsored, while the reference line sits below like a bouncer who saw everything. The tally clerk moves your name to a different page of her ledger. Nobody tells you which page.
effects: schedule 4 r18_union_election_crisis The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Host a forms night on the dock with soup and pens

success.text: Soup, pens, and forty people helping each other with a form designed to be filled out alone. The reference line causes the usual silence until the widow from the wages office observes that her manager owes her for two decades of not mentioning things, and collects his signature by asking for it in front of his mother. By midnight the forms are done and the soup is a memory with croutons in it. The treasurer projects, unprompted, a transparency of everyone's names in a circle, no top, no bottom, and for once nobody asks him to get to the figures.
effects: schedule 4 r18_union_election_crisis The election is pending. The kettle is on.

fail.text: The soup is a success, the pens are a success, the trestle table is not. It goes at the far end first, in the manner of trestle tables, and you catch two urns and take the third on the knee. Forty damp reference forms dry overnight on the dock ropes like confessions on a line. The forms survive, mostly legible, faintly oxtail, and the tally clerk rules that a soup stain does not invalidate a signature, setting a precedent everyone hopes will never matter again.
effects: schedule 4 r18_union_election_crisis The election is pending. The kettle is on.

text:
UNION ELECTION

Election night arrives wearing bunting the Leadership approved, itemised, and hung at regulation height. The Interim Chair personally certifies the quorum, counting the room twice with a brass clicker so there can be no dispute about who was present when he wins. The treasurer sets up Brenda beside the stage to display results as they come, and the ballot box sits on the trestle, fed all evening by ballots whose reference lines have been, one way or another, filled. At nine the count begins. At ten past, the Chair's face begins its long unscheduled renovation.

choices.text: Sell the running count to management by the hour

outcomes.text: A man from upstairs waits by the fire door with an envelope and no small talk. Every hour you bring him numbers, and every hour the envelope gets a sibling, fourteen coins across the evening, the going rate for other people's suspense. Management learns the Leadership is losing before the Leadership does, and quietly starts drafting congratulations to whoever, addressed to The Occupant. The tally clerk catches your eye once, at half nine, and clicks her pencil, and that is the whole conversation.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: A man from upstairs waits by the fire door with an envelope and no small talk. Every hour you bring him numbers, and every hour the envelope gets a sibling, fourteen coins across the evening, the going rate for other people's suspense. Management learns the Leadership is losing before the Leadership does, and quietly starts drafting congratulations to whoever, addressed to The Occupant. effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Keep the room fed and present until the last ballot

success.text: Tea holds a quorum together better than principle, and you have both urns going by eight. Nobody leaves, not when the count stalls, not when the Chair demands a recess for stability, not even when the biscuits reach the plain ones. At midnight the tally clerk announces the result to a room still legally, certifiably, entirely present: the slate has won, in front of the exact number of witnesses the Chair's brass clicker swore to. The Chair asks for the number to be checked. It is his own number, and he checks it anyway.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

fail.text: The second urn goes over at the turn of the count and you take the boiling side of it, which at least settles the question of whether you would stay to the end, since you are now part of the floor. The room stays too, quorate to the last, and the slate wins by nine. Scalded and bandaged in tea towels, you hear the Chair dispute a figure he certified himself, with his own clicker, in front of everyone. The night dock walks you home, slowly, like a trophy.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

choices.text: Read the quorum certificate back to its author

outcomes.text: This document, you say, certifies that this room, tonight, was sufficient, and it bears the only signature the Chair has ever fully trusted. He examines his own handwriting for forgery for a long moment and finds it authentic. The result stands, the slate wins, and the Permanent Interim Leadership becomes interim in the other direction. The Chair announces that the result is provisional. The room lets him have the word, since words are all that is left in his tray.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

outcomes.text: You read the certificate aloud, but the Chair produces a second certificate, certifying the first as preliminary. It transpires he certifies in duplicate, one for the record and one for emergencies, and tonight qualifies as an emergency because he lost. The result is suspended pending a review of whether the room he counted was the room that was there. Brenda projects the vote totals anyway, enormous, on the wall above the stage, and no review has ever managed to switch off a projector with the truth loaded.
effects: schedule 5 r18_union_election_compromise The election is pending. The kettle is on.

text:
UNION ELECTION

Negotiations convene in the small meeting room, the one with the good kettle, which both sides claimed as a concession. The Leadership will recognise the result, provisionally, if the reference line stays on all future ballots, for continuity. The slate will accept the line if the word management is removed from it, for obvious reasons. Between the two positions sits the treasurer, who has brought Brenda, and a transparency he says he has been saving for exactly this weather.

choices.text: Sit with the flexible three while terms are read

outcomes.text: The three members given flexibility attend in work clothes, on their own unpaid time, and you sit with them in the row the Leadership left empty for effect. Terms read better with witnesses in them. The Leadership drops the clause requiring future stability, a substance nobody has ever produced on request, and reinstates the three to hours that exist. On the way out, the smallest of the three signs your ballot's reference line under witnessed by, which is not what the line is for, and is exactly what the line is for now.
effects: schedule 2 r18_union_election_reversal The election is pending. The kettle is on.
